name: Home
title: Home
description: Entry page for the local boat insurance website that loads dynamic homepage content, exposes primary actions like starting a quote, and routes users to key tasks such as filing a claim, managing a policy, finding an agent, viewing discounts, and accessing coverage and help information.

core_features: Load homepage configuration (hero text, primary quote action label, secondary action list, coverage summaries) using the getHomepageContent interface on initial page load.
Display a primary action area that uses the dynamically provided primaryCtaLabel to start a new online quote via the startNewQuote interface, then routes the user to the quote boat details page.

Render a list of secondary actions (e.g., filing a claim, managing a policy, finding an agent, viewing discounts, opening the help center) based on the secondaryCtas returned by getHomepageContent, and navigate to the appropriate target page for each action.
Show a coverage summary section using the coverageSummary data from getHomepageContent and provide navigation from this section to the boat coverage overview page.

Use URL parameters to influence behavior of the quote-start process (such as preselecting a policy type or passing through discount codes to startNewQuote) and to annotate navigation to downstream pages with the user’s entry source.
user_workflows: Starting an online quote from the homepage: 1) User lands on index.html; 2) The page calls getHomepageContent and stores the result; 3) The primary actions component renders the hero content and primaryCtaLabel; 4) User clicks the primary quote button; 5) The quote-start handler reads relevant URL parameters (such as preselectedPolicyTypeId and preselectedDiscountCodes if present), then calls startNewQuote with entrySource set to the homepage’s entry source value and with policyTypeId and preselectedDiscountCodes taken from URL state; 6) On success, the handler uses the returned quoteNumber and step to build a navigation URL to quote_boat_details.html including query parameters (such as quoteNumber and entrySource); 7) Browser navigates to quote_boat_details.html where the quote wizard continues at the returned step.

Accessing other key tasks via secondary actions: 1) User lands on index.html and the page loads data using getHomepageContent; 2) The secondary actions list component iterates over secondaryCtas, displaying each item’s label and description; 3) User clicks one of the secondary actions; 4) The component reads the clicked item’s key and targetPageKey and maps that to the correct target page (e.g., claim_start.html, account_dashboard.html, agent_search.html, discounts.html, help_center.html); 5) The component appends a from=home URL parameter (and optionally the secondary action key) and navigates to the selected page.

Viewing coverage information from the homepage: 1) User lands on index.html; 2) getHomepageContent returns coverageSummary items; 3) The coverage summary component lists these items; 4) User clicks the coverage options link or a coverage summary item; 5) The component navigates to coverage_overview.html with URL parameters indicating from=home and optionally the coverage item that triggered navigation (for example, via iconKey or an index position).

Returning to the homepage from another page: 1) User is on another page and clicks the global Home navigation link or site logo; 2) Browser navigates to index.html, optionally preserving or adding URL parameters such as preselectedDiscountCodes or preselectedPolicyTypeId; 3) On load, the homepage reads any relevant URL parameters into its state before rendering actions; 4) Subsequent clicks on the primary quote action or coverage links use these parameters to pass context into startNewQuote and downstream pages.

Starting a quote that is influenced by discounts or policy type context: 1) User arrives on index.html with URL parameters such as preselectedPolicyTypeId and/or preselectedDiscountCodes (for example, from a discounts page or policy comparison page); 2) Homepage content loads via getHomepageContent; 3) When the user clicks the primary quote action, the quote-start handler includes the preselectedPolicyTypeId and parsed preselectedDiscountCodes array in the call to startNewQuote; 4) startNewQuote returns a quote with appliedDiscountCodes and selectedPolicyType (if applicable); 5) The handler navigates to quote_boat_details.html with query parameters that include quoteNumber and entrySource so the quote flow continues using that context.
interactions: Initial page load: On index.html load, a page-level initialization routine calls getHomepageContent with no parameters. While awaiting the response, the page may display a loading state (non-content-specific) or skeleton UI. When the promise resolves, the data is stored in page state and passed to child components such as the primary actions panel, secondary actions list, and coverage summary list.

Primary quote action click: When the user clicks the primary quote button, the corresponding event handler reads URL parameters (such as preselectedPolicyTypeId and preselectedDiscountCodes) from window.location.search, transforms preselectedDiscountCodes into an array if present, and calls startNewQuote with entrySource set to the appropriate enum value for the homepage and with policyTypeId and preselectedDiscountCodes based on the parsed parameters. The handler then inspects the returned object: if success is true, it constructs a navigation URL to quote_boat_details.html that includes query parameters like quoteNumber and entrySource; if success is false or message indicates an issue, it sets an error state to display a non-hardcoded error message derived from the response.

Secondary action click: For each secondaryCtas item returned by getHomepageContent, the secondary actions component attaches an onClick handler. When any item is clicked, the handler uses the item’s key and targetPageKey to determine the target route file (for example, claim_start.html for a claim-related key), appends from=home and optionally actionKey=<secondaryCta.key> to the query string, and navigates the browser to that URL.

Coverage summary item click: The coverage summary list component attaches an onClick handler to the overall coverage section link and optionally to individual coverage summary items. On click, the handler builds a URL to coverage_overview.html that includes from=home and, if needed, a parameter such as coverageRef derived from the clicked item (for example, coverageRef=<iconKey>). The browser then navigates to that URL.

Header and footer navigation: Clicking the global Home navigation item or site logo when on another page simply navigates to index.html. Any existing query parameters may be preserved or replaced depending on navigation strategy; the homepage then re-runs its initialization logic, calls getHomepageContent again, and updates its components.

Error handling interactions: If getHomepageContent fails, a page-level error state is set and a generic, non-hardcoded message is displayed using only data available from the error itself (for example, HTTP status or a message property). Users can retry by refreshing the page or triggering a manual reload control that re-calls getHomepageContent.

Quote-start retry: If startNewQuote returns success=false or fails due to connectivity issues, the primary quote action handler updates an error state tied to the primary actions panel. The user can click the primary quote action again to retry, which repeats the startNewQuote call using the current URL parameters and state.
page_functionality.state_logic: The homepage relies on URL parameters exclusively (not localStorage) to manage and propagate state to downstream pages and interfaces. On each load of index.html, a state initialization function parses window.location.search into a key-value map. Several parameters are recognized: (1) preselectedPolicyTypeId: optional, used to pre-populate the policyTypeId parameter when calling startNewQuote; the value is not interpreted beyond being passed through to startNewQuote, which is responsible for validating it. (2) preselectedDiscountCodes: optional, expected as a comma-separated string; when present, it is split into an array of discount codes and passed as preselectedDiscountCodes to startNewQuote without the homepage needing to know details of the Discount entity. (3) from: optional, indicating the upstream page that navigated to the homepage; this is used only for analytics or conditional display logic and is not sent to APIs. (4) focusSection: optional, may hold values such as 'claims', 'coverage', or 'help'; the homepage uses this value to decide which subsection to focus or scroll into view after load, but it does not affect API calls. When the user clicks the primary quote action, the startNewQuote interface is called using entrySource set to the homepage-specific enum value, and policyTypeId and preselectedDiscountCodes taken from the URL-derived state as described above. Upon receiving the startNewQuote response, the homepage builds a navigation URL to quote_boat_details.html that typically includes: quoteNumber (from the response), entrySource (matching the value used in the startNewQuote call), and optionally step (from the response) and any additional identifiers necessary for the quote wizard to restore context. For secondary actions and coverage navigation, the homepage uses static routing information (such as target filenames) combined with dynamic URL parameters like from=home and actionKey=<secondaryCta.key> or coverageRef=<coverageSummary.iconKey>. No quote or coverage details are stored in localStorage; everything needed for the next page is passed via URL parameters and managed server-side by the respective pages.

components.functionality: On initial render of index.html, this component calls the getHomepageContent interface, manages loading and error states, and stores the returned data (heroTitle, heroSubtitle, primaryCtaLabel, secondaryCtas, coverageSummary) in a shared page state object that is passed down to other components.
event_handlers: onInit: Triggered when the Home page is loaded. Calls getHomepageContent(), waits for the asynchronous response, and on success, updates pageState.homepageContent with the returned object.

onLoadSuccess: Fired after successful getHomepageContent completion. Distributes the returned heroTitle, heroSubtitle, primaryCtaLabel, secondaryCtas, and coverageSummary to child components via props or a shared store.

onLoadError: Fired if getHomepageContent fails. Sets pageState.homepageContentError to an error object derived from the failure and exposes it to other components so they can render a generic error message and/or retry controls.

components.functionality: Displays the main homepage action area, using heroTitle, heroSubtitle, and primaryCtaLabel from pageState.homepageContent. It exposes a primary button to start a quote and renders the list of secondaryCtas as quick links to other key tasks.
event_handlers: onPrimaryCtaClick: When the user clicks the primary action button, this handler delegates to the quote-start-controller component (or its logic) to call startNewQuote with entrySource set to the homepage-specific value and any policyTypeId and preselectedDiscountCodes read from URL parameters.

onSecondaryCtaClick: For each item in homepageContent.secondaryCtas, this handler receives the clicked item’s key and targetPageKey. It looks up the corresponding target HTML file (for example, claim_start.html, account_dashboard.html, agent_search.html, discounts.html, help_center.html) and navigates the browser to that file, appending URL parameters such as from=home and actionKey=<secondaryCta.key>.

components.functionality: Encapsulates the logic needed to start a new quote from the homepage. It reads URL parameters, prepares arguments for startNewQuote, calls the interface, handles success and error responses, and redirects the user to the quote boat details page.
event_handlers: onInitFromUrl: When the Home page loads, this handler parses window.location.search into a map of parameters and stores recognized keys (such as preselectedPolicyTypeId and preselectedDiscountCodes) into pageState.quoteInitParams for later use.

onStartQuote: Invoked by the primary-actions-panel’s onPrimaryCtaClick event. It reads pageState.quoteInitParams, converts preselectedDiscountCodes into an array if present, and calls startNewQuote with parameters: entrySource set to the homepage entry source enum value, policyTypeId set to the parsed preselectedPolicyTypeId or left undefined, and preselectedDiscountCodes set to the parsed array or left undefined.

onStartQuoteSuccess: Executed when startNewQuote returns success=true. It constructs a navigation URL for quote_boat_details.html that includes query parameters such as quoteNumber (from the response), entrySource (matching the value used in the call), and optionally step (from the response) and any other identifiers needed by the next page. It then sets window.location.href to this URL to move the user into the quote flow.

onStartQuoteError: Executed when startNewQuote returns success=false or throws an error. It updates a state property (for example, pageState.quoteStartError) with the response’s message or an error object. The primary-actions-panel reads this state and renders a generic error notice, allowing the user to retry by clicking the primary action again.

components.functionality: Renders the list of secondary actions returned in homepageContent.secondaryCtas, each with its dynamic label and description, and routes the user to the appropriate downstream page for tasks such as filing a claim, managing a policy, finding an agent, viewing discounts, or accessing the help center.
event_handlers: onSecondaryActionClick: Receives the clicked secondary action item (including key and targetPageKey). It translates targetPageKey into a concrete URL (for example, claim_start.html, account_dashboard.html, agent_search.html, discounts.html, help_center.html), appends query parameters like from=home and actionKey=<secondaryCta.key>, and then performs navigation by setting window.location.href.

onSecondaryActionFocus: Optionally used when the homepage is loaded with a focusSection URL parameter. If focusSection corresponds to one of the secondaryCtas keys, the component scrolls that action into view or highlights it after getHomepageContent has loaded.

components.functionality: Presents the coverageSummary items returned by getHomepageContent. Each item uses the dynamic title, description, and iconKey from the API and provides navigation to detailed coverage information on the coverage_overview.html page.
event_handlers: onCoverageSectionLinkClick: Triggered when the user clicks the general link associated with the Boat Coverage Options section. It builds a URL to coverage_overview.html that includes from=home and optionally a default coverageRef value, then navigates to that URL.

onCoverageItemClick: Triggered when the user clicks a specific coverage summary item. It uses the clicked item’s iconKey (or the item’s index) to set a URL parameter such as coverageRef=<iconKey> when navigating to coverage_overview.html, allowing that page to highlight or focus on the relevant coverage topic.

layout.grid_system: Likely a 12-column fixed-width grid (approx. 1140–1200px) centered within the viewport, with the left 7–8 columns for main content and right 4–5 columns for the sidebar CTA/agent column.
layout.layout_pattern: Use a centered 12-column grid (approx. 1140–1200px max width) with a full-width top navigation, then a two-column layout: the left 7–8 columns for main homepage content and the right 4–5 columns for the action/quote sidebar. Within each column, components stack in a clear top-to-bottom order. The left column leads with hero content (headline, supporting copy) followed immediately by the coverage-summary-list, then any additional narrative or imagery. The right sidebar starts at the same vertical level as the hero with the primary-actions-panel (containing the quote-start-controller), followed by the secondary-actions-list beneath it. The homepage-content-loader sits at the top of the content area and, when active, temporarily replaces or overlays the main two-column content with skeletons that mimic this structure.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Use a centered 12-column grid (approx. 1140–1200px max width) with a full-width top navigation, then a two-column layout: the left 7–8 columns for main homepage content and the right 4–5 columns for the action/quote sidebar. Within each column, components stack in a clear top-to-bottom order. The left column leads with hero content (headline, supporting copy) followed immediately by the coverage-summary-list, then any additional narrative or imagery. The right sidebar starts at the same vertical level as the hero with the primary-actions-panel (containing the quote-start-controller), followed by the secondary-actions-list beneath it. The homepage-content-loader sits at the top of the content area and, when active, temporarily replaces or overlays the main two-column content with skeletons that mimic this structure.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Allocate roughly 60–65% of the horizontal content width to the left main column and 35–40% to the right sidebar, aligned to the same baseline below the navigation. Vertically, the initial viewport (above the fold) is dominated by the hero content plus the top of the right sidebar: the hero text and coverage-summary-list occupy about 60% of the column height, while the primary-actions-panel (with the quote-start-controller inside) takes up the top ~40% of the sidebar. The secondary-actions-list fits immediately below with a comfortable gap. Major stack gaps between blocks are 24–32px, and each card/button uses 16–24px internal padding, following the 8px spacing scale.
layout_strategies.content_density: Maintain a medium-low density: components are information-focused but with ample breathing room. Each card (primary-actions-panel, any container around secondary-actions-list) has generous white space and clear separation from neighbors. The coverage-summary-list uses pill-like items with obvious gaps between chips and rows, avoiding crowding while still displaying multiple items at once. Text is not tightly packed; headings, CTAs, and supporting copy each have distinct vertical spacing, echoing the mockup’s clean, corporate marketing feel.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Preserve an F-shaped scanning pattern: the user first reads across the top navigation, then down to a large red-toned hero headline on the left. From that horizontal line, attention is drawn across to the right where the primary-actions-panel and the bold red pill CTA from the quote-start-controller sit high and prominent. From there, the eye moves down the left column through the coverage-summary-list and any supporting copy, then continues downward. Secondary attention flows down the right column through the secondary-actions-list. The homepage-content-loader, when visible, shows skeleton content in this same arrangement to teach the eye where content will appear.

layout.overall_description: Beneath a full-width top navigation bar, the main content is centered within a fixed-width container and split into two columns on desktop: a wider left column for narrative content and coverage information, and a narrower right sidebar for quote and task-oriented actions. The left side opens with a bold, corporate-style hero headline in red, followed by a short supporting line of copy; directly beneath sits the coverage-summary-list rendered as a grid of pill-shaped coverage items that wrap into multiple rows, mirroring the town chips in the mockup. This column then continues downward into any additional explanatory content or imagery. Starting at the same vertical line as the hero text, the right sidebar rests on a soft gray background and holds a white primary-actions-panel card with subtle shadow; inside this card, a concise prompt leads into the dominant red pill-shaped quote-start-controller button anchored near the bottom edge of the card. Below that card, separated by a 24–32px gap, the secondary-actions-list appears as a vertical stack of full-width outline-style buttons or a lighter-weight card, offering tasks like filing a claim or sending a payment. All blocks are separated by consistent vertical spacing, and the composition guides the eye from top-left hero, across to the main quote CTA on the right, and then down both columns in parallel. When loading, the homepage-content-loader displays skeleton placeholders occupying the same two-column structure, maintaining the visual hierarchy even before data arrives.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The homepage-content-loader sits directly below the top navigation and spans the full width of the centered content container, effectively covering all 12 grid columns. When active, it renders skeleton shapes that mimic the final layout: a wide text block in the upper left approximating the hero headline, a slightly smaller line beneath for supporting text, several horizontal pill-like placeholders representing the coverage-summary-list in the left column, and a tall rectangular card shape in the upper right corresponding to the primary-actions-panel. Below these, narrower skeleton bars suggest the secondary-actions-list. The loader extends roughly the height of the initial viewport, giving a clear preview of the two-column structure; once real content arrives, this component collapses to zero height and no longer occupies space in the flow.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Appears in the normal document flow at the top of the main content area and can visually overlay or replace the actual components beneath via skeleton placeholders; when inactive it is hidden and does not affect layout or scrolling.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The primary-actions-panel is the dominant card in the right sidebar. It is positioned at the top of the right 4–5 columns of the grid, aligned horizontally such that its top edge matches the baseline of the hero headline in the left column. The panel spans the full width of the sidebar and sits on a light gray page background, with a white card surface, very light shadow, and rounded corners. Internal padding is generous (around 24px on all sides), creating space for a short question or prompt at the top, optional brief descriptive text in the middle, and the quote-start-controller anchored toward the bottom. The card’s height is sufficient so that, together with its padding, it occupies roughly the upper 40% of the sidebar above the fold, clearly establishing it as the primary action area on the page.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the right column; it scrolls with the page and maintains its relative right alignment within the fixed-width centered container, without sticky or floating behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Within the primary-actions-panel, the quote-start-controller is rendered as a large, pill-shaped red button that functions as the hero CTA. It sits near the bottom of the card’s content area, aligned to the left but spanning almost the full internal width of the panel, leaving only the standard card padding on either side. Vertically, it has a height of about 48–56px, with 12–16px vertical padding and 24–32px horizontal padding built into its shape. The button’s strong red fill and flat design make it the most visually prominent interactive element in the sidebar, so that when users scan from the hero headline to the right, this control immediately catches the eye as the primary way to start a quote.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Lives inside the primary-actions-panel and participates in that card’s normal flow; it does not detach, float, or stick but always remains positioned at the bottom segment of the panel’s content area.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The secondary-actions-list is positioned directly below the primary-actions-panel in the right sidebar, separated from it by a vertical gap of approximately 24–32px, consistent with the spacing system. It spans the same width as the panel, filling the right 4–5 grid columns. Its actions are displayed as a vertical stack of 2–4 full-width controls, each styled as an outline or subtle card: white background, light gray border or shadow, pill-shaped corners, and red or dark text, echoing the mockup’s 'File a Claim' and 'Send Payment' buttons. Each item is separated by about 8–16px of vertical space, and the overall block occupies roughly a quarter to a third of the sidebar’s height above the fold, clearly distinct from the primary quote card yet immediately accessible in the downward scan.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block within the right column; it scrolls naturally under the primary-actions-panel and aligns with the grid, with no sticky or floating behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The coverage-summary-list resides in the left main content column, occupying approximately 7–8 of the 12 grid columns (about 60–65% of the content width). It is placed directly beneath the hero text—the main headline and its supporting sentence—separated by a vertical gap of about 16–24px. Visually, it takes the form of a grid or wrap layout of pill-shaped coverage items (e.g., 'Liability', 'Comprehensive', 'Towing'), each rendered as a flat chip or compact card with rounded edges, subtle border or very light shadow, and comfortable internal padding. Items flow left to right and wrap onto multiple rows, with 8–16px horizontal spacing between chips and 8–12px vertical spacing between rows, echoing the location chips in the mockup. The block’s bottom edge sits 24–32px above any subsequent body copy or imagery, so it reads as a distinct but integrated band of interactive or scannable coverage options within the main narrative column.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static content in the left column; it expands vertically as more coverage items are added, pushing subsequent content downward while preserving its alignment within the main grid.

name: Boat Insurance Coverage Overview
title: Boat Insurance Coverage Overview
description: Provides an overview of available boat insurance coverages and policy types and allows users to start a quote from a chosen policy type or from a general coverage context.

core_features: Load and display coverage overview content using getCoverageOverview, including an introductory section, a list of core coverages, and a summary of each available policy type.
Allow users to review core coverages with plain-language descriptions before starting a quote, using data returned in getCoverageOverview.coreCoverages.
Display policy type summaries (for example, different policy tiers) using getCoverageOverview.policyTypeSummaries, including descriptions, relative price positioning, and highlighted benefits.
Provide a generic "Get a Quote" action that initializes a new Quote via startNewQuote with entrySource set to the coverage overview page, then navigates the user to the boat details step.

Provide "Start Quote" actions on each policy type summary that call startQuoteForPolicyType with the selected policyTypeId (and an optional user-selected preferred liability limit) and then navigate to the boat details step in the quote flow.
Provide a "Compare Policy Types" navigation action that sends users to policy_comparison.html to view a detailed side-by-side comparison.
Support URL-parameter-driven page state (such as preselecting a policy type or focusing on a particular coverage topic) without using localStorage.
user_workflows:
Workflow 1 – Learn about coverage then start a general quote:
1. User navigates to coverage_overview.html via header, footer, or direct link.
2. On page load, the page calls getCoverageOverview and enters a loading state.
3. When getCoverageOverview returns successfully, the page displays the intro text, core coverages list, and policy type summaries.
4. The user reads through the core coverages section to understand basic protections.
5. The user clicks a generic "Get a Quote" button when they are ready.
6. The click handler calls startNewQuote with parameters:
   - entrySource = "coverage_overview_page"
   - policyTypeId = null (or omitted)
   - preselectedDiscountCodes = an array derived from any optional prior user selections on this page (may be empty).
7. If startNewQuote.success is true, the page reads startNewQuote.step (expected to be "boat_details") and startNewQuote.quoteNumber.
8. The user is redirected to quote_boat_details.html with URL parameters, for example:
   - quote_boat_details.html?quoteNumber={quoteNumber}&entrySource=coverage_overview_page
9. The quote flow continues on quote_boat_details.html using the initialized quote context.

Workflow 2 – Select a policy type on this page and start a pre-filtered quote:
1. User lands on coverage_overview.html (either directly or after browsing from another section).
2. The page loads coverage information via getCoverageOverview and renders policy type summaries.
3. The user reviews differences between policy types using the information provided (descriptions, highlightedBenefits, and flags like includesOnWaterTowing / includesUninsuredBoaterCoverage).
4. Optionally, the user selects a preferred liability limit option from controls within a specific policy type card (the value is stored in page state only and not persisted anywhere else yet).
5. The user clicks "Start Quote" on a specific policy type card.
6. The click handler calls startQuoteForPolicyType with parameters:
   - policyTypeId = the policyTypeSummaries[i].policyTypeId for the clicked card
   - preferredLiabilityLimit = the value selected by the user on that card (or null/omitted if none selected)
   - entrySource = "coverage_overview_page"
7. If startQuoteForPolicyType.success is true, the page reads startQuoteForPolicyType.quoteNumber and step (expected to be "boat_details").
8. The user is redirected to quote_boat_details.html with URL parameters, for example:
   - quote_boat_details.html?quoteNumber={quoteNumber}&entrySource=coverage_overview_page&policyTypeId={policyTypeId}
9. The quote flow continues with the chosen policy type preselected, and the preferred liability limit may influence defaults shown on subsequent steps.

Workflow 3 – Arrive with a focused policy type or coverage topic via URL parameters:
1. User navigates to coverage_overview.html with optional URL parameters, for example:
   - coverage_overview.html?policyTypeId={policyTypeId}
   - coverage_overview.html?coverageFocus={coverageFeatureCode}
2. The page reads URL parameters on load and stores them in internal state (e.g., focusedPolicyTypeId, focusedCoverageCode).
3. The page calls getCoverageOverview and waits for the response.
4. After getCoverageOverview returns, the page:
   - Scrolls or jumps to the policy type section matching focusedPolicyTypeId (if present in policyTypeSummaries) and visually marks it as focused.
   - Or, when coverageFocus is provided, emphasizes any coreCoverage items whose code matches coverageFocus and may pre-expand additional explanatory text for that coverage.
5. The user can still review all policy types and coverages, and can start a generic or policy-type-specific quote using the same buttons.
6. When the user starts a quote from this page, navigation to quote_boat_details.html always includes entrySource=coverage_overview_page and may optionally include policyTypeId and quoteNumber as described in other workflows. The original URL parameters (policyTypeId, coverageFocus) do not need to be forwarded, as their purpose is limited to the coverage overview page state.
interactions:
Page load / initialization:
- Event: onInit (page-level)
- Behavior: Read any URL parameters relevant to state (e.g., policyTypeId, coverageFocus). Call getCoverageOverview with no arguments. While awaiting the response, show a loading state in the sections that depend on this data. On success, populate intro, core coverages, and policy type summaries; on failure, show a functional error message and provide a retry action that re-calls getCoverageOverview.

Interaction: Retry loading coverage overview data after an error:
- Event: onClick on a "Retry" control in the error state of the coverage overview loader.
- Behavior: Re-issue getCoverageOverview. If successful, replace the error state with the loaded content; if not, remain in error state and optionally show updated error messaging while still not exposing static fallback data.

Interaction: Generic "Get a Quote" button:
- Event: onClick on the generic quote CTA.
- Behavior: Call startNewQuote with entrySource set to "coverage_overview_page" and optional preselectedDiscountCodes derived from any user selections on this page (if present). Do not pass a policyTypeId here so that the quote is not pre-filtered to a specific policy type. On success, navigate to quote_boat_details.html with at least quoteNumber and entrySource in the URL. On failure, display an inline error message and keep the user on this page.

Interaction: Policy type card "Start Quote" action:
- Event: onClick on a "Start Quote" control within a specific policy type summary card.
- Behavior: Read the selected policyTypeId from the card’s bound data and any optional preferred liability limit selected within that card. Call startQuoteForPolicyType with policyTypeId, preferredLiabilityLimit (if any), and entrySource="coverage_overview_page". On success, redirect to quote_boat_details.html with quoteNumber, entrySource, and policyTypeId as URL parameters. On failure, show an inline error state on that card while keeping other cards functional.

Interaction: Policy type selection focus (non-API, client-only):
- Event: onClick on a policy type header or a "Set as focus" control in a card.
- Behavior: Update local page state to mark that policy type as focused (for example, for highlighting or sorting within this page only). This does not call any interfaces. When the user later clicks "Start Quote" on the focused card, the same startQuoteForPolicyType workflow applies.

Interaction: "Compare Policy Types" navigation:
- Event: onClick on the "Compare Policy Types" link or button.
- Behavior: Navigate to policy_comparison.html using standard browser navigation. No interfaces are called as part of this interaction from this page, and no additional URL parameters are required by the base navigation definition.

Interaction: Coverage focus from URL changes (e.g., using back/forward browser navigation):
- Event: onPopState or similar URL change listener.
- Behavior: When URL parameters such as policyTypeId or coverageFocus change (for example, via the browser back button), re-evaluate page state to update which policy type or coverage is focused. This does not trigger new API calls unless the page is configured to fully reload; getCoverageOverview data already in memory is reused.

Interaction: Inline controls for preferred liability limit within a policy type card (if implemented):
- Event: onChange on a select/dropdown or other control for choosing a preferred liability limit.
- Behavior: Update local state for that card with the selected numeric limit. This state is then passed into startQuoteForPolicyType.preferredLiabilityLimit when the user clicks "Start Quote" for that card. No API calls occur until the user explicitly starts the quote.
page_functionality.state_logic:
URL parameters and internal state behavior:

1. URL Parameters Read on Page Load:
- policyTypeId (optional): When present, the page stores this value in a focusedPolicyTypeId state variable. After getCoverageOverview resolves, the page checks whether any policyTypeSummaries item has a matching policyTypeId. If found, that policy type card is marked as focused (for example, pre-expanded or visually emphasized) and the page may scroll to it. This parameter is only used on this page and is not required to be forwarded to subsequent steps; instead, the quote-related interfaces provide their own policyTypeId context when the user starts a quote.
- coverageFocus (optional): Interpreted as a coverageFeature code or coverage-related identifier. On load, the page stores this in focusedCoverageCode. After getCoverageOverview completes, the coreCoverages list is filtered or highlighted to bring attention to any coverage whose code matches the parameter. Again, this is purely for page-level emphasis and does not affect interface calls.

2. Page-Level State:
- loadingState: Tracks loading, success, or error for getCoverageOverview. Initially 'loading', then 'success' or 'error' based on the interface response. Controls which UI segments are visible (loading indicator, content, or retry error message).
- coverageOverviewData: Holds the full response from getCoverageOverview, including introTitle, introBody, coreCoverages[], and policyTypeSummaries[]. All display components for this page bind directly to this state rather than to static placeholders.
- focusedPolicyTypeId: Derived from URL parameter policyTypeId or from inline user actions (such as clicking on a card's header). Used to apply highlighting or positioning logic within the policy type list.
- focusedCoverageCode: Derived from URL parameter coverageFocus or potentially from user interaction with the coverage list (for example, selecting a coverage from a small index). Used to emphasize a specific coverage within coreCoverages.
- policyTypeLocalSelections: A mapping keyed by policyTypeId that stores user selections within each policy type card (for example, the currently selected preferred liability limit). These values are not persisted beyond the page and are only used when invoking startQuoteForPolicyType.
- quoteStartStatus: Temporary state used while awaiting responses from startNewQuote or startQuoteForPolicyType. For example, it may include isSubmitting (boolean) and an error message string. This state is typically scoped either to the whole page (for the generic quote CTA) or to a single card (for a policy-type-specific quote CTA).

3. Navigation to Other Pages:
- Generic quote CTA navigation: After a successful startNewQuote call, the page constructs a URL to quote_boat_details.html that includes at least: quoteNumber (from the startNewQuote response) and entrySource=coverage_overview_page. Additional parameters may be added as needed by the consuming application, but the page design does not rely on storing or reading them from localStorage.
- Policy-type-specific quote CTA navigation: After a successful startQuoteForPolicyType call, the page constructs a URL to quote_boat_details.html including: quoteNumber (from startQuoteForPolicyType), entrySource=coverage_overview_page, and policyTypeId corresponding to the selected card. This ensures that the downstream quote page can identify the active quote and understand the user’s entry context.
- Compare Policy Types: Navigation to policy_comparison.html does not require additional URL parameters by default. Any future enhancements to pass a focusedPolicyTypeId could be done via query parameters, but are not required by the current page architecture.

4. No Local Storage Usage:
- All persistent state used for cross-page flows (such as quote context) is represented via URL parameters and/or managed by the backend using the quoteNumber returned from startNewQuote or startQuoteForPolicyType. The page explicitly does not rely on localStorage or similar browser storage mechanisms for functional behavior.

components.functionality: Fetches coverage overview content when the page initializes, manages loading and error states, and exposes the loaded data (intro content, core coverages, and policy type summaries) to child components.
event_handlers: onInit: Triggered when coverage_overview.html is loaded. Calls getCoverageOverview with no parameters. On success, stores the response in coverageOverviewData and sets loadingState='success'. On failure, sets loadingState='error' and stores error details.
onClickRetry: Triggered by a retry control when loadingState='error'. Re-calls getCoverageOverview, resetting loadingState='loading' and then updating it based on the response.

components.functionality: Displays the introductory title and body text that explain what the coverage overview is about and how users should use the information before starting a quote.
event_handlers: onDataChange: When coverageOverviewData is updated by coverage-overview-data-loader, bind introTitle and introBody from the getCoverageOverview response into this section for display.
onClickGetQuote: Optionally delegated to the generic-quote-cta component; the intro section may surface the same action as a primary button that triggers the generic quote workflow.

components.functionality: Renders a list of core boat insurance coverages using the coreCoverages array from getCoverageOverview, showing each coverage’s name, description, and any code-based highlighting based on page state.
event_handlers: onDataChange: When coverageOverviewData.coreCoverages changes, render one list item per coverage object, accessing its code, name, and description fields directly from the interface response.

onStateChange: When focusedCoverageCode changes (for example, from URL parameters or internal UI), update list item states to highlight the matching coverage and optionally expand additional explanatory text. This does not call any interfaces.

onClickCoverageItem: When the user clicks a coverage item, update focusedCoverageCode in page state to the clicked item’s code so that this coverage becomes emphasized. This interaction is purely client-side.

components.functionality: Displays all available policy type summaries returned from getCoverageOverview.policyTypeSummaries, typically as individual cards or rows, each describing a high-level boat insurance policy type and its key benefits.
event_handlers: onDataChange: When coverageOverviewData.policyTypeSummaries changes, generate one policy-type-summary-card instance per item. Each instance receives its policyTypeId, code, name, description, relativePriceRank, includesOnWaterTowing, includesUninsuredBoaterCoverage, and highlightedBenefits.

onStateChange: When focusedPolicyTypeId changes (from URL parameters or local selection), reorder or visually highlight the matching policy type summary and ensure it is visible to the user (for example, by scrolling it into view).

components.functionality: Represents a single policy type option, showing its description, key indicators (such as whether on-water towing or uninsured boater coverage are included), highlighted benefits, and a "Start Quote" action that passes the policy type context to the quote initialization interface.
event_handlers: onBindData: For each policyTypeSummaries item, bind policyTypeId, code, name, description, relativePriceRank, includesOnWaterTowing, includesUninsuredBoaterCoverage, and highlightedBenefits to the card for display and logic.

onChangePreferredLiabilityLimit: When the user selects a preferred liability limit from a control within the card (for example, a dropdown), update policyTypeLocalSelections[policyTypeId].preferredLiabilityLimit in the page state. This value is later used when calling startQuoteForPolicyType.

onClickSetFocus: When the user indicates they want to focus on this policy type (for example, by clicking its header), set focusedPolicyTypeId to this card’s policyTypeId so that other components can adapt (such as highlighting this card).

onClickStartQuote: When the user clicks the card’s "Start Quote" action, invoke startQuoteForPolicyType with parameters: policyTypeId (from this card), preferredLiabilityLimit (from policyTypeLocalSelections if available), and entrySource='coverage_overview_page'. While waiting for the response, mark this card as isSubmitting to prevent duplicate requests. On success, read quoteNumber and step from the response and navigate to quote_boat_details.html with query parameters including quoteNumber, entrySource=coverage_overview_page, and policyTypeId. On failure, clear isSubmitting and show an inline error message on this card.

components.functionality: Provides a generic "Get a Quote" action independent of any specific policy type, intended for users who want to start the quote process after reviewing general coverage information but without preselecting a policy tier.
event_handlers: onClickGetQuote: When the primary CTA button is clicked, call startNewQuote with parameters: entrySource='coverage_overview_page', policyTypeId omitted or null, and preselectedDiscountCodes supplied from any optional discount-intent interactions on this page (which may be an empty array). Set a submitting state to disable the button while the request is in progress.

onNewQuoteResponse: When startNewQuote returns, if success is true, navigate to quote_boat_details.html with at least quoteNumber={response.quoteNumber} and entrySource=coverage_overview_page as URL parameters. If success is false, display an inline error message and reset the submitting state so the user can retry.

components.functionality: Provides a simple navigation control that sends users from the coverage overview page to the dedicated policy comparison page for more detailed side-by-side evaluation of policy types.
event_handlers: onClick: Navigate directly to policy_comparison.html in the same browser context. No interfaces are called before navigation, and no additional URL parameters are required by the baseline design.

components.functionality: Synchronizes certain aspects of page behavior with URL parameters, allowing deep-linking into focused policy types or coverage topics and maintaining consistency when the user uses browser navigation controls.
event_handlers: onInit: When the page loads, parse URL parameters such as policyTypeId and coverageFocus. Store them in focusedPolicyTypeId and focusedCoverageCode state variables respectively. Do not call any interfaces here; getCoverageOverview is handled by the data-loader component.

onPopState: When the browser back/forward navigation changes the URL, re-parse policyTypeId and coverageFocus from the current URL and update focusedPolicyTypeId and focusedCoverageCode. Notify core-coverages-list and policy-type-summary-list so they can update highlighting.

onFocusChangeFromUI: When other components (such as policy-type-summary-card or core-coverages-list) change focus states, optionally update the URL query parameters to reflect the new focusedPolicyTypeId or focusedCoverageCode using history APIs, without triggering a full page reload. This remains client-side and does not invoke interfaces.

layout.grid_system: Likely a 12-column fixed-width grid (approx. 1140–1200px) centered within the viewport, with the left 7–8 columns for main content and right 4–5 columns for the sidebar CTA/agent column.
layout.layout_pattern: Use a two-column layout beneath the global header. The left column is the main narrative flow for coverage content: start with a strong hero-style intro (coverage-intro-section), then immediately support it with the core-coverages-list, followed by a more decision-oriented section (policy-type-summary-list with repeated policy-type-summary-card), and finish with a compare-policy-types-link. The right column is a persistent sidebar that begins at the same vertical height as the hero intro and houses the generic-quote-cta as a prominent card. Non-visual logic components (data-loader and url-state-listener) sit at the top of the DOM and do not influence visual layout.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Use a two-column layout beneath the global header. The left column is the main narrative flow for coverage content: start with a strong hero-style intro (coverage-intro-section), then immediately support it with the core-coverages-list, followed by a more decision-oriented section (policy-type-summary-list with repeated policy-type-summary-card), and finish with a compare-policy-types-link. The right column is a persistent sidebar that begins at the same vertical height as the hero intro and houses the generic-quote-cta as a prominent card. Non-visual logic components (data-loader and url-state-listener) sit at the top of the DOM and do not influence visual layout.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Apply a 12-column centered grid with a max content width around 1140–1200px. Allocate roughly 7–8 columns (about 60–65% of the width) to the left main content and 4–5 columns (about 35–40%) to the right sidebar. The hero band (intro + sidebar CTA) fills roughly the top 40% of the initial viewport on desktop, with generous vertical padding (48–64px) above and below the intro content. Subsequent sections (core-coverages-list, policy-type-summary-list) stack vertically with 32–40px gaps. The sidebar CTA card occupies the top portion of the sidebar and scrolls naturally with the page.
layout_strategies.content_density: Maintain a medium-low content density: each section has clear breathing room, with internal card padding around 20–24px and vertical spacing between stacked components around 24–32px. The coverage lists and policy cards use concise text blocks with clear typographic hierarchy and avoid cramped layouts; items within lists have 16–24px vertical separation. The layout shows plenty of white space while still surfacing multiple options (several coverage items and policy types plus the primary quote CTA) above the fold.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Create an F-shaped scanning pattern: the user first reads left-to-right across the global nav, then down to the large hero heading in the left column (coverage-intro-section). From there, their eye is pulled horizontally to the right by the strong red button within the generic-quote-cta card. Next, they scroll down the left column to read the core-coverages-list, then further into the policy-type-summary-list and individual policy-type-summary-card items. As they move down, the sidebar CTA remains visually cohesive on the right, periodically catching peripheral attention. The compare-policy-types-link appears at the bottom of the left content as a subtle but clear next step, closing the F-pattern with a horizontal navigation cue.

layout.overall_description: Below a full-width top navigation on a light gray or white background, the page content sits inside a centered, fixed-width container using a 12-column grid. The main content column on the left occupies about two-thirds of the width and starts with a bold red page title and short explanatory text about boat insurance coverage, spaced generously from the nav. Directly to its right, aligned with the top baseline of the title, is a tall white quote card in the sidebar that stands out against a slightly tinted background and holds a red primary 'Request a Quote' style CTA and supporting copy. As users scroll, the left column continues as a clean vertical story: an organized list of core coverages, followed by a set of neatly spaced policy summary cards describing each policy type, ending with a simple textual link to compare policy types in more detail. The right column remains a single, clearly framed CTA card that feels like an always-available action anchor without being sticky. Overall, the wide gutters, 24–32px vertical gaps, pill-shaped red buttons, and flat, low-shadow cards give the page a modern, trustworthy corporate marketing feel, with content clarity and CTAs coexisting in a balanced, easy-to-scan layout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This is a non-visual orchestration component that sits logically at the top of the page’s main content structure, just inside the app’s layout wrapper but before any visible sections. It does not render any visible elements and therefore occupies no intentional space in the layout: no padding, no margins, and no impact on the grid. It simply ensures that when the page loads, the data needed for the coverage-intro-section, core-coverages-list, and policy-type-summary-list is fetched before they render.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual, zero-height/width, positioned near the top of the DOM hierarchy; does not affect visual spacing or scrolling.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The coverage-intro-section is the hero-like starting block of the main column. It is positioned immediately below the global navigation, centered within the page container, and anchored to the left 7–8 columns of the 12-column grid (about 60–65% of the total content width). Top padding from the nav is generous (approximately 48–64px), creating a clear separation. Inside this section, a large, bold red headline spans the full width of the left column, followed by a short subtitle and a concise paragraph that explain the boat insurance coverage overview. Vertical spacing between the headline, subtitle, and paragraph uses 16–24px increments, keeping the section airy but connected. The right edge of this intro aligns horizontally with the left edge of the sidebar generic-quote-cta card, making the hero heading and the primary CTA appear at the same vertical starting line for a strong F-pattern. The overall section height fills roughly the upper 35–40% of the viewport on desktop before the user scrolls into the lists below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block-level section that scrolls with the page; adheres to the main left-column grid and does not overlap with the sidebar.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The core-coverages-list is positioned directly below the coverage-intro-section in the left main column, separated by a clear vertical gap of about 32px. It spans the same 7–8 grid columns as the intro, maintaining a consistent left column width. At the top of this section, a medium-weight subheading like 'Core Coverages' is aligned with the intro’s left edge, with 8–16px space above the first list items. Below the subheading, individual coverage items are stacked vertically, each row using a comfortable 16–24px vertical spacing and, if card-like, light flat boxes with about 16–20px internal padding and subtle separation (e.g., 8–12px gaps between cards). The list does not feel cramped; text lines are kept to reasonable lengths, and the list ends well before the bottom of the sidebar CTA card in most cases, reinforcing a stepwise scroll progression: intro, core coverages, then policy types. The left and right margins within this section align precisely with the intro text, maintaining a strong column rhythm.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static vertical stack within the left column; no fixed or sticky behavior. On smaller screens, it naturally expands to full width below the intro, preserving vertical spacing.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The policy-type-summary-list sits below the core-coverages-list in the same left main column, separated by a slightly larger vertical gap of around 32–40px to signal a shift from coverage detail to decision-support content. A clear section heading such as 'Policy Types' or similar is left-aligned with the other headings, followed by a short line or two of explanatory copy if needed. Below that, multiple policy-type-summary-card components are laid out in a responsive grid: on wider screens (desktop), cards are arranged in two columns within the left main column, each card occupying roughly 3–4 grid columns (about half the left column width with 16–24px horizontal gutter between them); on narrower screens, they stack in a single column. Vertically, rows of cards are spaced by 24–32px to maintain the medium-low density. The section is tall enough that, as users scroll through these cards, the right sidebar CTA card remains visible for at least part of the scroll, reinforcing the option to start a quote from any policy context.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static section that flows after core-coverages-list, using a responsive grid for its child cards; no fixed positioning. On mobile, it becomes a single column, full-width below the intro and core coverages.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Each policy-type-summary-card is a discrete, tappable card within the policy-type-summary-list. On desktop, each card occupies roughly half of the left content width (3–4 out of 7–8 grid columns), creating two cards per row with a consistent gutter between them. On tablet and mobile, each card expands to nearly 100% of the available width, with side margins matching the main content padding. Visually, the card is a flat white rectangle with slightly rounded corners and a very subtle shadow or hairline border, following the modern corporate style. It includes about 20–24px internal padding on all sides. The card content is vertically arranged: a clear policy type name at the top, a brief description beneath it, and a small but noticeable CTA area (e.g., a pill-shaped 'Start Quote for this Policy' button or link) anchored at the bottom-left. Vertical spacing inside the card uses 8–16px increments to avoid crowding. Cards in the same row align their top edges, and rows are vertically separated by approximately 24–32px. This creates a clean, scannable matrix of options that feels consistent with the broader grid.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Contained within the policy-type-summary-list’s grid; no independent sticky or floating behavior. Cards reflow based on screen size but maintain consistent internal padding and spacing.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The generic-quote-cta is the primary sidebar feature and sits in the right column, directly aligned with the top of the coverage-intro-section’s headline. It occupies about 4–5 of the 12 grid columns (35–40% of the total content width) and rests on a very light gray sidebar background that subtly distinguishes it from the main content area. The CTA itself is a vertically oriented white card with rounded corners, 24px internal padding, and a soft, low-elevation shadow. Near the top of the card is a concise question or title (e.g., 'Ready to protect your boat?') followed by a short reassuring line about starting a quote. Below this copy, centered or left-aligned, is a large red pill-shaped button labeled something like 'Request a Quote', spanning roughly 70–80% of the card’s width with 12–16px vertical and 24–32px horizontal padding. If there are secondary actions within this component (e.g., a text link or muted button for 'Talk to an agent'), they appear beneath the primary button with 16–24px vertical separation and subdued styling, ensuring the red primary CTA remains dominant. The height of the card takes up roughly 35–45% of the visible sidebar area above the fold, leaving comfortable open space below and maintaining a light, uncluttered feel. The card scrolls naturally as the user moves through the page but visually anchors the right side of the F-pattern.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static sidebar card positioned within the right column; scrolls with the content and is not sticky. On tablet and mobile, it reflows below the main content sections, becoming a full-width card with top and bottom margins of about 24–32px.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The compare-policy-types-link appears at the bottom of the left main column, directly following the policy-type-summary-list. It is separated from the last row of policy-type-summary-card components by a modest vertical gap of about 24px, enough to indicate it is a footer-level action for that section. The link is left-aligned with the headings and card content but can be styled to visually extend slightly toward the right using an arrow icon or underline, subtly echoing the horizontal part of the F-pattern at the content’s end. The text is concise, such as 'Compare all policy types', and is set in the standard body font with a brand-red color and perhaps a subtle hover underline. The link sits alone on its line, surrounded by ample white space above and a larger 32–40px margin below before any subsequent page content or footer, making it easy to spot without competing with primary CTAs.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline text-level navigation element anchored in the left column. It scrolls normally and has no sticky or floating behavior; on smaller screens it remains left-aligned within the full-width content area.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This is a non-visual state management component that listens to URL parameters or hash changes to adjust which coverage or policy type content is emphasized. It does not produce any visible UI and therefore has no explicit footprint in the layout. Conceptually, it is positioned at the same hierarchy level as the coverage-overview-data-loader, near the top of the page’s component tree, ensuring its logic can influence the intro, lists, and CTAs without disrupting the spatial design.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual, zero-size component. It does not impact spacing, positioning, or scroll behavior; it purely manages state in the background.

name: Policy Comparison
title: Policy Comparison
description: Compare available boat policy types side by side, highlight specific coverage features, see which policies meet your selected criteria, and start a quote for a chosen policy type.

core_features: Fetch and display a dynamic comparison of policy types and their coverage features using the getPolicyComparison interface, driven by URL parameters for highlighted features.
Allow users to select and adjust highlighted coverage features and whether all selected features are required, updating the URL parameters and reloading the comparison via getPolicyComparison.
Visually identify which policy types meet the current highlighted-feature criteria using the meetsHighlightedCriteria value from getPolicyComparison.
Display a recommended policy type, based on recommendedPolicyTypeId from getPolicyComparison, with clear indication within the comparison.
Provide per-policy actions including a "View Details" navigation to policy_type_detail.html with the selected policyTypeId.
Provide per-policy actions to start a quote using startQuoteForPolicyType and then navigate to quote_boat_details.html with entrySource and policyTypeId in the URL.
user_workflows:
Workflow 1 – Arrive with preselected feature highlights from coverage overview:
1. User clicks a comparison-related link on coverage_overview.html that includes highlightFeatureCodes and optionally requireAllHighlightedFeatures in the URL.
2. Policy Comparison page reads highlightFeatureCodes and requireAllHighlightedFeatures from the URL query string.
3. Page calls getPolicyComparison with these parameters.
4. The comparison table renders features and policy types, with indicated highlighted features and meetsHighlightedCriteria flags.
5. User reviews which policy types satisfy their highlighted features and sees any recommended policy type.
6. User clicks "Get a Quote" for a chosen policy type.
7. Page calls startQuoteForPolicyType with the selected policyTypeId, an optional preferredLiabilityLimit (if provided by the UI), and entrySource set to "policy_comparison_page".
8. On success, the page navigates to quote_boat_details.html with entrySource=policy_comparison_page and policyTypeId set to the selected policy type.

Workflow 2 – Adjust feature highlights directly on this page:
1. User lands on policy_comparison.html without any highlightFeatureCodes in the URL.
2. Page calls getPolicyComparison with empty or default highlightFeatureCodes and requireAllHighlightedFeatures=false (or absent), rendering the unfiltered comparison.
3. User selects one or more features to highlight and optionally toggles the setting that all selected features must be included.
4. Component updates the URL query string to reflect the new highlightFeatureCodes and requireAllHighlightedFeatures values.
5. Page re-calls getPolicyComparison with updated parameters.
6. Table updates to show which policy types now meet the highlighted criteria, and any new recommendedPolicyTypeId.
7. User either clicks "View Details" under a policy type to navigate to policy_type_detail.html with policyTypeId in the URL, or clicks "Get a Quote" to follow the quoting steps as in Workflow 1.

Workflow 3 – Explore details for a recommended policy type:
1. Page loads and calls getPolicyComparison based on current URL parameters.
2. Response includes a recommendedPolicyTypeId that matches one of the returned policyTypes.
3. The recommendation component locates that policy type in the comparison data and displays its name and key indicators.
4. User clicks an action within the recommendation to either:
   - View more information: navigate to policy_type_detail.html with policyTypeId set to the recommended policy type id, or
   - Start a quote: trigger startQuoteForPolicyType with policyTypeId equal to recommendedPolicyTypeId, then navigate to quote_boat_details.html with entrySource and policyTypeId in the URL.

Workflow 4 – Change highlight requirement behavior (any vs all):
1. User has already selected multiple highlight features using the highlight controls.
2. User toggles the option indicating whether all selected features are required.
3. Component updates the requireAllHighlightedFeatures value in the URL query string while keeping highlightFeatureCodes unchanged.
4. Page re-calls getPolicyComparison with updated requireAllHighlightedFeatures.
5. PolicyType columns update their meetsHighlightedCriteria status according to the new rule; recommendedPolicyTypeId may also change.
6. User proceeds to view details or start a quote based on the updated recommendation.
interactions: Initial load: On page initialization, read highlightFeatureCodes and requireAllHighlightedFeatures from the URL query string and call getPolicyComparison with those values.
Change highlighted features: When the user selects or deselects a coverage feature in the highlight controls, update highlightFeatureCodes in the URL query string and re-call getPolicyComparison.
Toggle requirement mode: When the user toggles the option for requiring all highlighted features, update requireAllHighlightedFeatures in the URL query string and re-call getPolicyComparison.

View policy details: When the user clicks "View Details" for a policy type, navigate to policy_type_detail.html with policyTypeId set to the selected policy type’s identifier from getPolicyComparison.

Start quote from table: When the user clicks "Get a Quote" for a policy type in the comparison table, call startQuoteForPolicyType with policyTypeId from the selected column, an optional preferredLiabilityLimit chosen in the UI (if any), and entrySource="policy_comparison_page"; upon success, navigate to quote_boat_details.html with entrySource and policyTypeId in the URL.

Start quote from recommendation: When the user starts a quote from a recommended policy section, call startQuoteForPolicyType with policyTypeId set to recommendedPolicyTypeId and entrySource="policy_comparison_page"; on success, navigate to quote_boat_details.html with entrySource and policyTypeId.

Refine liability preference before quote (optional): If the table allows the user to select a preferred liability limit for a policy type, capture that value and pass it as preferredLiabilityLimit to startQuoteForPolicyType for that policy type.

Row-level feature focus: When the user clicks or taps on a specific feature row within the comparison table, update the highlight controls to include that feature’s code and re-call getPolicyComparison so the feature is treated consistently across the page.
page_functionality.state_logic: The page state is fully driven by URL query parameters and API responses. On initialization and whenever filters change, the page reads the following parameters from the URL: highlightFeatureCodes (string) and requireAllHighlightedFeatures (boolean-like). If highlightFeatureCodes is present and non-empty, it is passed directly to getPolicyComparison in the same format. If it is absent or empty, the page passes a value that indicates no specific features are highlighted, relying on the API’s default behavior. For requireAllHighlightedFeatures, the page interprets the query value (e.g., 'true'/'false') into a boolean and passes it to getPolicyComparison; if it is absent, the page may treat it as false by default and pass that to the API. After each user interaction that changes the selected features or requirement mode, the page updates the URL query string via navigation APIs (e.g., pushState or by reconstructing the URL) so that highlightFeatureCodes and requireAllHighlightedFeatures always reflect the current UI state. This ensures the state is shareable and restorable via URL. The getPolicyComparison response (features, policyTypes, recommendedPolicyTypeId) is not stored in URL parameters; it is transient page state used to render the comparison and drive interactions. Navigation to policy_type_detail.html includes policyTypeId in the URL query string so that the detail page can fetch its own data. When starting a quote, the page calls startQuoteForPolicyType with the selected policyTypeId, optional preferredLiabilityLimit, and entrySource='policy_comparison_page'. Upon a successful response, the page navigates to quote_boat_details.html, including entrySource=policy_comparison_page and policyTypeId in the URL query string. The quoteNumber and other values returned from startQuoteForPolicyType are handled by server-side mechanisms or subsequent pages and are not persisted as URL parameters on this page.

components.functionality: Reads URL parameters for highlightFeatureCodes and requireAllHighlightedFeatures, calls getPolicyComparison with those parameters, and exposes the returned comparison data to downstream components.

Allows users to choose which coverage features to highlight and whether all selected features must be included by a policy type. Updates URL parameters and triggers a refresh of the comparison data via getPolicyComparison.

Displays a side-by-side comparison matrix of policy types and coverage features. Uses the features and policyTypes arrays from getPolicyComparison to render rows and columns, indicates which features are included for each policy type, visually emphasizes highlighted features, and marks policy types that meet the current highlighted criteria.

Uses recommendedPolicyTypeId from getPolicyComparison to identify the recommended policy type within the policyTypes list, summarizes its key attributes (such as relative position and whether it meets highlighted criteria), and offers actions to view details or start a quote.
Handles navigation to policy_type_detail.html when users click any "View Details" action in the table or recommendation components, appending the appropriate policyTypeId as a URL query parameter.

Invokes the startQuoteForPolicyType interface when a user initiates a quote from any policy type (in the table or recommendation), passing policyTypeId, an optional preferredLiabilityLimit selected in the UI, and entrySource='policy_comparison_page'. On success, redirects the user to quote_boat_details.html with entrySource and policyTypeId in the URL.

Keeps the page’s filter UI in sync with the current URL parameters. When URL query parameters change (via back/forward navigation or programmatic updates), it updates the highlight controls accordingly and triggers the data-loader to re-fetch comparison data.

Manages loading and error feedback for the getPolicyComparison and startQuoteForPolicyType calls, controlling whether the comparison table and actions are interactive and displaying appropriate messaging when requests are in progress or fail.

layout.grid_system: Likely a 12-column fixed-width grid (approx. 1140–1200px) centered within the viewport, with the left 7–8 columns for main content and right 4–5 columns for the sidebar CTA/agent column.
layout.layout_pattern: The page uses a two-column grid under a full-width top navigation (assumed from the design DNA). A centered, fixed-width content container (approx. 1140–1200px) is split into a wider left main column and a narrower right sidebar. In the left column, content flows linearly from a page heading and explanatory copy into feature-highlight controls, then a navigation row for different comparison views, then the comparison table, followed by a recommendation summary. The right column begins at the same vertical height as the left column heading and contains a prominent quote-start card as the topmost element. Non-visual components (data loading, URL sync, handlers) are logically attached to the main container but do not interrupt the visual flow.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page uses a two-column grid under a full-width top navigation (assumed from the design DNA). A centered, fixed-width content container (approx. 1140–1200px) is split into a wider left main column and a narrower right sidebar. In the left column, content flows linearly from a page heading and explanatory copy into feature-highlight controls, then a navigation row for different comparison views, then the comparison table, followed by a recommendation summary. The right column begins at the same vertical height as the left column heading and contains a prominent quote-start card as the topmost element. Non-visual components (data loading, URL sync, handlers) are logically attached to the main container but do not interrupt the visual flow.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The main content container spans about 80–90% of the viewport width up to a max of ~1140–1200px, centered. Within it, the left column occupies roughly 60–65% of the width (about 7–8 grid columns) and the right sidebar about 35–40% (about 4–5 grid columns). Vertical spacing between major blocks (filters, navigator, table, summary, sidebar card stacks) is around 24–32px, with internal card padding around 20–24px. The hero band (page title + left controls paired with the right quote card) occupies roughly the top 35–45% of the initial viewport height on desktop before the user scrolls into the deeper comparison table rows.
layout_strategies.content_density: Density is medium-low. Each major section (filters, navigator, table, summary, sidebar quote card) has clear separation and breathing room using 24–32px gaps. Controls like filter chips are laid out in rows with ample horizontal and vertical spacing, inspired by the location chip grid from the mockup. The comparison table is information-dense but framed within generous padding from the container edges and separated from adjacent sections with whitespace rather than heavy dividers.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Visual flow follows an F-shaped pattern: users read left-to-right across the top nav and page heading, then their attention is pulled right to the bold red pill-shaped 'Start Quote' CTA in the sidebar. From there, the eye returns to the left and scans downward through the feature-highlight controls, the comparison mode navigator, and then the policy comparison table. Secondary attention flows down the right sidebar, staying anchored to the sticky quote card while the left column scrolls, before ending at any additional contextual actions (if present). Feedback states (loading/error) appear inline above the table, briefly becoming the focal point without disrupting the established two-column balance.

layout.overall_description: Inside a centered, fixed-width page container, the layout resolves into a clean two-column marketing/comparison view. The left column, slightly wider, opens with a strong page title and short description about comparing boat policies, immediately followed by a grid of pill-shaped feature-highlight controls that echo the chip styling from the original mockup. Just below, a slim navigation row lets the user switch comparison modes or detail views, and beneath that the main comparison table stretches down the page in an airy but information-focused block. Below the table, a softly framed recommendation summary card reinforces which policy best matches the selected criteria. On the right, starting level with the page heading, a white card with subtle shadow contains a concise question and a bold red pill CTA to start a quote for the selected policy type; this card anchors the right sidebar and remains visible as the user scrolls. The overall effect is a modern, corporate marketing layout where the primary comparison experience lives on the spacious left, while the right column consistently promotes conversion with a focused, trustworthy quote-start panel.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This component is mounted at the root of the main content container, logically just inside the two-column layout wrapper but does not render any visible UI. It occupies no width or height and does not affect the grid or spacing. Visually, the page appears identical whether it is present or not; it simply ensures that by the time the user sees the left and right columns, the necessary policy data has begun loading.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual background behavior; no spatial footprint and no effect on document flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: In the left column, directly below the page heading and a short explanatory line of text, the feature-highlight-controls component spans the full width of the left column (approximately 60–65% of the total page width). It is laid out as a horizontal row (or multiple wrapped rows) of pill-shaped toggle chips, visually echoing the location chip grid from the mockup. Each chip has generous horizontal padding and a small gap (8–12px) between neighbors, with 16–24px vertical spacing between rows, sitting on a white or very light gray background with around 16–20px padding above and below the chip cluster. The entire block sits about 24–32px below the heading, forming the top of the main interactive area in the left column.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Participates in normal document flow within the left column; it expands vertically as chips wrap to new rows, pushing the content below downward but without any fixed or sticky behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The policy-comparison-table fills the width of the left column below the feature-highlight-controls and the policy-details-navigator, beginning roughly one screenful down from the top nav depending on filter height. It is framed with 24–32px of whitespace above and 32px below to separate it from adjacent sections. The table’s outer edges align with the left column grid, creating a clean vertical line from the filters above. Internally, the table uses a flat design with subtle horizontal rules and a lightly emphasized header row; it typically occupies the majority of the vertical space below the fold, making it the dominant content block in the left column. On first view, the top portion of the table is visible along with the right sidebar quote card, reinforcing the side-by-side comparison and CTA pairing.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Flows normally within the left column, expanding vertically as rows are added. The table can internally support a sticky header row that stays at the top of the table’s scrollable area, but the table itself is not fixed and scrolls with the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Positioned directly below the policy-comparison-table in the left column, the policy-recommendation-summary appears as a full-width card aligned to the same grid edges as the table. It sits about 32px beneath the table, with 20–24px of internal padding on all sides, a white or slightly tinted background, a fine border or hairline shadow, and rounded corners consistent with the design DNA.
