If sortBy is missing from the URL, the page may use a default sort order (defined externally, not hardcoded here) and reflect that in both state and UI. When the user changes sort order, only the sortBy query parameter is updated; quoteId is preserved. A new call to getQuotePlans is then made using the updated sortBy. When the user selects a plan, no URL changes occur until after selectPlanForCurrentQuote returns success; at that point, navigation occurs to quote_plan_summary.html with quoteId set to the returned quoteNumber. When the user navigates back to coverage options, the page constructs a URL to quote_coverage_options.html with quoteId set to the quoteNumber from getQuotePlans. No localStorage or other client-side persistence mechanisms are used; returning to this page always relies on the quoteId and sortBy URL parameters and fresh calls to getQuotePlans to reconstruct state.

components.functionality: Coordinates initial data loading and high-level state for the plans list page. On initialization, reads quoteId and sortBy from the URL, ensures the active quote context is set based on quoteId, and calls getQuotePlans with the current sortBy. Stores quoteNumber, sortBy, and plans in page-level state, and exposes them to child components. Handles global error state for failed getQuotePlans calls.

Displays contextual information for the current quote, such as an identifier label using getQuotePlans.quoteNumber, and a brief functional description or guidance on how to select a plan. It does not hardcode any quote-specific values and updates automatically when page controller state changes.

Provides a control (for example, a select element or radio group) for choosing the current sort order of plans. It is bound to the current sortBy state. When the user changes the selection, it updates the sortBy URL parameter and triggers a new getQuotePlans call via the page controller.

Allows the user to switch how plan prices are displayed (for example, between showing monthly totals and annual totals). This component only manages local display state and chooses between price fields already present in each plan object (such as totalPriceMonthly and totalPriceAnnual or basePriceMonthly and basePriceAnnual) without calling any interfaces.

Renders the collection of plans returned by getQuotePlans in a list or tabular structure. It uses sortBy and displayOrder from the plan data to determine the order if needed, but does not implement its own sorting beyond what is already provided by getQuotePlans. Each plan is passed to a child plan-row component for detailed rendering.

Displays a single plan’s details, including name, policyTypeName, policyTypeCode, liabilityLimit, deductible, pricing fields, isFeatured, and isAvailableOnline. It embeds a key-features-list component for showing the plan’s keyFeatures. If isAvailableOnline is true, it shows an active "Select Plan" action; otherwise, the select action is disabled or non-interactive. It exposes the planId for use when the select action is invoked.

Takes the keyFeatures array from a plan and displays each feature, indicating whether it is included or not based on isIncluded. Optionally supports an expand/collapse interaction to show more or fewer features for readability. It is purely presentational in terms of data use, with all data coming from getQuotePlans.

Encapsulates the logic for the "Select Plan" button associated with a plan row. When activated for a plan that is available online, it calls selectPlanForCurrentQuote with that planId, handles loading and error states for the action, and, on success, reads quoteNumber and nextStep from the response before navigating the browser to quote_plan_summary.html with quoteId set to quoteNumber.

Provides a navigation control to return to the coverage options step for the current quote. It constructs a link to quote_coverage_options.html using quoteId equal to the quoteNumber returned by getQuotePlans, ensuring consistency with outgoing navigation requirements.

Displays an informative state when getQuotePlans returns no plans or when the call fails. In an empty state, it indicates that no plans are currently available for the quote and may prompt the user to adjust coverage (via the back-to-coverage-link). In an error state, it shows that loading failed and offers a retry control. It uses no hardcoded plan data; content is generic and driven by an error/empty flag in page state.

layout.grid_system: Likely a 12-column fixed-width grid (approx. 1140–1200px) centered within the viewport, with the left 7–8 columns for main content and right 4–5 columns for the sidebar CTA/agent column.
layout.layout_pattern: Use a two-column layout beneath the existing full-width site navigation. The left column is the main working area for the quote plans: it starts with a large, bold context header for the quote, followed by a compact control band (sorting and billing toggle), and then a vertical stack of plan cards. Each plan card contains its own hierarchy: plan summary row at the top, key features beneath, and a prominent select button on the right. A back-navigation link sits below the list as a subtle footer element for this flow. The right column is reserved as a sidebar zone controlled by the page controller for supporting quote information and actions (e.g., summary, help, agent info), following the mockup’s pattern of a primary main column plus a functional sidebar.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Use a two-column layout beneath the existing full-width site navigation. The left column is the main working area for the quote plans: it starts with a large, bold context header for the quote, followed by a compact control band (sorting and billing toggle), and then a vertical stack of plan cards. Each plan card contains its own hierarchy: plan summary row at the top, key features beneath, and a prominent select button on the right. A back-navigation link sits below the list as a subtle footer element for this flow. The right column is reserved as a sidebar zone controlled by the page controller for supporting quote information and actions (e.g., summary, help, agent info), following the mockup’s pattern of a primary main column plus a functional sidebar.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The content sits in a centered fixed-width container of roughly 1140–1200px. Inside this, the left main column occupies about 60–65% of the width, while the right sidebar occupies 35–40%. Vertically, the header plus sorting/toggle band take up the upper portion of the main column above the fold (roughly the top 25–30% of the page content area), with the top two to three plan cards visible below them. Internal padding for cards and controls follows an 8px-based scale (around 16–24px padding inside cards and 12–16px vertical, 24–32px horizontal padding for buttons). Vertical gaps between sections and between plan cards are in the 24–32px range.
layout_strategies.content_density: Maintain a medium-low content density. The header, sorting form, and billing toggle have clear whitespace above and below, not crowding the top of the page. Plan rows are card-based with generous padding and clear separation between cards so each plan feels scannable. Key features are shown in a compact but not crowded list, likely arranged into two columns on wide screens to avoid long vertical lists. Controls are not stacked too tightly; each functional group has breathing room that reflects a modern, calm, corporate marketing style rather than a dense comparison table.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow mirrors the mockup’s F-pattern. The user’s eye moves from the global nav/logo across the top to the quote plans header in the left column, then to the right sidebar’s top card area (if populated by the controller). From there, attention drops down the left column through the sorting/toggle band to the first plan card. Within each card, the eye scans left-to-right across the plan name and pricing to the bold red pill-shaped ‘Select Plan’ CTA on the right, then down into the key features. Secondary elements (back link, status messages) sit lower or in reduced visual weight. As the user scrolls, the right sidebar remains visible (sticky), providing a vertical anchor while the main column’s plan list scrolls.
layout_strategies.overall_layout_description: The page presents as a clean, corporate quote selection screen with a strong left main column and a supportive right sidebar, all contained within a centered, fixed-width layout. At the top of the main column, a bold quote header acts as the hero, clearly stating the plan selection context for the active boat insurance quote. Directly below it, a horizontal control band with sort options on the left and a pill-shaped billing toggle on the right sets how plans are shown. A series of white plan cards then cascades down the left, each card spanning the full width of that column, with strong plan titles, pricing, and a prominent red ‘Select Plan’ button aligned to the right. Key features are neatly listed within each card, making comparisons easy without feeling cramped. A light, understated back link sits at the bottom-left of the main column, allowing users to step back to coverage details. Meanwhile, the right column hosts a sticky sidebar zone defined by the page controller, visually echoing the mockup’s quote/agent panel by reserving space for supporting information and actions. The overall effect is a trustworthy, functional layout where the primary task—choosing a plan—is obvious and well-supported by clear hierarchy and ample breathing room.

layout.overall_description: The page uses a modern two-column layout within a centered 1140–1200px content area on a light gray or off-white background, echoing the original mockup. The left column, taking roughly two-thirds of the width, is the main working area: a strong, red-accented quote header at the top anchors the page, followed by a horizontally aligned band of controls that let the user sort plans and toggle billing frequency. Beneath this, a stack of white plan cards fills the column, each card generously padded and separated by consistent vertical gaps. Each plan card has a clear top row showing the plan name and price on the left and a large red pill-shaped ‘Select Plan’ button on the right, with key features listed below in a tidy, scannable arrangement. At the bottom of the left column, a low-key text link lets users go back to adjust their coverage. The right column, about one-third of the width, forms a sticky sidebar starting at the same vertical level as the main header; it is reserved for supporting content controlled by the page controller, such as quote details or guidance, creating a balanced, marketing-style composition. Users naturally scan from the header to the control band, into the first plan card and its CTA, then continue down the list, all while the right sidebar provides a stable, contextual anchor as they scroll.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The quote plans page controller defines the overall page canvas and two-column grid beneath the site-wide navigation. It positions a centered fixed-width content container (approximately 1140–1200px wide) within the viewport, with equal left and right margins. Inside this container, it establishes a left main content column occupying about 60–65% of the width and a right sidebar column occupying about 35–40%. Both columns start at the same vertical offset from the bottom of the top navigation (about 48–64px of top padding), mirroring the hero alignment from the mockup. The controller ensures consistent vertical spacing between primary sections: around 24–32px between the header and the control band, 24–32px between the control band and the plan list, and 24–32px between stacked cards or blocks. It also sets a slightly darker, very subtle background for the sidebar column so that any white cards placed there sit clearly against it, just as the quote card does in the original mockup. All main-column components (header, sort form, toggle, list, back link, and status states) are placed within the left column, while the right column is reserved for supporting content and can remain visually present even as the left column scrolls.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Defines a fixed-width, centered two-column grid. The right sidebar column is configured to allow its topmost card or content to behave as sticky, remaining anchored near the top of the viewport (with an offset of about 24–32px from the bottom of the global nav) while the left main column scrolls. The controller itself does not scroll independently; it establishes the structural flow for all child components.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The quote plans header sits at the top of the left main column, directly beneath the global navigation and aligned flush with the left edge of the main content column. It acts as the hero text for this page, spanning nearly the full width of the left column (roughly 90–100% of the left column width) with generous top padding of about 48–64px from the nav and 16–24px bottom padding before the next element. The primary line is a large, bold, left-aligned headline in the brand’s red or dark gray, clearly stating that the user is choosing a plan for their boat insurance quote. If a subtitle or contextual line (e.g., boat name, location, quote reference) is present, it appears directly underneath the main headline with a smaller font size and a 8–12px vertical gap, still left-aligned and limited to the same text width. The header’s horizontal alignment visually matches the left edge of the sort form and plan cards below, forming a clean vertical spine on the left.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Participates in the normal document flow at the top of the main column. It does not scroll independently or stick; as the user scrolls, it moves out of view while controls and plans scroll up under it.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The plan sort form is positioned in the left column directly beneath the quote plans header, with a vertical gap of about 24–32px to keep the hero header visually distinct. It forms the left half of a horizontal control band: the sort label and its dropdown or select control are left-aligned with the header above and occupy roughly 55–60% of the left column’s width. The primary sort dropdown uses a medium width (about 240–320px) so it feels substantial but not oversized, and it is vertically centered within this control band. There is 16–24px of internal spacing between the label and the control itself, and the entire sort form has about 8–16px of top and bottom breathing room within its row. To the right of the sort form, aligned on the same baseline, sits the billing display toggle, creating a single, cohesive horizontal strip of controls that users can scan in one pass.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Sits in the normal flow within the left column. On narrower viewports (e.g., tablet), it can wrap so that the billing toggle drops below the sort control, still maintaining a 16–24px vertical gap between the two, but on desktop it is intended to remain in a single horizontal row with the billing toggle.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The billing display toggle is placed on the right side of the same horizontal control band that contains the sort form, within the left main column. Its left edge aligns roughly at 60–65% of the left column width, leaving a consistent horizontal gap of about 24–32px from the right edge of the sort form. The toggle is right-aligned within the control band, occupying about 30–35% of the left column width, and appears as a pill-shaped segmented control (e.g., ‘Monthly’ vs. ‘Annual’) with a subtle border and active segment filled in the brand red. It is vertically centered with the sort dropdown so that the entire band looks balanced. There is minimal vertical padding around the text within the pill segments (about 8–12px), and 16–24px horizontal padding for each segment, giving it a substantial, tap-friendly feel without dominating the header.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Flows inline within the control band. On smaller widths, it can wrap below the sort form and center itself horizontally, but on desktop it remains on the same row, visually anchoring the right side of the main column’s upper controls.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The plans list sits directly beneath the control band (sort form and billing toggle) in the left main column, separated from it by a vertical gap of about 24–32px. It spans nearly the full width of the left column (about 100% of the available width minus standard inner padding) and acts as a stack container for individual plan row cards. The list begins high enough that at least the first one to two plan cards are visible above the fold on common desktop resolutions. Each plan row within this list is separated by a consistent vertical spacing of about 24px, maintaining the medium-low density of the layout. The list itself does not add additional borders or visuals; instead, the individual plan cards provide the visual structure. When the list is long, it scrolls within the left column under the fixed-height header and sticky sidebar on the right, maintaining clear left and right margins within the column.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Acts as a vertical stack container in normal document flow. It expands in height as more plans are present and scrolls with the page. When there are no plans or an error occurs, it yields its vertical space to the plans-empty-or-error-state component, which occupies the same position in the layout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Each plan row is rendered as a card-like block within the plans list, occupying the full available width of the left column (with a small inset margin if desired, e.g., 8–16px per side) and sitting on a white background with a very light shadow or soft border. The card has generous internal padding of about 20–24px on all sides. At the top of the card, a primary horizontal strip runs left to right: on the left side (roughly 60–65% of the card width), the plan name appears as a bold, mid-sized heading, with supporting details such as coverage level or taglines directly beneath it with an 8–12px gap; on the right side (roughly 35–40% of the card width), the price and billing frequency are displayed near the top with the large pill-shaped ‘Select Plan’ button aligned to the far right, vertically centered in this top strip. The price and CTA are visually grouped and visually heavier than the supporting text. Below this top row, separated by either 12–16px of vertical space or a subtle divider line, the key-features-list occupies the full width of the card, providing a scannable list of highlights. Overall, each plan row is tall enough that cards feel distinct and easy to scan, with about 24px of vertical gap between each card and the next.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Participates in the vertical stacking within plans-list. The card height is flexible, expanding to accommodate the number of features and any supplementary labels. It does not have fixed positioning; it scrolls normally within the left column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The key features list lives inside each plan row card, positioned beneath the top strip that contains the plan name, details, price, and select button. It begins after a vertical gap or divider of about 12–16px from the top row, and aligns with the left edge of the plan name, maintaining a clean vertical alignment. On desktop widths, features are arranged in one or two columns depending on the amount of text: for typical content, a two-column layout within the card is preferred, with each feature represented as a short line or bullet, allowing users to scan across and down without excessive scrolling. Each feature item has about 4–8px vertical spacing from the next, providing enough separation for readability while maintaining compactness. The overall key features area usually stays within the lower half of the card’s height, leaving some breathing room below (about 12–16px of bottom padding) before the end of the card.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as an internal layout within each plan-row card. It flows vertically and, where implemented, may use a simple responsive grid (e.g., flex or CSS grid) to switch from two columns on wider screens to a single column on narrow ones, but it does not change the card’s overall alignment or positioning.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The select plan action appears as a large, pill-shaped primary button within each plan row, occupying the top-right portion of the card’s content area. It is horizontally aligned flush to the right edge of the card’s inner padding, and vertically centered within the top row that contains the plan name and price. The button’s width hugs its label text with generous horizontal padding (around 24–32px) and vertical padding (about 12–16px), making it clearly tap/click-friendly. In line with the design DNA, the button uses the brand’s red as a solid fill, with white text and possibly subtle rounded pill edges to match the broader system. The spacing between the price text and the button is about 8–16px, ensuring they are visually related but not cramped. The button is intended to be the single most visually prominent element within each card, drawing the eye to the primary action after users scan the plan details.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Stays fixed within the top-right of its plan-row card, aligned to the card’s internal layout. It remains visible as long as the card is on screen and does not float or stick beyond the card’s bounds. On smaller screens, it may wrap beneath the price text but still appears near the top of the card, separated by a 8–12px vertical gap.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The back to coverage link is positioned at the bottom of the left main column, just below the plans list (or below the last plan card in the list). It is aligned with the left edge of the main content column, maintaining the same vertical alignment as the header and plan cards. A vertical gap of about 32–40px separates it from the last plan card, visually signaling the end of the plan options section. The link appears as a small to medium-sized text link with a muted color (e.g., dark gray or less saturated red) and may include a left-facing arrow icon. It does not span the full width; instead, it occupies only the space needed for its text, leaving ample whitespace to the right so it feels light and secondary compared to the primary CTAs above.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Participates in the normal vertical flow at the bottom of the main column. As the page scrolls, it moves off screen along with the rest of the left column content; it is not sticky or fixed and is primarily encountered when the user reaches the end of the plan options.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The plans empty or error state occupies the same position in the left main column where the plans list would ordinarily appear. It is placed directly beneath the control band (sort form and billing toggle), with a vertical gap of about 24–32px. Instead of multiple cards, it is presented as a single, centered status block. This block spans roughly 60–70% of the left column width and is horizontally centered within that column, leaving comfortable whitespace on either side. Inside, there is a concise headline (e.g., indicating no plans found or an error occurred), possibly in a slightly larger font and a neutral color, with one or two lines of explanatory text beneath it separated by 8–12px. If there are any recovery actions (e.g., a retry button or link), they appear beneath the message with 16–24px of vertical spacing, centered. The status block has 24–32px of padding all around and may sit on a very subtle card background to differentiate it from the page background, while still reflecting the clean, corporate style. The right sidebar remains unchanged, providing context even when no plans are listed.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Conditionally replaces the visible content of the plans-list area. When active, it occupies the vertical space that would otherwise be used by plan cards and scrolls normally within the left column. It does not alter the structure or behavior of the header, control band, or right sidebar.

name: Quote  Plan Summary & Options
title: Quote Plan Summary & Options
description: This page displays the selected boat insurance plan for a quote, allows users to adjust optional coverages with real-time pricing, choose a billing frequency, save the quote with a custom label, and begin the applicant information step without leaving the flow.

core_features: Load and display the selected quote’s plan summary, including coverage details, limits, deductibles, and base and total premium amounts using getSelectedPlanSummaryAndOptions.
List all configurable optional coverages for the quote, including current selection state, any limit options, and additional monthly cost, with changes handled via updateQuoteOptionalCoverage.

Track and display the total additional monthly cost of optional coverages, surfacing any budget-related warnings (such as the optional coverage cost exceeding a configured threshold) based on the warnings returned from updateQuoteOptionalCoverage.
Allow users to select a billing frequency (e.g., monthly or annually) and immediately update displayed premiums using setQuoteBillingFrequency.
Provide a form to save the current quote with a user-defined label and show a confirmation including the returned reference number using saveCurrentQuote.
Enable users to start the applicant information step for the current quote and reveal the required fields on this page using beginApplicantInfoForCurrentQuote.
Provide navigation back to the plans list with the active quote carried via URL parameters, and navigation to the account dashboard after saving a quote.
user_workflows: Review selected plan: User arrives on quote_plan_summary.html with a quoteId URL parameter → page initialization associates the quoteId with the active quote context → getSelectedPlanSummaryAndOptions is called → the page shows the plan name, policy type, limits, deductibles, base prices, current billing option and total premiums, and the list of optional coverages with their current selections and costs.

Adjust optional coverages within a budget: User reviews the list of optional coverages → user toggles a coverage on or off or changes its limit → updateQuoteOptionalCoverage is called with optionalCoverageId, selected, and (if applicable) selectedLimit → the response updates that coverage’s selection and cost, the overall optionalCoveragesTotalMonthly, and the totalPremiumMonthly → if the response contains warnings (such as optional_cost_over_threshold), those warnings are displayed so the user can adjust coverages to stay within their desired budget.

Change billing frequency: User selects a different billing option (e.g., monthly vs annually) from the available billingOptions displayed from getSelectedPlanSummaryAndOptions → setQuoteBillingFrequency is called with the chosen billingFrequency → on success, totalPremiumMonthly and totalPremiumAnnual on the page are updated and any returned message is shown to the user.

Save quote with custom label: User clicks a control to save the current quote → a form prompts for a custom label → user submits the form → saveCurrentQuote is called with the label → on success, a confirmation area displays the label, quoteNumber, referenceNumber, savedAt, and message from the response → links within the confirmation allow navigation to account_dashboard.html to review saved quotes or policies.

Begin applicant information step within the same flow: After confirming plan options and billing, user clicks a button to continue to applicant information → beginApplicantInfoForCurrentQuote is called → on success, step is returned as 'applicant_info' along with a list of requiredFields → the page updates its state (and URL parameters) to indicate the applicant_info step is active and renders a dynamic applicant information section based on requiredFields so the user can begin entering their details.

Return to plan selection: If the user wants to compare or change plans instead of adjusting options, they click 'Back to Plans' → the page navigates to quote_plans.html with a quoteId URL parameter set to the current quoteNumber so the plans page can reload the corresponding quote context.
interactions: On initial page load, read the quoteId from the URL query string; if present, establish it as the active quote context and call getSelectedPlanSummaryAndOptions to populate plan, billingOptions, optionalCoverages, optionalCoveragesTotalMonthly, and canSaveQuote.

When the user changes the selected billing option (e.g., via radio buttons or a select control) the page calls setQuoteBillingFrequency with the chosen billingFrequency and updates displayed premiums using the returned totalPremiumMonthly and totalPremiumAnnual.

When the user toggles an optional coverage on or off, the page calls updateQuoteOptionalCoverage with optionalCoverageId and selected, plus the current selectedLimit if applicable; after the response, the component updates that coverage row, the overall optionalCoveragesTotalMonthly, totalPremiumMonthly, and displays any returned warnings.

When the user changes a limit for a coverage that supports limits, the page calls updateQuoteOptionalCoverage with optionalCoverageId, selected (which should remain true when adjusting an already-selected coverage), and the new selectedLimit; the returned additionalCostMonthly and totals are then shown.

When warnings are returned from updateQuoteOptionalCoverage (such as optional_cost_over_threshold), the page displays these messages in a dedicated budget/warning area associated with optional coverage selection so the user understands that their options exceed a configured threshold and can make changes.

When the user submits the 'Save this quote' form with a label, the page calls saveCurrentQuote with the label; on success, it shows a confirmation area with the returned label, referenceNumber, and message; from that area, the user can click to navigate to account_dashboard.html for their saved quotes or policies.

When the user clicks the 'Continue to Applicant Information' button, the page calls beginApplicantInfoForCurrentQuote; on success, it updates the URL with a step=applicant_info parameter (via history APIs to avoid a full reload) and renders an applicant information section based on requiredFields, allowing the user to begin entering those details.
When the user clicks 'Back to Plans', the page navigates to quote_plans.html with a quoteId URL parameter equal to the current quoteNumber so the plans list can resume for that quote.

If the URL is loaded or updated with step=applicant_info, the page shows both the summary/options area (read-only or still editable, as desired) and the applicant information section without requiring the user to re-trigger beginApplicantInfoForCurrentQuote, relying on the stored step state and previously fetched requiredFields if available.
page_functionality.state_logic: The primary external state identifier for this page is the quoteId URL parameter (e.g., quote_plan_summary.html?quoteId={quoteId}). On initial load, the application reads quoteId and uses it to set the active quote context before calling getSelectedPlanSummaryAndOptions. The response includes quoteNumber, which is used for outgoing navigation (e.g., linking back to quote_plans.html?quoteId={quoteNumber}). Step state for the multi-step flow (plan summary/options vs applicant information) is encoded in a step URL parameter (e.g., step=plan_options or step=applicant_info). When beginApplicantInfoForCurrentQuote succeeds, the page pushes an updated URL with step=applicant_info, so a refresh or direct link can re-open the applicant info state. The page does not rely on localStorage; instead, it reads quoteId and step from the URL on load and re-fetches necessary data from getSelectedPlanSummaryAndOptions, and, when needed, beginApplicantInfoForCurrentQuote. Any pricing and budget thresholds are taken from the interfaces (totalPremium* fields, optionalCoveragesTotalMonthly, and warnings) rather than hardcoded values, ensuring consistent behavior between sessions and avoiding static values on the client.

components.functionality: On page initialization, reads quoteId and step from the URL, sets up the active quote context, and calls getSelectedPlanSummaryAndOptions to populate the plan summary, billing options, and optional coverages. It also interprets the canSaveQuote flag to control visibility of save-quote functionality.

Displays the selected plan’s key information, including plan name, description, policyTypeName, liabilityLimit, deductible, and both base and total premiums derived from the plan and billingOptions data returned by getSelectedPlanSummaryAndOptions. It also shows the quoteNumber to clearly identify the active quote.

Allows the user to choose a billing frequency from the billingOptions returned by getSelectedPlanSummaryAndOptions. It visually indicates which frequency is selected and updates premium amounts when the user changes the selection by calling setQuoteBillingFrequency.

Renders the list of optional coverages from the optionalCoverages array returned by getSelectedPlanSummaryAndOptions. Each row shows the coverage name, description, whether it is selected, any limit options, the currently selected limit (if applicable), and the additionalCostMonthly. It delegates row-level interactions to the optional-coverage-item components and aggregates the optionalCoveragesTotalMonthly for display.

For a single optional coverage, displays selection controls (e.g., checkbox) and limit controls (e.g., dropdown) based on hasLimit and limitOptions. When the user toggles selection or changes a limit, it calls updateQuoteOptionalCoverage with optionalCoverageId, selected state, and selectedLimit (if applicable), and updates its own view with the returned updatedCoverage and additionalCostMonthly.

Displays the current total additional monthly cost of all selected optional coverages using optionalCoveragesTotalMonthly from getSelectedPlanSummaryAndOptions or updateQuoteOptionalCoverage. It also renders any warnings returned from updateQuoteOptionalCoverage (e.g., warnings with code such as optional_cost_over_threshold) to guide the user in keeping optional coverage costs within the configured threshold.

Provides a control for saving the current quote when canSaveQuote is true. It collects a user-defined label and, on submission, calls saveCurrentQuote with that label. Based on the response, it either displays a success confirmation (via the save-quote-confirmation component) or surfaces errors as needed.

Shows the result of saving a quote, including the returned label, quoteNumber, referenceNumber, savedAt, and message from saveCurrentQuote. It offers actions to navigate to account_dashboard.html to view saved quotes or policies, without modifying quote state.

Provides the 'Continue to Applicant Information' action. When clicked, it calls beginApplicantInfoForCurrentQuote. On success (step returned as 'applicant_info'), it updates the URL with step=applicant_info and passes the requiredFields to the applicant-info-form component for rendering.

Renders a set of applicant information fields dynamically based on the requiredFields array returned by beginApplicantInfoForCurrentQuote. It supports capturing the user’s input for each fieldName and marking required fields appropriately. Actual submission of applicant data is handled by subsequent steps or pages outside the scope of this page; this component focuses on rendering and collecting input once the step state is 'applicant_info'.

Provides navigation controls for moving back to the plan selection page or onward to account-related views. 'Back to Plans' constructs a URL to quote_plans.html using the current quoteNumber as the quoteId parameter. After a successful saveCurrentQuote call, 'View All Saved Quotes' and 'Go to My Policy' navigate to account_dashboard.html.

layout.grid_system: Likely a 12-column fixed-width grid (approx. 1140–1200px) centered within the viewport, with the left 7–8 columns for main content and right 4–5 columns for the sidebar CTA/agent column.
layout.layout_pattern: Use a clean two-column layout beneath the existing full-width site header. The left column functions as the main quote configuration area, flowing top-to-bottom: plan summary at the top, then billing frequency selector, then optional coverages list, and finally the applicant information form that can be revealed within the same column. The right column is a stacked sidebar of functional cards: a primary quote overview and applicant-start card at the top (including budget status), followed by the save-quote form / confirmation state, and any secondary navigation links tied to the quote journey. A slim horizontal quote-step navigation bar sits directly below the site nav and above both columns, visually aligning with the grid.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Use a clean two-column layout beneath the existing full-width site header. The left column functions as the main quote configuration area, flowing top-to-bottom: plan summary at the top, then billing frequency selector, then optional coverages list, and finally the applicant information form that can be revealed within the same column. The right column is a stacked sidebar of functional cards: a primary quote overview and applicant-start card at the top (including budget status), followed by the save-quote form / confirmation state, and any secondary navigation links tied to the quote journey. A slim horizontal quote-step navigation bar sits directly below the site nav and above both columns, visually aligning with the grid.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Adopt an ~12-column centered grid with the left content column occupying about 7–8 columns (~60–65% width) and the right sidebar taking 4–5 columns (~35–40% width). Within the sidebar, the top quote overview card occupies the first ~40% of the sidebar height above the fold, with the save-quote card and any navigation/secondary elements stacked beneath. Vertical spacing between major blocks follows 24–32px gaps, with 20–24px of internal padding in each card. Overall content width should stay within ~1140–1200px, centered in the viewport with generous outer margins.
layout_strategies.content_density: Maintain a medium-low density. Each card or section uses clear headings, concise labels, and generous white space around interactive elements. Forms and lists are grouped logically with enough spacing (16–24px) between fields and list items so the interface feels spacious but still information-rich. Optional coverages list can be longer and more text-heavy, but each item remains clearly separated with adequate breathing room.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Guide the eye in an F-shaped pattern: from the global nav across the top to the quote-step navigation links, then down to the prominent plan summary heading on the left. From there, the user’s eye is pulled horizontally to the right by the strong red pill-shaped applicant-start CTA within the top sidebar card. Vertical scanning then continues down the left column through billing options and optional coverages, while secondary attention drops down the right sidebar through the save-quote card and any navigation links. The budget indicator and price information provide quick-glance status points that the eye revisits as options are adjusted.

layout.overall_description: Below the full-width site navigation and logo bar, a slim horizontal quote-step navigation row anchors the top of the content area. Beneath this, the page divides into two columns within a centered, fixed-width container: a wider left column for configuring the quote and a narrower right sidebar for summary and actions. At the top of the left column, a prominent plan summary card introduces the selected boat insurance plan and pricing, followed immediately by a clean billing options selector. Below, a structured list of optional coverages flows down the page, each item in its own lightly outlined row with clear titles, prices, and toggles. When the user chooses to begin the applicant information step, a dynamic form expands at the bottom of the left column, still within the same page context. On the right, a white quote-overview card sits against a very light gray page background, showing a concise premium summary, a budget-status indicator, and a large pill-shaped red button to start applicant info; this card visually aligns with the top of the left plan summary, creating a balanced hero-like band. Below this, a second sidebar card offers a simple 'Save this quote' form, which transitions into a confirmation state once saved. Additional text links for other quote steps are placed near the bottom of the sidebar or under the save card, maintaining clear stacking and 24–32px gaps. The overall effect is a modern, corporate, and highly functional layout where the main configuration work happens on the left, constantly supported by a stable, action-focused summary column on the right.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The data-loader appears as a full-viewport, semi-translucent overlay that sits above all content, including the navigation bar and both columns. It dims the background with a subtle dark or frosted tint while keeping the page structure faintly visible underneath, reinforcing that the user is loading data for this specific plan. In the absolute center of the viewport, a compact white pill-shaped or circular loader area (approximately 280–320px wide) contains a spinner and a short status message. This central loader respects the overall visual style: flat design, soft shadow, and generous padding around the spinner and text (16–24px). When inactive, this component is completely hidden and does not affect the layout of the two-column grid.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed overlay positioned at the viewport level, covering the entire screen when active and non-scrolling; it appears above all other components and disappears once data loading completes.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The plan summary panel is the first and most prominent block in the left column, positioned directly below the quote-plan navigation links and aligned flush with the left edge of the main content grid. It spans the full width of the left column (approximately 60–65% of the total content width) and is presented as a white card with slightly rounded corners and a very subtle drop shadow, consistent with the corporate flat style. Internal padding is generous on all sides (20–24px), with a bold, red headline at the top naming the plan and a prominent premium figure just below it. Supporting details (coverage highlights, deductible, key limits) are arranged in a neat vertical stack or a simple two-column information grid within the card, using 8–16px spacing between text rows. There is roughly 24–32px of vertical space between the bottom of this card and the next component (the billing options selector), making this panel read as the main ‘hero’ of the left column.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the left column; it scrolls naturally with the page content and anchors the top of the configuration flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The billing options selector sits immediately below the plan summary panel in the left column, separated by about 24–32px of vertical whitespace. It spans the full width of the left column and visually reads as a compact control band. At the top of this band is a short label such as 'Billing frequency' aligned to the left, with a light-weight caption style. Below the label, a horizontal row of pill-shaped options (e.g., Monthly, Quarterly, Annual) stretches across 60–80% of the left column width, centered horizontally within the column if space permits. Each pill has 10–12px vertical and 20–28px horizontal padding, with the selected option filled in a solid red and unselected options in white with a thin light-gray outline. There is 8–16px of space between the pills so they don’t feel crowded. The entire selector block has 16–20px padding on top and bottom of its internal content, with a subtle divider line or ample whitespace separating it from the optional coverages list below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the left column; it maintains its position in the vertical flow and expands/wraps pills responsively on smaller screens while staying grouped under the plan summary.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The optional coverages list is placed directly below the billing options selector in the left column, with a 24–32px vertical gap to maintain a clear separation. It occupies the full width of the left column and extends vertically down the page, potentially becoming the tallest content block as more optional items are available. At the top of the list, a concise section heading like 'Optional Coverages' appears left-aligned, with a short descriptive line underneath explaining that changes update the quote in real time. This header is followed by a vertical stack of optional-coverage-item rows. The list itself does not appear as one large card; instead, it reads as a clean section where each item is a discrete card or row with 8–16px vertical spacing between them. The left column background is the same light gray as the page, while each item sits on white or slightly elevated surfaces, ensuring the list feels organized but not dense.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static vertical list in the left column; its height grows with the number of coverage items and pushes subsequent components (like the applicant-info-form) further down the page as needed.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Each optional coverage item appears as an individual white row or mini-card within the optional coverages list, stretching across nearly the full width of the left column but with small horizontal padding (20–24px) on both sides. The row height is comfortable yet compact, with about 16–20px padding top and bottom. Content is arranged in a clear horizontal layout: the coverage name and a one-line description occupy the left 60–70% of the row, aligned to the left. On the right side, the price impact (e.g., '+$8/month') and a toggle control or checkbox are aligned to the right edge, using the remaining 30–40% of the row. The toggle or control is large enough to be easily tappable, with 8–12px of breathing room around it. Items are separated by 8–16px of vertical spacing and may optionally have light bottom borders to enhance separation. When an item is selected, you can subtly emphasize it with a light red outline or a faint background tint, consistent with the flat, low-shadow style. The overall spacing keeps the list readable without feeling cramped.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static rows within the list; they stack vertically and realign their internal content responsively (e.g., stacking price and toggle below the title on narrow screens) while maintaining their overall card-like appearance.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The budget indicator is positioned in the right sidebar within the top quote overview card, just below the key premium figure and above the primary applicant-info-starter button. It occupies the full inner width of that card minus padding (approximately 80–90% of the sidebar width) and appears as a compact status strip: a horizontal bar or pill with a label like 'Within your budget' and a subtle progress-style visualization. The bar might be about 24–32px tall with rounded ends and 16px of spacing above and below, maintaining the spacious feel. The coloring follows a restrained palette: neutral or soft green for a positive status, amber if approaching budget. This placement ensures that when users adjust coverages on the left, they can quickly glance to the right and see the budget status just above the main CTA. There is consistent vertical spacing (12–16px) between this indicator, the premium text above, and the CTA button below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static inside the top sidebar card; it stays in a fixed relative position between the premium summary and the primary CTA, updating content visually without altering the card’s overall layout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The save-quote form is located in the right sidebar directly beneath the top quote overview card, separated from it by a 24–32px vertical gap. It appears as its own white card with rounded corners and a faint shadow, matching the visual style of the other cards. The card width matches the sidebar’s inner width (about 35–40% of the full page content width), with 20–24px of internal padding on all sides. At the top of the card, a small but clear heading like 'Save this quote' is left-aligned. Below the heading, a single text input field for naming or labeling the quote spans nearly the full width, with about 8–12px of spacing above its label and 16px below. Under the input, a medium-sized pill-shaped button (in red or outlined, depending on hierarchy vs. the applicant-info-starter) sits aligned to the left or full width, with 10–12px vertical and 20–28px horizontal padding. Extra helper text or reassurance (e.g., 'We’ll email you a link to return later') can sit below the button with 8–12px top spacing. The spacing and sized elements make this card feel secondary but still important and easy to complete quickly.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the right sidebar; it remains stacked under the top card and above any additional navigation links, scrolling with the page while maintaining its relative spacing.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The save-quote confirmation appears in the same vertical region as the save-quote form, using its footprint in the right sidebar. Once the quote is saved, the form either collapses or is replaced by a slimmer confirmation panel within the same card frame. This confirmation panel retains the card’s white background, rounded corners, and 20–24px padding. Inside, a left-aligned success message (e.g., 'Quote saved') is paired with a subtle success icon on its left or right, with the text and icon aligned on a single horizontal line. Below the main message, smaller secondary text may show the saved label and instructions to revisit. The overall height is slightly shorter than the form state, keeping the sidebar visually lighter once the task is complete. There is a 16–24px gap between this confirmation panel and any components above or below in the sidebar, maintaining the consistent vertical rhythm.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Occupies the same card location as the save-quote form, effectively toggling views. It remains static in the right sidebar stack and scrolls with the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The applicant-info-starter is the primary call-to-action button inside the top quote overview card in the right sidebar. Within that card, it is positioned toward the lower portion, directly beneath the budget indicator, separated by about 12–16px of vertical space. The button spans nearly the full width of the card (approximately 80–100% of the inner width) and is styled as a large pill-shaped red button with white text, consistent with the brand’s primary CTA. Its padding is generous—around 12–16px vertically and 24–32px horizontally—making it visually dominant and easy to tap. There is ample whitespace (16–24px) below the button before the bottom of the card so the button feels anchored, not cramped. This prominent placement ensures that as the user’s gaze moves from left to right across the plan summary, this CTA draws attention and clearly signals the next major step: starting the applicant information.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the top sidebar card; when activated, it triggers the appearance/expansion of the applicant-info-form in the left column but does not move position itself.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The applicant-info-form resides in the left column and is introduced lower in the page, after the user has had a chance to review plan details and adjust optional coverages. By default, the space for this form is either minimized to a concise teaser state or hidden. When the applicant-info-starter button is clicked, this form expands into view directly below the optional coverages list, separated from the last coverage item by about 32px of vertical space. It spans the full width of the left column and appears as a structured white card with rounded corners and a subtle shadow, visually similar to the plan summary card but slightly less tall at first. Inside, the form fields (e.g., name, contact details, address) are organized in a clean multi-row layout with 16–20px vertical spacing between field groups and standard labels above inputs. The card includes 20–24px padding on all sides and may optionally feature a small section header like 'Applicant Information' at the top. The bottom of the card includes inline navigation or a secondary CTA (e.g., 'Continue', 'Save and continue later') aligned to the right, with 16–24px padding before the card edge. The form’s position allows users to stay within the same page, scrolling naturally from options to personal details without losing context.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Initially collapsed or not rendered; when triggered, it expands in place in the left column under the optional coverages list. It scrolls with the page and can be collapsed or kept open depending on user interaction design.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The quote-plan navigation links form a slim horizontal navigation bar that sits immediately below the site’s main header/navigation and above the two-column content area. This bar spans the full width of the main content container and aligns with the left and right edges of the underlying grid. Within this bar, 3–5 text-based links (e.g., 'Boat details', 'Plan & options', 'Applicant info', 'Review & purchase') are arranged from left to right in a single line. The currently active step (this page) is emphasized using a red underline and/or bold text, while inactive steps use a neutral gray and a lighter weight. There is about 12–16px of vertical padding above and below the links, keeping the bar relatively slim but distinct. A subtle bottom border or faint shadow separates this navigation strip from the content below, reinforcing the F-shaped scan: users read across these links before dropping down into the main plan summary on the left and sidebar on the right.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static at the top of the content area; it scrolls off-screen with the page in a standard flow (not sticky by default), maintaining a simple and unobtrusive presence.

name: Account Dashboard
title: Account Dashboard
description: Authenticated account area where a customer can view existing policies and manage saved boat insurance quotes, including a demo login option for trying the dashboard.

core_features: Provide an in-page login form (including demo login support) that authenticates the user via the login interface and initializes access to the dashboard.
After successful authentication, retrieve and display an overview of the user’s policies and saved quotes using the getAccountDashboardSummary interface.
Show a structured list of existing policies (e.g., boat insurance policies) with status and key summary details, and allow navigation to the policy_detail.html page using a policyId URL parameter.
Show a structured list of saved quotes with their core attributes (quoteNumber, label, referenceNumber, timestamps, status) and allow resuming quotes via the resumeQuote interface.

Determine the correct quote step (boat_details, coverage_options, plans_list, plan_summary, or later steps) using resumeQuote and then navigate to the appropriate quote_*.html page with a quoteId URL parameter.

Handle empty states where no policies or no saved quotes are returned, and guide users to continue or start quotes from other parts of the site (via simple informational messages, not direct quote creation here).
Handle interface errors (login failure, dashboard summary load failure, quote resume failure) by displaying clear error messages without blocking unrelated parts of the page.
user_workflows:
Workflow: Demo or standard login and dashboard initialization
1) User lands on account_dashboard.html (typically via the header "My Policy" link or a direct link from a confirmation screen).
2) If the user is not already authenticated server-side, the page shows the login form with an optional demo login helper.
3) User enters a username and password OR chooses a demo login option that pre-populates the fields and submits the same login interface.
4) On form submit, the login interface is called with the entered username and password.
5) If login.success is false, the page displays login.message and keeps the login form visible.
6) If login.success is true, the page stores the returned userDisplayName in page state (not in URL or localStorage) and immediately calls getAccountDashboardSummary.
7) When getAccountDashboardSummary returns successfully, the page renders the dashboard sections (policies list and saved quotes list) and hides the login form for this session.

Workflow: Viewing an existing policy from the dashboard
1) After successful login and summary load, the policy list component renders each policy returned in getAccountDashboardSummary.policies.
2) User selects a policy row/card in the policy list.
3) The click handler reads the selected policy’s policyId from the bound data.
4) The page navigates to policy_detail.html using a URL such as policy_detail.html?policyId={policyId}, without invoking any additional interfaces from this page.
5) The policy_detail.html page (not defined here) is responsible for loading full policy details using its own interfaces.

Workflow: Resuming a saved quote from the dashboard
1) After successful login and summary load, the saved quotes list component renders each quote from getAccountDashboardSummary.savedQuotes.
2) User clicks on a saved quote row/card.
3) The click handler reads the quoteId for that item and calls resumeQuote with that quoteId.
4) When resumeQuote returns, the component checks resumeQuote.success.
5) If resumeQuote.success is false, the component shows resumeQuote.message in an error banner and does not navigate away.
6) If resumeQuote.success is true, the component reads resumeQuote.currentStep and determines the appropriate next page:
   - If currentStep is "boat_details", navigate to quote_boat_details.html?quoteId={quoteId}.
   - If currentStep is "coverage_options", navigate to quote_coverage_options.html?quoteId={quoteId}.
   - If currentStep is "plans_list", navigate to quote_plans.html?quoteId={quoteId}.
   - If currentStep is "plan_summary", navigate to quote_plan_summary.html?quoteId={quoteId}.
   - If currentStep is some other step (such as "applicant_info" or any additional step), the component may either stay on account_dashboard.html and show a message, or navigate to the closest supported step based on product rules; the behavior should be configurable without hardcoding specific non-supported steps here.
7) The target quote page (not defined here) uses the quoteId URL parameter to load the resumed quote data.

Workflow: Handling empty states and reloading dashboard data
1) After getAccountDashboardSummary returns, if the policies array is empty, the policy list component shows an informational message indicating that there are no policies associated with the account.
2) If the savedQuotes array is empty, the saved quotes list component shows an informational message indicating that there are no saved quotes to resume.
3) The page may display simple, non-promotional guidance that quotes can be started or continued from other quote-related pages outside this dashboard, without creating or modifying any quotes directly here.
4) A "Refresh" or similar action can be offered that re-calls getAccountDashboardSummary to update the lists, for example after activity on other pages in the same session.

Workflow: Accessing the dashboard via URL state
1) A user may arrive at account_dashboard.html with URL parameters that influence the initial view (for example, whether the quotes tab or policies tab is active initially, or whether a particular panel is expanded).
2) On page load, the state logic component parses allowed URL parameters (such as view, focusPolicyId, or focusQuoteId) and stores them in page state.
3) After login and summary load, the dashboard uses these state values to set which section is emphasized or scrolled into view.
4) If a focusPolicyId is provided and exists in the returned policies list, that policy is highlighted and scrolled into view.
5) If a focusQuoteId is provided and exists in the returned savedQuotes list, that quote is highlighted and scrolled into view.
interactions: Login form submission: User enters username and password and submits. The onSubmit handler invokes login(username, password). On success, it hides the login form and triggers getAccountDashboardSummary; on failure, it shows login.message.

Demo login toggle: User activates a demo login option (such as a button). This fills in demo credentials in the username and password fields (or uses predetermined values configured outside this specification) and triggers the same login submission flow using the login interface.

Dashboard initialization: After login success, the page automatically calls getAccountDashboardSummary once. If this call fails (for example, if an authorization error occurs), the page shows an error banner and can optionally show the login form again if appropriate.

Policy selection: User clicks on a policy row/card in the policy list. The onClick handler reads the bound policyId and navigates to policy_detail.html?policyId={policyId}. No additional interface call is made from this page for that action.

Saved quote selection: User clicks on a saved quote row/card in the saved quotes list. The onClick handler calls resumeQuote(quoteId). On success, it uses resumeQuote.currentStep to decide which quote_*.html page to navigate to, passing quoteId in the URL. On failure, it shows resumeQuote.message in an error area.

Tab or section switching: If the dashboard groups information into sections (e.g., Policies vs Saved Quotes), user clicks a tab or section header. The onClick handler updates the active section in page state and may also update the URL (e.g., view=policies or view=quotes) using history APIs without page reload.

Manual dashboard refresh: User clicks a refresh control in the dashboard area. The onClick handler re-invokes getAccountDashboardSummary and updates the policies and savedQuotes lists when the new data arrives.

Error dismissal: When any error banner or inline error is shown (for login, summary loading, or quote resume), user can click a close or dismiss control. The onClick handler clears the corresponding error message from page state so the banner disappears without affecting data.

Deep-link focusing: If the URL includes focusPolicyId or focusQuoteId and the corresponding item is present in the data, the dashboard automatically scrolls that item into view and highlights it. If the user clicks elsewhere or switches tabs, the highlight is cleared from state.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The page relies on URL parameters to govern view state and navigation, without using localStorage. All parameters are optional and read on each page load:

- view: Determines which dashboard section is active on initial load. Allowed values include 'policies' and 'quotes'. If view is not provided or unrecognized, a default section (such as 'policies') is active. When the user manually switches sections, the page may update the URL's view parameter using the history API without forcing a reload.

- focusPolicyId: If present, indicates a policy that should be highlighted and scrolled into view after getAccountDashboardSummary returns. The logic is:
  1) After summary load, search getAccountDashboardSummary.policies for a policy where policyId equals focusPolicyId.
  2) If found, mark that item as focused in page state and scroll it into view.
  3) If not found, ignore this parameter without error.

- focusQuoteId: If present, indicates a saved quote that should be highlighted and scrolled into view after summary load. The logic mirrors focusPolicyId using getAccountDashboardSummary.savedQuotes.

- authMode: Optional parameter to hint the login component to emphasize demo login (for example, authMode=demo). If authMode=demo, the login component can pre-select the demo login option or show explanatory text; actual credentials still come from user input or external configuration, and the page always uses the same login interface.

- Any navigation from this page uses URL parameters on target pages rather than storing state locally. For example:
  - When the user selects a policy, navigate to policy_detail.html?policyId={selectedPolicyId}.
  - When the user resumes a quote via resumeQuote, navigate to one of:
    * quote_boat_details.html?quoteId={selectedQuoteId}
    * quote_coverage_options.html?quoteId={selectedQuoteId}
    * quote_plans.html?quoteId={selectedQuoteId}
    * quote_plan_summary.html?quoteId={selectedQuoteId}
  based on resumeQuote.currentStep.

No user or session data is stored in localStorage. Authentication state and authorization for getAccountDashboardSummary and resumeQuote are handled by the backend session created via the login interface, and transient UI state (such as active tab, error messages, and focused items) lives only in in-memory page state and URL parameters.

components.functionality: Controls whether the user sees the login form or the account dashboard. It manages the overall flow: initial login via the login interface, then fetching and displaying summary data via getAccountDashboardSummary when authentication is successful.
data_binding: login (interface result: success, message, userDisplayName, hasActivePolicies)

components.functionality: Provides a username/password login form and supports a demo login option. On submit, it calls the login interface. If login is successful, it passes control to the auth-gate-container to load the dashboard; otherwise, it shows the error message returned.
data_binding: login (interface schema for success, message, userDisplayName, hasActivePolicies)
event_handlers: onSubmit: Collects username and password inputs and calls login(username, password).
onDemoLoginClick: Prepares demo credentials (configured outside this page) in the form fields and triggers onSubmit using the login interface.
onLoginResponse: Receives the login interface response, updates error state if success=false, or emits onLoginSuccess to the auth-gate-container if success=true.

components.functionality: Displays top-level account information once the user is authenticated, such as the user’s display name (from either login or getAccountDashboardSummary) and high-level counts of policies and saved quotes. It does not fetch data itself but reacts to summary load events.
event_handlers: onSummaryLoad: Updates displayed name and counts whenever getAccountDashboardSummary returns.
onRefreshClick: Emits an event to the dashboard-refresh-control (or auth-gate-container) to re-call getAccountDashboardSummary.

components.functionality: Renders each policy from getAccountDashboardSummary.policies as an interactive item with key summary fields (policyNumber, productType, status, policyTypeName, boatDescription, billingFrequency, nextPaymentDueDate). Supports focusing a specific policy from URL state and navigation into policy_detail.html.
event_handlers: onPolicyClick: Reads the clicked item’s policyId and navigates to policy_detail.html?policyId={policyId}.
onSummaryLoad: Populates the list with the latest policies from getAccountDashboardSummary.
onFocusFromUrl: After summary load, checks focusPolicyId; if it matches a policyId in the list, highlights and scrolls that item into view.

components.functionality: Displays the list of saved quotes returned in getAccountDashboardSummary.savedQuotes with their quoteNumber, label, referenceNumber, createdAt, lastUpdatedAt, and status. Handles resuming a quote using the resumeQuote interface and then navigating to the correct quote step page based on the returned currentStep.
data_binding: resumeQuote (interface result: success, quoteNumber, currentStep, message)
event_handlers: onQuoteClick: Takes the selected quote’s quoteId and calls resumeQuote(quoteId).

onResumeQuoteResponse: Uses resumeQuote.success to decide whether to show an error or navigate; if success=true, reads resumeQuote.currentStep and navigates to the appropriate quote_*.html page with quoteId in the URL.
onSummaryLoad: Populates the list with the latest savedQuotes from getAccountDashboardSummary.
onFocusFromUrl: After summary load, checks focusQuoteId; if it matches a quoteId in the list, highlights and scrolls that item into view.

components.functionality: Manages which main section of the dashboard is visible (for example, Policies vs Saved Quotes). It reads the initial view from the URL and updates both the UI and the URL when the user switches sections.
data_binding: getAccountDashboardSummary.policies (to determine if the Policies tab has data)
getAccountDashboardSummary.savedQuotes (to determine if the Saved Quotes tab has data)
event_handlers: onInit: Reads the view parameter from the URL and activates the corresponding section (defaulting to a chosen section if not present).
onTabClick: Updates internal activeSection state and updates the URL's view parameter using the history API.
onSummaryLoad: Enables or disables tabs appropriately (e.g., if there are no savedQuotes, the Saved Quotes tab may still be shown but can indicate that it is empty).

components.functionality: Provides a mechanism to manually refresh the dashboard data (policies and saved quotes) by re-calling getAccountDashboardSummary without requiring the user to log out and back in.
data_binding: getAccountDashboardSummary (for current data, loading state, and error state)
event_handlers: onClick: Triggers a new call to getAccountDashboardSummary and updates loading indicators while the request is in progress.
onSummaryLoadSuccess: Clears any reload-related errors and updates the timestamp or visual indicator that the dashboard is current.
onSummaryLoadFailure: Shows an appropriate error message or indicator while keeping the last successfully loaded data visible.

components.functionality: Displays error messages from the login, getAccountDashboardSummary, and resumeQuote interfaces in a centralized, dismissible area. It allows different error sources to be distinguished so that users understand which action failed.
data_binding: getAccountDashboardSummary (error state, if any, managed by the auth-gate-container or refresh control)
event_handlers: onShowLoginError: Triggered when the login interface returns success=false; updates the banner with the login error message.
onShowSummaryError: Triggered when getAccountDashboardSummary fails; updates the banner with a summary-related error message.
onShowResumeQuoteError: Triggered when resumeQuote returns success=false; updates the banner with the quote resume error message.
onDismiss: Clears the currently displayed error from the banner without altering underlying data or state.

layout.grid_system: Likely a 12-column fixed-width grid (approx. 1140–1200px) centered within the viewport, with the left 7–8 columns for main content and right 4–5 columns for the sidebar CTA/agent column.
layout.layout_pattern: Use the same two-column hierarchy as the marketing mock: a full-width top nav (outside this spec), then a centered 12-column content container. Within that, arrange content in a linear top-to-bottom flow: global feedback first (error banner), then a hero-like dashboard summary (dashboard-header on the left, sidebar actions on the right), followed by detail sections (policy list on the left, saved quotes on the right). The auth gate + login form replace the dashboard content when the user is not authenticated, appearing as a framed card in the right-side column to echo the original quote card pattern.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Use the same two-column hierarchy as the marketing mock: a full-width top nav (outside this spec), then a centered 12-column content container. Within that, arrange content in a linear top-to-bottom flow: global feedback first (error banner), then a hero-like dashboard summary (dashboard-header on the left, sidebar actions on the right), followed by detail sections (policy list on the left, saved quotes on the right). The auth gate + login form replace the dashboard content when the user is not authenticated, appearing as a framed card in the right-side column to echo the original quote card pattern.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Within the max-width content container (~1140–1200px), allocate ~60–65% of the width (7–8 grid columns) to the left main column and ~35–40% (4–5 columns) to the right sidebar. Vertically, the upper ~40% of the initial viewport is occupied by the hero area: dashboard-header + tabs on the left and the top of the sidebar (refresh control, or auth gate when logged out) on the right. Below this, the policy-list continues down the left, while the saved-quotes-list occupies the upper-to-middle portion of the right sidebar. Internal paddings and gaps follow an 8px-based scale: 20–24px padding inside cards, 24–32px vertical gaps between stacked sections, and 16–24px between tightly related controls.
layout_strategies.content_density: Maintain a medium-low density: generous whitespace around headings and lists, cards that breathe, and clear separation between stacked sections. Each list (policies, saved quotes) uses card rows with 16–24px internal padding and 16px gaps between items, avoiding clutter. The tabs and refresh control are compact but not cramped, with comfortable spacing between tab labels. The auth gate and login form use especially airy spacing to signal importance and reduce friction.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Preserve the F-shaped scanning pattern: users first read across the top (nav, then the error-banner if present), then the eye moves to the prominent dashboard-header in the upper-left. From there, attention is pulled horizontally to the right by the small but high-contrast dashboard-refresh-control or auth gate card. The user then tracks downward through the left column (tabs, then the policy-list), periodically glancing to the right where the saved-quotes-list sits as a secondary focus. When the auth gate is visible, it becomes the primary right-side focal card at the hero level, mirroring the original quote card CTA and strongly attracting attention from the headline area on the left.

layout.overall_description: Below the site’s full-width red-accented navigation, the account dashboard content sits in a centered, fixed-width container aligned to a 12-column grid. At the very top of this content area, a full-width error banner (when present) stretches across all columns, forming a slim band that immediately communicates issues before any dashboard information. Under this, the page resolves into a two-column layout: the left ~65% of the width is the main dashboard column, and the right ~35% is a sidebar. The upper half of the screen forms a hero-like zone where a large, confident dashboard header anchors the left side, and a compact, high-contrast control or card anchors the right. Just beneath the header in the left column, pill-like tabs allow switching between dashboard sections while a refresh control aligns toward the top-right, echoing the marketing layout’s cross-column tension. As you scan downward, the left column becomes a vertical stack of policy cards, each separated by ample white space, while the right column hosts a slightly more compact, card-framed list of saved quotes. The composition feels clean and corporate: large red accents for key labels and controls, gentle shadows on cards, and consistent 24–32px gaps between modules. In unauthenticated states, a prominent white auth gate card occupies the right column’s hero area with the login form inside, pulling attention from the left column’s contextual copy and mirroring the original quote-card-on-gray aesthetic. The whole page encourages an easy F-shaped scan: across the top banner and header, across to the right control card, then vertically down both columns as users review policies and saved quotes side by side.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The auth-gate-container appears directly under the error-banner and within the 12-column content area, occupying the right sidebar portion of the hero zone. On desktop, it is aligned with the top of the dashboard-header so that its top edge sits on the same horizontal line as the main dashboard headline. It spans roughly 4–5 grid columns (about 35–40% of the content width), hugging the right side of the page with a fixed card width inside that column. The container itself is a white, rounded-rectangle card set against the page’s subtle light-gray background, with 20–24px of internal padding on all sides and a very light shadow to echo the marketing quote card. There is a clear 24–32px vertical gap below this card before any additional right-column content (such as the saved-quotes-list) appears. To its left, across a 32px column gutter, the dashboard-header sits in the main column, visually pairing the left headline with the right auth card. On tablet and mobile breakpoints, the auth-gate-container drops out of the two-column grid and instead spans nearly 100% of the available width, centered, with 16–24px of margin on the sides and 24–32px of margin above and below. When the auth gate is active (user not authenticated), the dashboard-header and both lists are visually de-emphasized or hidden, making the auth-gate-container the dominant hero card in this area.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Flows with the page content but is conditional: when rendered, it occupies the top of the right column hero area and may replace or hide underlying dashboard sections for unauthenticated users. It is not sticky; it scrolls away with the page. On smaller viewports, it becomes a full-width stacked card above main dashboard content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The login-form is nested inside the auth-gate-container and never appears outside of it. It is vertically centered within the card’s interior padding, forming a vertical stack of fields and buttons that spans the full usable width of the auth-gate card minus the 20–24px side padding. The form elements are left-aligned, with labels and inputs of equal width, and a primary pill-shaped submit button stretching nearly the full width of the form content area. Vertical spacing between form fields is in 8px multiples (typically 12–16px between label and input, and 16–24px between stacked fields), keeping the layout open and legible. A secondary link or small text for demo login, if present, sits directly beneath the main button with a tighter 8–12px spacing, still comfortably separated.
