The form leaves a band of white space both above the first field and below the final button (around 16–24px each) to maintain the airy, corporate feel of the card.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Completely contained within and dependent on the auth-gate-container. It scrolls normally with the page and does not affect the overall column layout beyond the size of its parent card.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The dashboard-header is positioned under the error-banner at the top of the left main column, forming the visual hero headline of the authenticated dashboard. It spans approximately 7–8 grid columns (about 60–65% of the content width) from the left edge of the centered container, with its right edge roughly aligned to the gutter before the sidebar. The header uses a large, bold title line in red or near-black, with ample white space above (24–32px from the error-banner) and below (24–32px before the dashboard-tabs). If the auth-gate-container is visible, the top edge of the dashboard-header aligns horizontally with the top edge of the auth card on the right, creating a balanced two-column hero row similar to the headline / quote-card pairing in the marketing mockup. The header may include brief summary text or policy counts beneath the main title, but all within this left column width, maintaining clear left alignment. The overall block reads as a clear starting point for the dashboard, with no other element competing at this vertical level in the left column.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, in-flow element at the top of the main content column. It does not stick to the viewport and scrolls away as the user moves down the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The policy-list sits directly below the dashboard-tabs in the left main column, maintaining the same 7–8-column width as the dashboard-header above it. There is a 16–24px vertical gap between the bottom of the tabs and the top of the first policy item. The list is composed of vertically stacked cards or rows, each spanning nearly the entire width of the left column with 16–20px of internal padding and slight rounded corners, mirroring the lightly elevated card treatments from the mockup. Items are separated by 12–16px of vertical spacing, giving the list a medium-low density that is easy to scan. As the user scrolls, this column can extend beyond the initial viewport, while the right sidebar with saved quotes remains visible for a bit longer, reinforcing the two-column structure. At the bottom of the visible policies, there is a 32–48px buffer before any footer content or the end of the page, maintaining generous breathing room. On smaller screens, the policy-list becomes a full-width stack below the dashboard-header and tabs, with spacing preserved but margins tightened to 16px on the sides.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard scrolling list within the main content flow. It does not overlap other components and adapts in height based on the number of policies. On mobile, it reflows into a single-column layout below the header and tabs.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The saved-quotes-list occupies the right sidebar column beneath the dashboard-refresh-control (or beneath the auth-gate-container if both are present in a given configuration, though typically the auth gate replaces the sidebar content when visible). On desktop, it spans 4–5 grid columns, aligned to the right edge of the content container. The top of the saved-quotes-list sits about 24–32px below the refresh control, or 24–32px below the auth-gate-container if that card is not present. It is presented as a self-contained white card or stack of smaller cards with 16–20px internal padding and 12–16px vertical gaps between each quote row, a bit more compact than the policy-list to reflect its secondary emphasis. The card shares the same rounded corners and light shadow as the policy cards to maintain visual consistency, but occupies less width, giving it the feel of a right-hand supporting panel. The list height is allowed to grow, but generally remains shorter than the policy-list in the left column, so scanning down the page the eye reaches the end of the sidebar sooner, reinforcing the primary importance of the main policies column. On tablet and mobile, the saved-quotes-list reflows below the policy-list as a full-width section with a 32px vertical gap separating it from the main list.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Flows as part of the right sidebar column and scrolls with the page. Its presence and height do not affect the layout of the left column except through shared vertical alignment at the top of the hero zone. On smaller screens, it becomes a stacked section below the main content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The dashboard-tabs sit immediately below the dashboard-header in the left column, with a 24–32px vertical gap separating them from the bottom of the header content. They occupy roughly 70–80% of the left column’s width, starting from the left edge, and consist of a horizontal row of pill-shaped tab labels. Each tab is sized according to its text but uses consistent horizontal padding (12–16px on each side) and 8–12px vertical padding, in line with the large pill CTA style from the marketing mockup. The tabs are left-aligned, leaving an open space to their right within the same row that visually balances with the dashboard-refresh-control anchored toward the overall top-right. There is a slim 2–4px underline or filled pill for the active tab in the brand red, with inactive tabs in a light gray outline. A 16–24px vertical gap separates the bottom of the tabs from the start of the policy-list, making it clear that the tabs control which dashboard section or view is in focus. On narrower screens, the tabs may wrap into two lines but still maintain left alignment and 8–12px vertical spacing from the header above and the list below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, in-flow control that sits between the header and the main list in the left column. It may influence which data is shown in the policy-list (or other content) but does not change its own position; it is not sticky and scrolls away with the rest of the hero area.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The dashboard-refresh-control is a compact, high-visibility control located in the upper-right area of the content container, echoing the strong right-side CTA from the marketing mockup. On desktop, it aligns horizontally with the dashboard-tabs: while the tabs are anchored to the left side of the main column, the refresh control is positioned to the far right, within or slightly overlapping the right sidebar column, so that its vertical centerline roughly lines up with the top of the saved-quotes-list. It may appear as a small pill-shaped button or icon-with-label with about 8–10px vertical and 12–16px horizontal padding, using a subtle border or red accent to make it noticeable but not overpowering. There is minimal vertical spacing (8–12px) between this control and the top of the saved-quotes-list beneath it, maintaining their perceived connection as a sidebar "utility" stack. The placement helps complete the F-pattern: from the dashboard-header across to this upper-right control, then down to the saved quotes. On tablet and mobile, the refresh control moves to align right within the main column above the policy-list, sitting just below the tabs with a 8–16px gap.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: In-flow but visually aligned to the top-right of the content area. It is not fixed or sticky; as the user scrolls past the hero zone, it scrolls away. On smaller screens, it reflows to sit within the single-column stack, typically right-aligned above the main list.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The error-banner, when present, is the first element inside the main content area directly beneath the site’s top navigation. It spans the full width of the centered content container, covering all 12 grid columns. The banner is a horizontal strip with a height driven by its content, typically 40–64px, and uses the brand red as its background color with white or off-white text for maximum contrast. It has 12–16px of vertical padding and 16–24px of horizontal padding inside, giving the message room to breathe while keeping the profile relatively slim to avoid overwhelming the page. The bottom of the error-banner sits 16–24px above the dashboard-header and auth-gate-container row, clearly separating status messaging from functional dashboard content while still keeping it within immediate view. If multiple errors or warnings are shown, they are stacked within the same band with 8–12px spacing, not as multiple separate bars, to preserve the clean corporate aesthetic. On mobile, the banner remains full-width, touching the left and right edges of the viewport with consistent padding.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: In-flow, always at the top of the dashboard content when active. It is non-sticky and scrolls up and out of view as the user moves down the page; its presence simply pushes the rest of the layout downward by its height.

name: Policy Detail
title: Policy Detail
description: Page to view a specific boat insurance policy, manage its mailing address, and access policy documents including the digital ID card, based on a selected policyId.

core_features: Load and display detailed policy information using getPolicyDetail with the policyId URL parameter.
Show an overview section with key policy attributes such as policy number, status, dates, coverage limits, usage, billing frequency, primary ZIP, and boat description using getPolicyDetail.
Display a "Contact Details" / "Mailing Address" section that shows the current mailing address from getPolicyDetail.
Allow the user to edit the mailing address fields (line 1, line 2, city, state, ZIP) and submit changes using updatePolicyMailingAddress.
After a successful address update, refresh the address data on the page using the response from updatePolicyMailingAddress.
Provide a "Documents" / "ID Cards" section that lists available policy documents using getPolicyDocuments.
Allow filtering or grouping of documents by type (such as ID card, declarations page, billing statement) by passing an optional type parameter to getPolicyDocuments.
Enable inline viewing of the current digital ID card within the page using getPolicyIdCard, without requiring a download or print action.
Allow navigation back to the account dashboard via a breadcrumb or "Back to My Policies" link that routes to account_dashboard.html without altering the current policy data.
user_workflows:
Workflow: Load policy details
1. User navigates to policy_detail.html with a policyId query parameter in the URL.
2. On page initialization, the page reads the policyId parameter from the URL.
3. The page calls getPolicyDetail with the policyId.
4. When getPolicyDetail returns successfully, the page populates the policy overview section, determines which sections are available from availableSections, and renders section navigation accordingly.
5. If getPolicyDetail fails or returns an error, the page shows an error state and does not call other interfaces until a valid policyId is provided (e.g., via URL correction or navigation back).

Workflow: Switch between sections (Overview, Contact Details, Documents)
1. After policy details are loaded, the page checks a section query parameter (for example, section=overview/contact_details/documents). If absent, a default section (such as overview) is assumed.
2. When the user clicks a section tab or navigation link, the page updates the section parameter in the URL using client-side navigation without a full page reload.
3. The page reads the updated section parameter and conditionally renders the corresponding section component (overview, mailing address, or documents).
4. For sections that require additional data (e.g., documents, ID card), the page triggers the appropriate interface calls (getPolicyDocuments or getPolicyIdCard) once the section is active.

Workflow: View and edit mailing address
1. User navigates to the Contact Details / Mailing Address section, either by default or by clicking the appropriate tab; the URL section parameter is set to contact_details.
2. The page uses the mailingAddress object returned by getPolicyDetail to display the current address and the lastUpdatedAt timestamp (if present).
3. The user clicks an Edit button; the page switches from read-only display to an editable form populated with the current mailing address fields.
4. As the user edits the address fields, the component updates its local form state (not stored in URL or localStorage) and performs any inline validation rules as needed.
5. When the user submits the form, the page calls updatePolicyMailingAddress with policyId and the current form values for mailingAddressLine1, mailingAddressLine2, mailingCity, mailingState, and mailingZip.
6. While waiting for the response, the page shows a submitting state on the form (e.g., disables the submit button).
7. If updatePolicyMailingAddress returns success=true, the page updates the displayed mailing address using the mailingAddress object from the response and shows a success notification. The form returns to read-only mode.
8. If updatePolicyMailingAddress returns success=false or if the call fails, the page shows an error notification, keeps the form values so the user does not lose changes, and allows the user to correct and resubmit or cancel edits.

Workflow: Cancel mailing address edits
1. While in edit mode on the mailing address form, the user clicks a Cancel or Discard button.
2. The component discards any unsaved form changes and reverts the address fields to the last successfully loaded values from getPolicyDetail or the latest successful updatePolicyMailingAddress response.
3. The component returns to read-only display mode without calling any interfaces.
4. The URL section parameter remains contact_details so the user remains in the same section.

Workflow: View policy documents list
1. The user navigates to the Documents / ID Cards section; the URL section parameter is set to documents.
2. On first entry into the documents section, the page calls getPolicyDocuments with policyId and without a type parameter to retrieve all associated documents.
3. Once documents are loaded, the page displays a list showing each document's title, description (if provided), type, createdAt, documentFormat, and whether it isCurrent.
4. If the user applies a type filter (for example, selects only ID card documents), the page calls getPolicyDocuments again with the policyId and the selected type value.
5. When the list updates, the page maintains the user's current filter selection in the URL via an additional query parameter (for example, docType=selectedValue) so that reloading or sharing the URL preserves the same filtered view.
6. If getPolicyDocuments fails, the page shows an error message within the documents section and allows the user to retry the request.

Workflow: View digital ID card inline
1. While in the Documents / ID Cards section, the user either selects an "ID Card" option in a document type filter or clicks a "View ID Card" control associated with the ID card document.
2. The page updates the URL to include a parameter indicating that the ID card is being viewed (for example, idCardView=true) while keeping the same policyId and section parameters.
3. The page calls getPolicyIdCard with the current policyId.
4. If getPolicyIdCard returns a document with documentFormat=html and htmlContent is provided, the page renders the htmlContent directly inside the ID card viewer component.
5. If getPolicyIdCard returns a document with documentFormat not equal to html, the page uses viewUrl in an embedded viewer component appropriate for the format (e.g., an inline frame or browser-native viewer), keeping the user on the same page.
6. If getPolicyIdCard fails or does not return a current ID card, the page shows an inline error message in place of the viewer content and allows the user to close the viewer.
7. When the user closes or hides the ID card viewer, the page removes or updates the corresponding URL parameter (for example, idCardView=false) so a refreshed URL reflects the current viewing state.

Workflow: Navigate back to My Policies
1. The page displays a breadcrumb or a "Back to My Policies" link once getPolicyDetail has successfully loaded, so that the link label can incorporate policy context if needed.
2. When the user clicks this link, the browser navigates to account_dashboard.html without appending additional parameters specific to this page.
3. No additional interfaces are called during this navigation from policy_detail.html; state related to policyId and section is discarded when leaving the page.

Workflow: Handle missing or invalid policyId in URL
1. If the page loads without a policyId query parameter, it does not call getPolicyDetail and instead shows a message indicating that a policy must be selected, along with a link or button to navigate to account_dashboard.html.
2. If getPolicyDetail is called with a policyId and the response indicates an error or cannot be retrieved, the page displays an error state and provides a navigation link back to account_dashboard.html.
3. While in an error state, the page does not call getPolicyDocuments or getPolicyIdCard until a valid policyId is available (e.g., after navigation and re-entry).
interactions: On initial page load: read policyId and optional section, docType, and idCardView parameters from the URL query string; if policyId is present, call getPolicyDetail with policyId and render the page based on the response.

When the user clicks on a section tab (Overview, Contact Details, Documents): update the section parameter in the URL, update the active section state, and conditionally render the associated section without reloading the entire page.
When the user opens the Contact Details section: display the mailingAddress fields and lastUpdatedAt value from getPolicyDetail; show an Edit button to enter edit mode.

When the user clicks the Edit button in the mailing address area: switch the component into edit mode, pre-fill form fields with existing address data, enable input fields, and display Save and Cancel controls.
On mailing address form input change: update local form state for the corresponding field and run any synchronous client validation (such as required fields) before allowing submission.

On mailing address form submit: prevent default form submission behavior, call updatePolicyMailingAddress with policyId and form values, display a loading or disabled state on the submit control, and wait for the response.

On successful updatePolicyMailingAddress response: update the displayed mailingAddress with values from the response, update any displayed lastUpdatedAt timestamp, switch the form back to read-only mode, and trigger a success notification component.

On failed updatePolicyMailingAddress response or network error: keep the user in edit mode with their current form entries, display an error notification indicating the failure, and allow the user to retry or cancel.

When the user clicks Cancel in the mailing address form: discard unsaved edits by restoring the last known mailingAddress data from getPolicyDetail or the latest updatePolicyMailingAddress response, and return the component to read-only mode.
When the user navigates to or refreshes the Documents section: call getPolicyDocuments with policyId and any docType filter from the URL; populate the documents list when the response arrives.

When the user changes the document type filter (for example, to view only ID card documents): update the docType parameter in the URL and call getPolicyDocuments with policyId and the selected type; replace the documents list with the new results.

When the user clicks a "View" or "View ID Card" control for an ID card document: update the idCardView parameter in the URL to indicate the ID card viewer is active and call getPolicyIdCard with the current policyId.

On successful getPolicyIdCard response: depending on documentFormat and htmlContent, render the ID card inline either by injecting htmlContent into the viewer component or by loading viewUrl into an embedded viewer; allow the user to close the viewer.
On failed getPolicyIdCard response or error: display an inline error message in the ID card viewer area and provide a control to close the viewer, which will also update the idCardView URL parameter.

When the user clicks the breadcrumb or "Back to My Policies" link: navigate the browser to account_dashboard.html; no additional data from this page is persisted via URL parameters beyond the target page requirements.

On any interface call error (getPolicyDetail, updatePolicyMailingAddress, getPolicyDocuments, getPolicyIdCard): trigger the global notification / error component to show an appropriate message and, when relevant, provide retry or navigation options.
page_functionality.state_logic: The primary page state is driven by URL query parameters and interface responses. The policyId URL parameter determines which policy to load; without it, the page does not call getPolicyDetail and instead prompts navigation back to the dashboard. On load, the page reads policyId and calls getPolicyDetail. The availableSections field from getPolicyDetail controls which sections (such as overview, contact_details, documents) are rendered in the section navigation. A section URL parameter determines which section component is currently active. If section is missing or not recognized, a default (for example, overview if provided in availableSections) is selected automatically but not hardcoded in data. When the section parameter is contact_details, the mailing address components are shown, and their content is bound to the mailingAddress object from getPolicyDetail or the latest successful updatePolicyMailingAddress response. When the section parameter is documents, the documents list is shown. An optional docType URL parameter narrows the getPolicyDocuments call to a specific document type; changing this parameter triggers a new getPolicyDocuments call with policyId and type. For ID card viewing state, an idCardView URL parameter is used: when it indicates that the ID card viewer is open, getPolicyIdCard is called with policyId and its response drives the ID card viewer component. Closing the viewer updates idCardView accordingly. Throughout, in-memory page state is used only for transient UI concerns (such as form field values, loading flags, and error flags), while durable navigation state and section selection are encoded in URL parameters. No localStorage or other persistent client storage is used. All dynamic content is sourced from the four assigned interfaces, and no static policy, document, or address values are hardcoded in the page logic.

components.functionality: Provides navigation back to the account dashboard and optionally displays the current policy context in a breadcrumb trail. The main action navigates to account_dashboard.html when activated.

Displays core policy information such as policy number, status, effective and expiration dates, liability limit, deductible, usage, billing frequency, primary ZIP, boat description, and any other high-level details returned by getPolicyDetail. It only renders after getPolicyDetail succeeds.

Renders navigation tabs or links for sections (for example, overview, contact_details, documents) based on availableSections returned by getPolicyDetail. Updates the section URL parameter and triggers conditional rendering of section-specific components.

Shows the current mailing address and lastUpdatedAt timestamp in read-only mode within the Contact Details section, using data from the mailingAddress object provided by getPolicyDetail or the latest updatePolicyMailingAddress response. Exposes an Edit action that switches to the editable form.

Provides inputs for mailing address line1, line2, city, state, and ZIP. Initializes values from the current mailingAddress data. On submission, calls updatePolicyMailingAddress with policyId and the form field values, handles loading and error states, and updates the display with the response data on success.

Fetches and displays a list of documents for the policy using getPolicyDocuments with policyId (and optional type from the docType URL parameter). Renders document attributes such as title, type, description, documentFormat, createdAt, and isCurrent. Supports user-initiated filtering by type, which updates the docType parameter and refetches the list.

Provides controls to filter the documents list by type. When the user selects a type, updates the docType URL parameter and triggers the documents-list component to call getPolicyDocuments with the policyId and selected type.

Displays the current digital ID card for the policy inside the page. When activated (based on the idCardView URL parameter or user click), calls getPolicyIdCard with policyId. If htmlContent is provided with documentFormat indicating HTML, injects that content directly; otherwise loads viewUrl into an embedded viewer compatible with the returned documentFormat. Provides a close control that hides the viewer and updates the idCardView URL parameter.

Shows success, warning, and error messages resulting from interface calls, such as successful or failed address updates, document load failures, or ID card retrieval issues. Messages are triggered by the outcomes of getPolicyDetail, updatePolicyMailingAddress, getPolicyDocuments, and getPolicyIdCard.

Controls global and section-level loading indicators and error views for all interface calls. For example, shows a page-level loader while getPolicyDetail is pending, and section-level loaders for getPolicyDocuments and getPolicyIdCard. When errors occur, it coordinates with the global-notifications component to display messages and with section components to present retry options.

layout.grid_system: Likely a 12-column fixed-width grid (approx. 1140–1200px) centered within the viewport, with the left 7–8 columns for main content and right 4–5 columns for the sidebar CTA/agent column.
layout.layout_pattern: The page uses a two-column layout beneath the global site header, matching the mockup. A full-width breadcrumb and notification area spans the top of the content. Below that, the content splits into a left main column and a right sidebar. The left column carries the core policy content in a linear top-to-bottom flow: policy overview at the top, then section tabs, then tab-specific content such as mailing address, documents, or ID card. The right column follows the mockup’s pattern with a vertical stack of utility/marketing-style cards (e.g., payment and claim CTAs, agent info) that are not modeled as separate components but visually echo the quote/CTA sidebar from the reference design.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page uses a two-column layout beneath the global site header, matching the mockup. A full-width breadcrumb and notification area spans the top of the content. Below that, the content splits into a left main column and a right sidebar. The left column carries the core policy content in a linear top-to-bottom flow: policy overview at the top, then section tabs, then tab-specific content such as mailing address, documents, or ID card. The right column follows the mockup’s pattern with a vertical stack of utility/marketing-style cards (e.g., payment and claim CTAs, agent info) that are not modeled as separate components but visually echo the quote/CTA sidebar from the reference design.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The main content grid is centered and fixed-width (~1140–1200px) using a 12-column system. The left column takes roughly 7–8 columns (about 60–65% of the width) and the right sidebar takes 4–5 columns (about 35–40%), separated by a consistent gutter in the 24–32px range. Vertically, the upper 35–40% of the viewport is dominated by the hero band consisting of the policy overview on the left and the first sidebar card on the right. Below that, the left column grows with the tabbed content (address, documents, ID card), while the right sidebar continues as a shorter vertical stack that often fits above the fold on common laptop screens.
layout_strategies.content_density: The layout follows a medium–low density similar to the mockup: generous white space around cards and between sections, but with enough text and actionable items visible to feel functional. Each card (policy overview, address, documents list, ID card) uses 20–24px internal padding and is separated from neighboring blocks by about 24–32px of vertical space. Controls like tabs and filter bars are compact but not cramped, aligning with the 8px spacing system so nothing feels crowded.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Visual flow mirrors the F-shaped pattern: users scan the top horizontal region (site header, then breadcrumb and any notifications), drop down to the bold policy overview in the upper-left, and from there their attention is pulled horizontally toward the top of the right sidebar where primary CTAs live. As they scroll, the eye continues vertically down the left column through the tabs and their active content (address, documents, or ID card), while the right column provides secondary action options and reassurance. Within each left-column section, headings and red pill-shaped CTAs stand out clearly, guiding users from summary (policy overview) to detail (documents list or mailing address form) to key actions (downloading the ID card, updating address).

layout.overall_description: The Policy Detail page sits within a centered, fixed-width content frame on a light gray background, echoing a modern, corporate marketing style. At the very top of this frame, a subtle breadcrumb row and any global notification banners span the full width, pushing the main content downward when present. Directly beneath, the page resolves into a two-column grid: on the left, a prominent white policy overview card with a bold red policy title anchors the hero area; on the right, a vertical stack of clean, white sidebar cards presents high-value actions like payment or claims, visually similar to the quote card and CTAs in the reference mockup. Below the overview in the left column, a horizontal tab bar allows switching between sections such as Mailing Address, Documents, and ID Card, with the active tab’s content rendered immediately underneath in a series of well-spaced cards and lists. The right sidebar maintains its stack of compact utility cards as the user scrolls, while the left column becomes the main scrolling canvas for the address display/form, documents filters and list, and the inline ID card viewer. The overall effect is a trustworthy, spacious, and functional layout where policy information and actions are clearly separated but visually tied together by consistent spacing, card styling, and strong red CTAs that stand out against a calm white and light-gray foundation.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The breadcrumb navigation is placed at the very top of the main content region, directly beneath the global site header and spanning the full 12-column content width. It is left-aligned within this frame, occupying only the upper-left portion visually but running on a single line where possible. There is a modest top margin (about 24px from the bottom of the site header) and about 8–12px of space below it before any notification banners, keeping it visually light and unobtrusive while clearly anchoring the user’s location on the site.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Sits in normal document flow; when present, it gently pushes all other content downward but does not stick or float.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Global notifications appear immediately below the breadcrumb row, spanning the same centered 12-column grid from left to right. Each notification is rendered as a full-width bar or card with 16–20px of vertical padding and 20–24px horizontal padding, separated from the breadcrumb above and the hero content below by around 8–16px of space. Multiple notifications stack vertically with 8px gaps, forming a short band that occupies the full width above the two-column hero area and temporarily becomes the first element users see beneath the navigation.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Appears in normal flow but effectively behaves like a temporary banner band; it pushes the hero content down while active and disappears without leaving extra gaps when dismissed.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The policy overview panel serves as the left-side hero card of the two-column layout. It sits directly beneath the notifications (or breadcrumb if no notifications), aligned to the left column and spanning roughly 7–8 of the 12 columns (about 60–65% of the content width). The card uses a white background with a very subtle shadow and 20–24px internal padding, and it is separated from the elements above by approximately 24–32px of vertical space. Within the viewport, the panel visually occupies the upper-left quadrant, roughly the top third to half of the left column, with a bold red policy title at the top, key details arranged in one or two rows beneath, and a row of pill-shaped primary actions along the bottom edge aligned to the right of the card. The top of the right sidebar’s first card aligns horizontally with the top of this panel, reinforcing the hero band across the page.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Normal flow within the left column; responsive in width with the column, and on narrower screens may expand to full width above any sidebar content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The section tabs are placed directly below the policy overview panel, occupying the full width of the left column. There is a clear gap of around 24px between the bottom of the overview card and the top of the tab bar, ensuring visual separation while keeping them clearly related. The tabs are arranged horizontally in a single line, with each tab label centered vertically within a strip that’s about 40–48px tall, and the active tab highlighted with a strong red underline or pill highlight that echoes the brand CTAs. The tab row itself aligns with the left column’s content edge, with its right edge stopping at the column boundary, leaving the right sidebar clearly distinct to the side.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Lives in normal document flow just under the hero, but may optionally become sticky at the top of the viewport within the left column when the user scrolls past it, so the section switcher remains accessible while content beneath scrolls.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The mailing address view appears in the left column directly beneath the section tabs when the Mailing Address tab is active. It is rendered as a white card matching the style of the policy overview but slightly more compact, spanning the full width of the left column with 20–24px of internal padding. There is roughly 24px of space separating it from the tabs above and any subsequent content below. Inside the card, the address label and status sit in the top-left, while an ‘Edit’ pill-shaped button is aligned to the top-right, creating a clear, functional header row. The address lines are stacked in the lower portion of the card with 8–12px vertical spacing, giving the entire block a clean, medium-low density that fits comfortably in the upper half of the viewport when no other sections are expanded.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Appears only when the Mailing Address tab is selected, occupying the tab-content slot in normal flow; it is fully replaced by the mailing-address-form or other tab content when necessary.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The mailing address form occupies the exact spatial footprint of the mailing-address-view card in the left column, replacing it inline when the user chooses to edit the address. It spans the full width of the left column, with the same white card styling and 20–24px internal padding, but is taller to accommodate form fields. The form fields are arranged in one or two columns depending on available width, with horizontal spacing following the 8px grid and vertical gaps of 12–16px between field groups. A clear button row is anchored at the bottom-right of the card with a prominent red pill-shaped Save/Update button and a secondary Cancel button to its left, while 24px of space separates this form card from any content above or below. The form remains comfortably within the main scroll region, ensuring that on most laptop screens the full header and the upper part of the form fields are visible without scrolling.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Replaces the mailing-address-view in normal flow when in edit mode, expanding vertically as needed and pushing any content below it downward; it is not sticky or overlayed but may be accompanied by a subtle page-level scroll-to-top-on-edit behavior for usability.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: When the Documents tab is active, the documents filter controls appear directly below the section tabs, occupying the full width of the left column. They are arranged in a low-profile, horizontally oriented control bar that is either a subtle raised strip or a compact white card with about 12–16px of vertical padding and 16–20px of horizontal padding. The primary filter elements (such as a document type dropdown and a date range selector) are left-aligned, while a search field or secondary filter may be aligned to the right edge, all within the same row. There is a modest 16–24px gap between the bottom of this control bar and the top of the documents list, visually linking the two while preserving breathing room.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Lives in normal flow beneath the tabs only when the Documents tab is selected; it scrolls with the content, though on smaller screens it may wrap into multiple rows without breaking the overall layout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The documents list sits immediately below the documents filter controls in the left column, aligned to the same content edges and spanning the full width of the column. It is arranged as a vertical stack of document rows or compact cards, each with 16–20px of vertical padding and 12–16px gaps between rows, following the medium–low density of the overall design. The list header (e.g., column titles) occupies the top of the card region if present, with the list itself extending down, often beyond the first viewport height to allow scrolling. A 24–32px vertical margin separates the bottom of the list from any subsequent sections within the left column, maintaining the clear, card-based rhythm of the page.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Appears in normal flow only when the Documents tab is active; as the list grows, it becomes the primary scrollable content in the left column, while the right sidebar scrolls in parallel with the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The ID card viewer is rendered in the left column as the primary content block directly beneath the section tabs when the ID Card tab is active. It occupies the full width of the left column and is styled as a prominent white card that visually mimics the proportions of a physical ID card: a wide, horizontally oriented rectangle centered within the card container, surrounded by 20–24px of padding. Key ID information (policyholder name, vessel details, policy number) is arranged in neat rows or columns, with clear labels and strong alignment. Along the bottom-right edge of the card, one or two high-contrast red pill-shaped buttons (e.g., Download ID Card, Add to Wallet) are placed, creating a focal CTA area similar in feel to the quote button in the reference mockup. There is around 24–32px of space above and below the card to separate it from the tabs and any other content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Appears in the main tab-content slot in normal flow when the ID Card tab is selected; on smaller screens it can scale down proportionally while remaining full-width within the available content area.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The loading and error state handler sits conceptually over the main content region, primarily governing the left column from just below the breadcrumb/notifications down through the tab-based content area. When data is loading, it replaces key blocks (such as the policy overview panel and the active tab content) with gray skeleton cards that match their approximate size and position, preserving the overall layout and spacing so the user can anticipate the final structure. When an error occurs, it injects a prominent full-width error card within the left column, typically just below the breadcrumb and notifications and above the policy overview, with 16–24px padding and about 16–24px of surrounding space, so the error message is immediately visible without completely displacing the layout. The right sidebar and overall two-column structure remain intact visually, reinforcing that the issue is with content loading rather than the entire page.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Mostly overlay-like within the left column: it controls whether underlying components are visible or replaced by skeletons, and can insert inline error cards into normal flow. It does not fix itself to the viewport but ensures that loading/error presentations occupy the same spatial slots as the content they represent.

name: Claim Start
title: Start a Claim
description: Page where users select the claim type, identify their policy by policy number, and initiate a new boat insurance claim before continuing to the multi-step claim form.

core_features: Fetch and display available claim types and guidance text using getClaimStartOptions.
Allow the user to select a claim type from the list returned by getClaimStartOptions, with specific support for starting a boat_insurance claim.
Accept a policy number associated with the claim, prefilled from the optional policyNumber URL parameter when present.
Validate presence of a policy number before starting a boat_insurance claim.
Initiate a new boat insurance claim by calling startBoatClaim with the entered policy number when the user chooses the boat_insurance claim type.
Handle success and failure responses from startBoatClaim, including displaying messages and extracting claimId and policySummary when available.
Redirect the user to claim_form.html with claimId as a URL parameter upon successful creation of a boat_insurance claim.
Provide basic claim guidance text using instructions from getClaimStartOptions and contextual messages based on user actions.
Provide navigation back to the site home page (index.html) via a Back to Home link.
user_workflows:
Workflow A – Start a boat claim from navigation (no prefilled policy number):
1. User navigates to claim_start.html (e.g., via header "Claims" or footer "File a Claim").
2. On page load, the page controller reads the URL for a policyNumber parameter (none is found) and calls getClaimStartOptions.
3. The list of claim types from getClaimStartOptions.claimTypes is displayed. The user selects the option whose claimTypeCode is boat_insurance.
4. The policy number input is shown (or remains visible) and is empty since no URL parameter was provided.
5. The user enters their policy number into the input field.
6. The user submits the form (e.g., clicks a Start Claim/Continue button).
7. Client-side validation ensures a non-empty policy number is present; if missing, an inline validation error is displayed and the API is not called.
8. If validation passes and the selected claimTypeCode is boat_insurance, the page calls startBoatClaim with the entered policyNumber.
9. If startBoatClaim returns success=false, the error message is displayed to the user and the user can correct the policy number and resubmit.
10. If startBoatClaim returns success=true, the page reads claimId from the response and navigates to claim_form.html?claimId={claimId}.
11. The policySummary returned in the response is used only for contextual display (e.g., verifying the policy selected) on this page before navigation completes, if desired by the design.

Workflow B – Start a boat claim from policy detail (prefilled policy number):
1. User starts from policy_detail.html and clicks an action that links to claim_start.html with a policyNumber URL parameter corresponding to the current policy.
2. The browser loads claim_start.html?policyNumber={policyNumberFromSource}.
3. On page load, the page controller parses the policyNumber from the URL and pre-populates the policy number input field with this value.
4. The controller calls getClaimStartOptions and renders the returned claimTypes and instructions.
5. The user selects the claim type corresponding to boat_insurance in the list.
6. Since the policy number is already filled in, the user can immediately submit the form.
7. The same validation and startBoatClaim call as in Workflow A occurs. If the user edits the policy number before submitting, the edited value is sent in the request.
8. On success, the page redirects to claim_form.html?claimId={claimId}.
9. If the prefilled policy number is invalid or associated with a non-boat policy, startBoatClaim will respond with success=false and a message; the error is shown and the user may correct the policy number or abandon the process.

Workflow C – User selects a non-boat claim type:
1. User lands on claim_start.html and getClaimStartOptions populates the list of all available claimTypes.
2. User selects a claim type whose claimTypeCode is not boat_insurance.
3. The Start Claim/Continue button for the online flow is disabled or shows a message indicating that only boat_insurance claims can be started online from this page.
4. The user may switch back to selecting the boat_insurance option to proceed with entering a policy number and calling startBoatClaim.
5. Any additional instructions about how to handle other claim types are presented using appropriate static page content or help text (not sourced from any data model), without calling other APIs.

Workflow D – Error handling and retry:
1. User selects boat_insurance and enters a policy number.
2. User submits the form and the page calls startBoatClaim.
3. startBoatClaim returns success=false and a message describing the issue (e.g., validation error, policy not found, or other restriction).
4. The page displays the message from the API in an error banner or inline near the form, without altering it.
5. The user adjusts the policy number or, if instructed by the message, stops using the online flow.
6. The user can resubmit, triggering another startBoatClaim call, until success=true or until they leave the page.
interactions: Page load: On initial load of claim_start.html, the page controller parses the policyNumber URL parameter if present and stores it in page state. It then calls getClaimStartOptions once to populate available claim types and instructions.

Claim type selection: When the user selects a claim type (e.g., via radio buttons or a similar selector), the selected claimTypeCode is stored in page state. The boat_insurance selection enables the policy number input’s validation and the Start Claim/Continue action for the online claim workflow.

Policy number input: The user can type or edit the policy number. Every change updates the current policyNumber value in page state. If a policyNumber was provided via URL, it is used as the initial value but can be overwritten by the user.

Form submission for boat_insurance: When the user submits the form with the boat_insurance claim type selected, the component validates that the policyNumber field is non-empty (and optionally conforms to any configured format rules). If validation fails, the action is blocked and inline validation messages are shown. If validation passes, startBoatClaim is called with the current policyNumber.

Handling startBoatClaim responses: After calling startBoatClaim, the submission handler inspects the returned success flag. If success=false, it displays the message property to the user and does not navigate away. If success=true, it stores claimId and claimType in page state and immediately triggers navigation to claim_form.html?claimId={claimId}.

Displaying guidance: The guidance/instruction area on the page displays the instructions string returned by getClaimStartOptions. This content is read-only and updates only when getClaimStartOptions is called.
Back navigation: Clicking the Back to Home link triggers navigation to index.html without additional parameters. No API calls are made as a result of this interaction.

Non-boat claim type interactions: If the user selects a claim type whose claimTypeCode is not boat_insurance, the Start Claim/Continue button for the online flow is disabled or changed to a non-submitting element, and a contextual message is shown to explain that only boat_insurance claims can be started here. The user can change the selection back to boat_insurance to re-enable the online claim start functionality.
page_functionality.state_logic:
State is derived from the URL and API responses; no localStorage or other client-side persistence is used.

URL parameter handling:
- Incoming parameter policyNumber: When claim_start.html is loaded, the page controller reads the policyNumber query parameter, if present (e.g., claim_start.html?policyNumber=XYZ). If found, this value is used to initialize the policy number input field and page state. If not present, the policy number input starts empty.
- No other incoming parameters are read or used on this page.

API response state:
- getClaimStartOptions response (claimTypes and instructions) is fetched once on page load (or on explicit reload) and stored in transient page state. The claimTypes array drives the claim type selector, including detection of which option corresponds to boat_insurance by reading each item's claimTypeCode.
- The instructions string from getClaimStartOptions is stored and bound to the guidance component. It is not modified by the client.
- startBoatClaim response is not persisted beyond the current page navigation. When success=true, claimId and policySummary are stored in transient state only long enough to construct the redirect URL and optionally display confirmation context before navigation. When success=false, only the message property is stored in state to display to the user.

Navigation and outgoing URL parameters:
- Successful boat claim start: On success from startBoatClaim, the page navigates to claim_form.html with claimId in the query string: claim_form.html?claimId={claimId}. The claimId used is exactly the claimId returned by startBoatClaim.
- Back to home: Clicking the Back to Home link navigates directly to index.html with no additional query parameters.

State transitions:
- Changing the claim type selection updates the current claimTypeCode in state and recalculates whether the Start Claim/Continue button is enabled. It is enabled only when the selected claimTypeCode is boat_insurance and a non-empty policyNumber is present.
- Editing the policy number updates state and may enable/disable the Start Claim/Continue button depending on whether the field is empty.
- After navigation to claim_form.html, this page does not retain any state; returning to claim_start.html (via browser back or new navigation) causes a fresh read of URL parameters and new calls to getClaimStartOptions as described above.

components.functionality: Coordinates initial page setup: reads URL parameters, calls getClaimStartOptions, populates claim type options and guidance text, and initializes page-level state for selected claim type and policy number.
event_handlers: onPageLoad: Parse policyNumber from window.location.search and store it in controller state as initialPolicyNumber. Initialize selectedClaimTypeCode to null or to a default if desired by business rules.

onPageLoad: Call getClaimStartOptions with no parameters. On success, store claimTypes and instructions in state. Identify the boat_insurance option by inspecting each claimTypeCode in the returned array; store its identifier or index for downstream components.
onClaimTypesLoaded: Notify the claim-type-selector and claim-start-guidance-panel components with the loaded claimTypes and instructions so they can render.

onStateChange: When selectedClaimTypeCode or policyNumber changes (from other components), update derived flags such as canStartBoatClaim and propagate these to dependent components like the policy-number-form and submission handler.

components.functionality: Displays the list of claim types returned by getClaimStartOptions and allows the user to select one, with special logic to recognize the boat_insurance option.
event_handlers: onClaimTypesReceived: Render a selectable control (e.g., radio list) for each entry in getClaimStartOptions.claimTypes using the label and description fields, ensuring that claimTypeCode remains available in the selection value.

onClaimTypeChange: When the user selects a claim type, send the selected claimTypeCode to the page controller. If the selected code is boat_insurance, trigger enabling of the policy number form and Start Claim/Continue action. If it is not boat_insurance, trigger logic to disable or transform the Start Claim/Continue action and show explanatory messaging.
onReset: Clear the current selection when the page controller requests a reset (e.g., if an unrecoverable error occurs and the UI is reset).

components.functionality: Captures the policy number to associate with the boat insurance claim, pre-filling from the policyNumber URL parameter when available, and providing validation feedback before startBoatClaim is called.
event_handlers: onInit: Receive initialPolicyNumber from the page controller and pre-populate the policy number input field with it if provided.

onInputChange: Update the policyNumber in the page controller state on each change or blur. Also recompute whether the Start Claim/Continue button should be enabled based on non-empty value and selectedClaimTypeCode == 'boat_insurance'.

onSubmitAttempt: When the user attempts to submit the form, perform client-side validation: ensure policyNumber is not empty and optionally matches any configured pattern (if any). If validation fails, prevent form submission, highlight the field, and notify the error-banner component with validation messages. If validation passes, notify the submission handler component to proceed with the API call.

onClaimTypeChange: When notified that the selected claim type is not boat_insurance, the component can either hide or disable the policy number input or mark it as not required for the current selection. When boat_insurance is selected, the field becomes required again.

components.functionality: Shows guidance and instructions to the user about starting a claim, driven entirely by the instructions text returned from getClaimStartOptions.
event_handlers: onInstructionsReceived: Render the instructions string returned from getClaimStartOptions without modification. This may include text on what information will be needed to complete a claim.

onVisibilityChange: Optionally show or hide additional contextual hints based on selectedClaimTypeCode (e.g., additional emphasis when boat_insurance is selected), using only locally defined static hint text if required and without introducing any new data dependencies.

components.functionality: Handles the actual initiation of a boat insurance claim by calling startBoatClaim when the user submits the form with a boat_insurance claim type and valid policy number, then routes to the claim form or displays errors.
event_handlers: onSubmitFromPolicyForm: When the policy-number-form reports a valid submit attempt and the page controller indicates that selectedClaimTypeCode is boat_insurance, call startBoatClaim with the current policyNumber from state.

onStartBoatClaimResponse: Receive the response from startBoatClaim. If success=false, send the message to the claim-start-error-banner and keep the user on the page. If success=true, store claimId, claimType, and policySummary in transient local state and trigger navigation to claim_form.html?claimId={claimId}.

onNonBoatSelection: If the user attempts to submit while selectedClaimTypeCode is not boat_insurance, prevent the API call and notify the error-banner or UI to show an informational message that this page only starts boat_insurance claims.
onBeforeUnload: No special persistence is performed; ensure that no long-running requests are left unresolved when the page unloads if the environment requires cleanup.

components.functionality: Displays error and informational messages resulting from validation failures or from the startBoatClaim API response.
event_handlers: onValidationError: Display client-side validation messages provided by the policy-number-form (e.g., missing policy number). These messages are static UI text and are not derived from any external data models.
onApiError: Display the message returned by startBoatClaim when success=false. This message is rendered as provided without modification.
onClearErrors: Clear any displayed messages when the user changes input (e.g., edits the policy number) or changes claim type, as instructed by the page controller.

components.functionality: Provides navigation actions including Back to Home and handles redirection to the claim form after a successful startBoatClaim call.
event_handlers: onBackToHomeClick: Navigate to index.html without additional URL parameters.
onClaimCreated: When notified by the submission handler that startBoatClaim returned success=true with a claimId, construct a URL of the form claim_form.html?claimId={claimId} and trigger navigation.

onNavigationError: If for any reason claimId is missing in a success=true response (unexpected state), prevent navigation and send a message to the error-banner to inform the user that the claim could not be started online at this time.

layout.grid_system: Likely a 12-column fixed-width grid (approx. 1140–1200px) centered within the viewport, with the left 7–8 columns for main content and right 4–5 columns for the sidebar CTA/agent column.
layout.layout_pattern: The page uses a two-column layout under the existing full-width top navigation. A centered content container (approx. 1140–1200px wide) is split into a wider left column for the main claim start flow and a narrower right column for supporting guidance and actions. Within the left column, content flows top-to-bottom: page title and intro (owned by the page controller), then the claim-type selector, followed by the policy-number form. The right column starts at the same vertical level as the page title with a guidance panel, followed by stacked navigation controls (primary/secondary CTAs). A full-width error banner sits above both columns when present. Each block is separated by consistent vertical spacing, mirroring the mockup’s linear content flow.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page uses a two-column layout under the existing full-width top navigation. A centered content container (approx. 1140–1200px wide) is split into a wider left column for the main claim start flow and a narrower right column for supporting guidance and actions. Within the left column, content flows top-to-bottom: page title and intro (owned by the page controller), then the claim-type selector, followed by the policy-number form. The right column starts at the same vertical level as the page title with a guidance panel, followed by stacked navigation controls (primary/secondary CTAs). A full-width error banner sits above both columns when present. Each block is separated by consistent vertical spacing, mirroring the mockup’s linear content flow.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The left main column occupies roughly 60–65% of the content width (about 7–8 of 12 grid columns), housing the form elements that are central to starting a claim. The right sidebar occupies the remaining 35–40% (about 4–5 grid columns) and is visually framed on a subtle gray background to distinguish it from the main content. Vertically, the top portion of the page (above the fold) is dominated by the page title, the claim-type selector, and the right-side guidance/CTA stack, together taking about the upper 40% of the viewport. Below that, the policy-number form and any additional instructions extend the left column downward, while the right column’s navigation controls remain in view and may become sticky to maintain access to primary actions.
layout_strategies.content_density: Content density is medium-low: each form block is contained in a white card with generous internal padding (~20–24px), and there is comfortable vertical spacing (~24–32px) between stacked sections. Inputs and labels are well spaced, and the claim-type selector uses pill-shaped options with ample breathing room between chips, echoing the location chip layout in the mockup. The right sidebar shows only a few key elements (guidance panel and navigation controls), avoiding clutter while still surfacing multiple actions. Overall, the layout feels spacious and corporate, prioritizing clarity and trustworthiness over dense, text-heavy content.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow follows an F-shaped pattern derived from the mockup. The eye first moves across the top navigation and then down to the bold page title in the left column. From there, attention is pulled rightwards to the guidance panel and the prominent red primary CTA within the navigation controls in the sidebar. The user then scans down the left column through the claim-type selector and policy-number form, before glancing back to the right column for confirmation and action via the stacked buttons. When errors occur, the full-width red-tinted error banner briefly dominates the top of the content area, then the user resumes the left-to-right, top-to-bottom scanning pattern.

layout.overall_description: Below the existing full-width top navigation, the page content sits in a centered, fixed-width container with a light gray page background and white card surfaces for interactable blocks. The container is divided into a wider left column for the main claim initiation steps and a narrower right sidebar for guidance and CTAs, following a 12-column grid with approximately 7–8 columns on the left and 4–5 on the right. A red-tinted error banner, when shown, stretches across the full content width just under the nav, pushing the two-column layout downward. In the left column, a bold, corporate-style page heading like “Start a Claim” anchors the experience, with a short explanatory line beneath it. Immediately below, a card-like claim-type selector presents pill-shaped options in one or two rows, echoing the multi-row chip layout from the mockup. Further down, another white card holds the policy-number form fields, with clear labels, helper text, and spacious alignment. On the right, starting level with the main heading, a white guidance panel card sits on a subtle gray sidebar background, framed with generous padding and a concise question or reassurance about starting a claim. Directly under it, separated by a comfortable gap, a vertical stack of large pill-shaped buttons provides the primary path forward (“Continue”/“Start Claim”) in solid red and a less prominent secondary action in an outline style, spanning the full width of the sidebar. The right column can remain visible (via sticky behavior) as the user scrolls through the left column’s content, ensuring the CTAs are always accessible. Overall, the page feels modern, calm, and trustworthy, with clear separation between main form tasks and supportive guidance, and a strong emphasis on the red brand color for key actions.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This component defines the main page canvas and two-column structure but does not render visible UI elements itself. It establishes a centered content container below the global top navigation with a max width of approximately 1140–1200px and horizontal padding of about 24px on small screens, tightening to 16px on larger screens. Inside this container, it lays out a 12-column grid with the left main column spanning roughly 7–8 columns (about 60–65% width) and the right sidebar spanning 4–5 columns (about 35–40% width). The page controller also provides generous top and bottom padding (around 48–64px total vertical padding) to frame the “hero” region where the page title, claim-type selector, and right-side guidance/CTA block live. All other visible components (error banner, form sections, guidance panel, navigation controls) are positioned within or atop this grid, maintaining consistent vertical spacing between sections (24–32px) to match the mockup’s spacious, corporate feel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Acts as a structural container with a responsive, fixed-width center alignment; content inside is static and scrolls with the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The claim-type selector sits in the left main column, near the top of the page, directly below the page title and introductory text that are governed by the page controller. It occupies the full width of the left column (roughly 60–65% of the overall content width) and is presented as a white, flat card with very light shadow, consistent with the mockup. There is about 24px of vertical space between the bottom of the intro text and the top of this card. Inside the card, a bold label like “What type of claim are you filing?” appears at the top left, with about 12–16px of space below it before the interactive options. The claim types themselves are arranged as pill-shaped options (chips or segmented controls) laid out in one or more rows, similar to the grid of location chips in the mockup. Each pill has about 12–14px vertical padding and 24–28px horizontal padding, with 8–12px horizontal gaps between pills and 8–12px vertical gaps between rows, giving a clean, breathable look. On wide screens, the pills flow from left to right and wrap to a second row if necessary; on smaller screens, they stack in fewer columns while maintaining consistent spacing. The overall height of this card is moderate—tall enough to clearly show multiple options but not so tall that it overwhelms the hero area—occupying roughly the upper third of the left column’s above-the-fold visible area.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the left column; resizes and reflows its pill-shaped options responsively but scrolls normally with the content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The policy-number form appears directly beneath the claim-type selector in the left main column, separated by a vertical gap of about 24–32px to maintain clear section delineation. It spans the full width of the left column (again about 60–65% of the content container) and is rendered as another white card with subtle shadow and rounded corners, visually matching the claim-type selector card. The top of the card contains a heading or strong label such as “Find your policy” or “Enter your policy number,” aligned to the left with about 16–20px of padding above and on the sides. Below the heading, one or more input fields are stacked vertically with 16–24px spacing between them—e.g., a primary text input for the policy number and possibly secondary options (such as a link or small inline control for “I don’t know my policy number”). Labels are left-aligned, with helper text in a lighter gray beneath the relevant inputs, respecting the base 8px spacing units. The card’s internal padding is generous (around 20–24px on all sides), and the bottom of the card leaves enough room (16–24px) below the final input to avoid crowding. When first loaded, the bottom of this card roughly aligns with the upper portion of the right sidebar’s navigation controls, so that scanning down the page keeps both the form and the CTAs in peripheral view. As the user scrolls, this card continues downward while the right-side CTA block may remain partially visible if sticky behavior is enabled for the sidebar.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block in the left column; its internal layout adapts responsively (inputs may move from multi-column to single-column on smaller screens) but it scrolls with the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The guidance panel occupies the top of the right sidebar column, starting at approximately the same vertical level as the left-column page title so that the hero region feels horizontally balanced. It takes the full width of the right column (about 35–40% of the content container) and sits on a subtle light gray background that visually distinguishes the sidebar from the main content. Within this gray sidebar zone, the panel itself is a white card with rounded corners and a very light shadow, inset with about 16–24px of margin from the sidebar’s edges. The card has generous internal padding (~20–24px on all sides). At the top left of the panel card is a concise, reassuring heading such as “Need help starting your claim?” accompanied optionally by a small, flat icon to the left (e.g., an info or headset icon), with about 8–12px of space between icon and text. Below the heading is a short paragraph or bulleted list describing what information is needed or what to expect after starting a claim, spaced with about 8–12px between lines or bullets. Any contact hint (like “Call your local agent” or “We’re here 24/7”) sits at the bottom of the card, separated from the body text by around 16px. Vertically, this panel card occupies roughly the top 40% of the sidebar section that is visible above the fold, leaving enough space below for the large CTA buttons that live in the navigation controls component.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the right sidebar; participates in any sidebar-level sticky behavior so that if the sidebar is made sticky, it remains visible near the top of the viewport.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This component is a behavioral controller with no direct visual rendering. Conceptually, it is associated with the lower portion of the left column and the navigation controls in the right column, wiring the claim-type selector and policy-number form together with the primary CTA in the navigation controls. It lives at the page level, anchored to the content container defined by the page controller, and listens for submit events initiated via the navigation controls’ primary button. It coordinates validation and error triggering, but does not take up any spatial footprint or alter the visual layout other than causing the error banner to appear when necessary.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: No visual spatial behavior; logically bound to the form components and the navigation controls, operating in the background without occupying layout space.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The error banner appears as a full-width notification band just below the global top navigation and above the main two-column content container. When visible, it spans the entire width of the content area (matching the 1140–1200px container width) and slightly overlaps the conceptual grid, visually sitting atop both left and right columns. The banner has a red or red-tinted background aligned with the brand color, with white or dark text for strong readability. It has about 16–20px of vertical padding and 20–24px of horizontal padding, and its height is typically between 56–80px depending on the number of lines of text. There is a small vertical gap (about 16–24px) below the banner before the top of the hero content (page title and guidance panel) begins, preserving the spacious look from the mockup. On smaller screens, the banner remains full-width and pushes all other content downward, ensuring it is the first item users see after the nav. On larger screens, it sits flush with the content container edges, visually anchored to the grid. When multiple errors occur, they are consolidated here, but the layout still keeps the banner as a single compact strip rather than sprawling content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Appears conditionally; static at the top of the content area when present, pushing the entire two-column layout down. It scrolls out of view as the user moves down the page (not sticky) to avoid obstructing the main content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The navigation controls sit in the right sidebar, directly below the guidance panel card, separated from it by a vertical gap of approximately 24–32px. They occupy the full width of the right column’s inner content area (about 35–40% of the content container) and mirror the mockup’s stack of strong, pill-shaped CTAs. The component is structured as a vertical stack of two or three large buttons: at the top, a solid red, pill-shaped primary button labeled something like “Continue” or “Start Claim,” followed by one or more secondary actions in outline style (e.g., “Cancel and go back” or “Save and finish later”), each spanning nearly the full width of the sidebar card area. Each button has generous vertical padding (12–16px) and horizontal padding (24–32px), with rounded ends and minimal shadow, matching the modern, flat design. The vertical spacing between buttons is about 16–20px, giving them clear separation. On desktops, this stack typically occupies roughly the lower 60% of the sidebar above the fold, complementing the guidance panel above. The entire navigation-controls block can be placed inside a subtle white card or sit directly on the gray sidebar background; in either case, it maintains consistent alignment with the guidance panel edges. When the user scrolls, the navigation controls may adopt sticky behavior within the sidebar so that the primary CTA remains visible alongside the scrolling form in the left column, reinforcing the F-shaped visual flow: users scan across from the headline to the primary red button, then down through the form and buttons.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static in document flow but may be configured as a sticky sidebar section on larger screens, remaining pinned near the top-right of the viewport while the left-column content scrolls; on small screens, it becomes a standard block that follows the form content and sits near the bottom of the page.

name: Claim Form
title: Boat Insurance Claim Form
description: Multi-step form that lets users complete and submit a boat insurance claim by entering incident details, owner contact information, and a free-text incident description, reviewing all information, and receiving an on-page confirmation. The form supports resuming an in-progress claim using URL parameters.

core_features: Load the current claim context and active step using getCurrentClaimFormState, driven by URL parameters claimId and step.
Incident details step: capture and update incident date, location, cause of damage, estimated damage amount, injuries status, and police report status using updateCurrentClaimIncidentDetails.
Owner details step: capture and update owner first name, last name, phone, and email using updateCurrentClaimOwnerDetails.
Incident description step: capture and update a free-text description of what happened and visible damage using updateCurrentClaimIncidentDescription.
Review step: retrieve a consolidated claim summary (policy summary, incident details, owner details, and incident description) using getCurrentClaimReviewSummary.
Submission step: submit the active claim using submitCurrentClaim and show an on-page confirmation state using the returned status and claimNumber.
URL-driven step navigation (incident_details → owner_details → description → review → submitted) using step query parameter without relying on local storage.
Support for resuming a partially completed claim via claimId and restoring pre-filled values from getCurrentClaimFormState on each step.
user_workflows: Initial load with existing claim: 1) User lands on claim_form.html with claimId in the URL (and optionally step). 2) Page reads claimId and step from the URL. 3) Page calls getCurrentClaimFormState to retrieve the active claim state. 4) Page determines the active step based on the step URL parameter if present, otherwise from the returned step value. 5) The step router component renders the corresponding step form pre-filled with any existing data.

Incident details step completion: 1) User fills in incident date, location, cause of damage, estimated damage amount, injuries status, and police report status. 2) User clicks the primary action to continue. 3) Incident form component validates input locally. 4) On valid input, the component calls updateCurrentClaimIncidentDetails with the entered values. 5) If the API response indicates success, the component reads nextStep from the response. 6) Page navigates to claim_form.html with the same claimId and step set to nextStep via the URL (typically owner_details).

Owner details step completion: 1) User reviews or enters owner first name, last name, phone, and email. 2) User clicks the continue action. 3) The form validates required fields and formats. 4) The component calls updateCurrentClaimOwnerDetails with the entered values. 5) On success, the page uses the returned nextStep to update the step query parameter (typically description) and reloads the appropriate step.

Incident description step completion and move to review: 1) User enters a free-text incident description. 2) User clicks the action to review the claim. 3) Component validates minimum requirements (for example, non-empty). 4) Component calls updateCurrentClaimIncidentDescription with the entered text. 5) On success, the page uses nextStep from the response to set step=review in the URL and loads the review step.

Review and edit before submission: 1) On entering the review step (step=review), the page calls getCurrentClaimReviewSummary. 2) The review component renders the policy summary, incident details, owner details, and incident description. 3) User can click contextual edit actions next to each section (incident, owner, description). 4) Each edit action updates the step query parameter in the URL to the corresponding step (incident_details, owner_details, or description), causing the appropriate form step to render with pre-filled values. 5) After editing and saving a section, the user can navigate forward again through the steps or directly back to review (e.g., via a dedicated ‘Return to review’ action that sets step=review).

Claim submission and confirmation: 1) From the review step, user clicks the submit action. 2) Page optionally performs a lightweight validation that all required sections are present. 3) Page calls submitCurrentClaim without additional parameters. 4) On success, the page reads the returned success, claimNumber, status, and message. 5) The page updates the URL to step=submitted while staying on claim_form.html. 6) A confirmation component renders the confirmation message, including claimNumber and status, and hides or disables editing actions. 7) If submission fails, the component shows error feedback and keeps the step at review.
