Direct deep-link to a later step: 1) User follows a URL to claim_form.html with claimId and step set to a specific value (for example, description or review). 2) Page calls getCurrentClaimFormState to populate current details. 3) If the user’s URL step differs from the returned step, the router applies a page-level rule for resolving conflicts (e.g., allow the URL step but render with whatever data is available from the current claim state). 4) The relevant form or review components display pre-filled data where available, and the user can proceed or navigate back using step changes in the URL.
interactions: On page initialization, a controller component reads claimId and step from the URL, calls getCurrentClaimFormState, and sets the internal view state to the resolved active step.
When users change input fields on any step, the corresponding form components update their local form state for those fields (without calling APIs until form submission).
When users submit the incident details form, the form component triggers updateCurrentClaimIncidentDetails with the current form values and handles success or error responses.
When users submit the owner details form, the form component triggers updateCurrentClaimOwnerDetails with the current form values and handles success or error responses.
When users submit the incident description form, the form component triggers updateCurrentClaimIncidentDescription with the current form value and handles success or error responses.
When the review step is activated, the review component calls getCurrentClaimReviewSummary, binds the returned summary data to read-only fields, and displays it to the user.
When users click edit actions on the review step, the router updates the step query parameter to show the selected editing step while preserving claimId.
When users click the submit button on the review step, the submit handler calls submitCurrentClaim and, on success, updates the URL to step=submitted and renders the confirmation state.

When users click any back or previous-step controls, the router updates the step query parameter (for example, from owner_details to incident_details) and re-renders the relevant form with previously saved values loaded from getCurrentClaimFormState.
page_functionality.state_logic: The page relies on URL query parameters and the claim form APIs for state. On every load or navigation: 1) The page reads claimId and step from the URL. 2) If claimId is present, the server-side environment is assumed to have already associated it with the active claim context; the frontend then calls getCurrentClaimFormState to obtain hasActiveClaim, the canonical claimId, current step, and any partial data. 3) If the step URL parameter is missing, the page uses the step value returned by getCurrentClaimFormState as the active step and updates internal view state accordingly (the URL may be updated to include this step for consistency). 4) If the step URL parameter is present, it is used as the active step for rendering, while getCurrentClaimFormState provides pre-filled values; if the API indicates a different step, the application can either: (a) accept the URL step and render it with whatever data is currently available, or (b) optionally normalize future navigation so that the step parameter reflects the actual progression returned from the APIs. 5) Each successful update call (updateCurrentClaimIncidentDetails, updateCurrentClaimOwnerDetails, updateCurrentClaimIncidentDescription) returns a nextStep value; the page uses this to construct a new URL for claim_form.html with the same claimId but step set to nextStep, then navigates to that URL so that the browser history and reload behavior mirror the user's progression. 6) When getCurrentClaimReviewSummary is called for step=review, no additional URL changes occur until the user either edits a section (which updates step accordingly) or submits the claim. 7) submitCurrentClaim does not take parameters; on success, the page sets step=submitted in the URL and renders a confirmation state while staying on claim_form.html. 8) No localStorage or other client-side persistence is used; resuming or refreshing a claim relies entirely on claimId and step URL parameters and the data returned from the assigned interfaces.

components.functionality: On page load, reads claimId and step from the URL, calls getCurrentClaimFormState to fetch the current claim context, resolves the active step, and initializes page-level state for the rest of the components.

Determines which step-specific component to render (incident_details, owner_details, description, review, or submitted) based on the active step derived from URL parameters and getCurrentClaimFormState, and passes the necessary data and callbacks to child components.

Displays the current step in the multi-step claim process and how many steps remain, using the active step state; optionally allows non-linear navigation only to already-visited steps by updating the step URL parameter.

Renders the incident details step with fields for incident date, incident location, cause of damage, estimated damage amount, injuries status, and police report status. Pre-fills values from incidentDetails in getCurrentClaimFormState and updates the claim when submitted via updateCurrentClaimIncidentDetails.

Renders the owner details step with fields for owner first name, last name, phone, and email. Pre-fills values from ownerDetails in getCurrentClaimFormState and updates the claim when submitted via updateCurrentClaimOwnerDetails.

Renders the incident description step with a text area for the free-text description of the incident and visible damage. Pre-fills the value from incidentDescription in getCurrentClaimFormState and updates the claim when submitted via updateCurrentClaimIncidentDescription.

Renders a read-only summary of the claim for the review step, including policy summary (policy number, policy type name, boat description), incident details, owner details, and incident description. Retrieves data via getCurrentClaimReviewSummary whenever the review step becomes active.

Encapsulates the submission action on the review step. When the user clicks the submit button, calls submitCurrentClaim, handles success or error responses, and delegates to the router to move to the submitted confirmation state.

Displays the final confirmation state after a successful claim submission, showing the returned claimNumber, status, and message from submitCurrentClaim. This component is rendered when step=submitted.

Centralizes navigation between steps by reading and updating the step query parameter in the URL based on user actions and nextStep values returned from updateCurrentClaimIncidentDetails, updateCurrentClaimOwnerDetails, updateCurrentClaimIncidentDescription, and submitCurrentClaim.

Shows error or warning messages when API calls fail or when getCurrentClaimFormState indicates that no active claim is available for the provided claimId. It can also surface validation errors collected from the form components.

layout.grid_system: Likely a 12-column fixed-width grid (approx. 1140–1200px) centered within the viewport, with the left 7–8 columns for main content and right 4–5 columns for the sidebar CTA/agent column.
layout.layout_pattern: Under the existing full-width top navigation, the claim page uses the same two-column grid as the mockup: a centered, fixed-width content area (approx. 1140–1200px) split into a wider left main column and a narrower right sidebar. All claim steps (forms, review, confirmation) flow top-to-bottom in the left column, each as a clear card-like section. The right column consistently houses the claim-progress-indicator in a white card, acting like the mockup’s quote card: a high-level status block that stays visible while the user progresses. Error feedback and navigation controls sit in the left column above and below the active step respectively, preserving a clean, linear vertical reading order.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Under the existing full-width top navigation, the claim page uses the same two-column grid as the mockup: a centered, fixed-width content area (approx. 1140–1200px) split into a wider left main column and a narrower right sidebar. All claim steps (forms, review, confirmation) flow top-to-bottom in the left column, each as a clear card-like section. The right column consistently houses the claim-progress-indicator in a white card, acting like the mockup’s quote card: a high-level status block that stays visible while the user progresses. Error feedback and navigation controls sit in the left column above and below the active step respectively, preserving a clean, linear vertical reading order.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The main content container is centered and uses a 12-column grid. The left column occupies roughly 7–8 columns (about 60–65% width) and is reserved for the current step’s primary content: incident details form, owner details form, incident description form, review summary, and final confirmation. The right column spans the remaining 4–5 columns (35–40%) and is dedicated primarily to the claim-progress-indicator card. Vertical spacing follows the mockup: major blocks (forms, summary, confirmation) are separated by about 24–32px, with slightly larger breathing room (32–48px) above the first main card under the nav.
layout_strategies.content_density: Content density mirrors the mockup’s medium-low density. Each form and summary section appears as a spacious white card with generous internal padding of ~20–24px. Form fields are stacked with 16–24px gaps, and section headings have clear vertical separation from inputs. Only one main step is visible in the left column at a time to keep cognitive load low, while the right-hand progress card is compact but readable. The review and confirmation steps show more text but maintain the same airy spacing, preventing the layout from feeling cramped despite the amount of information.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow follows an F-shaped pattern adapted to a form context. The eye runs left-to-right across the top navigation, then down to the bold left-column step heading and introductory copy of the active form. From there, attention is pulled to the right by the red-highlighted steps inside the claim-progress-indicator card, mirroring the mockup’s hero CTA. The user then moves vertically down the left column, scanning through form fields or summary content, and finishes at the bottom-aligned step-navigation-controller and/or claim-submit-control buttons. On review and confirmation, this top-left headline → right-side status → down-left primary content → bottom controls sequence remains consistent, reinforcing the sense of progress and completion.

layout.overall_description: Below the full-width red-branded navigation, a centered content area uses a two-column marketing-style layout repurposed for the claim flow. On the left, a broad white card fills about two-thirds of the width, holding the current step: a clearly titled form or summary with ample padding and neatly stacked fields. On the right, a narrower sidebar on a light gray background contains a floating white card that shows the step-based claim-progress-indicator, visually echoing the quote card from the mockup. A thin red progress motif highlights the current step. When errors occur, a red-tinted banner appears at the top of the left column, just under the nav, without pushing anything off-center. As the user scrolls down long forms, the right-hand progress card remains in view, while the left column scrolls through inputs. At the bottom of each step, large pill-shaped red buttons provide clear navigation and submission actions. The result feels like a structured, corporate marketing layout that has been carefully adapted into a guided, multi-step form, with a strong sense of progression and completion.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The claim-form-initializer sits logically at the very top of the main content area, directly beneath the site’s global navigation but above any visible cards. It does not render a visible block of its own; instead, it acts as an invisible layer that initializes and restores claim state before the visual components appear. Spatially, it wraps the entire claim-step-router and its children, occupying effectively zero height and full content width, so it does not change the visual layout or spacing.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual controller; occupies logical full-width around the main claim components without affecting layout flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The claim-step-router defines the main two-column structure beneath the global navigation. It occupies the full width of the centered content container (approx. 1140–1200px), with 32–48px of top margin below the nav to create a hero-like breathing space. Inside it, a 12-column grid is used: the left main column spans roughly 7–8 columns (about 60–65% width) for the active step’s content, and the right sidebar spans 4–5 columns (about 35–40% width) for the progress card. All child components—error banner, forms, summary, confirmation, progress indicator, and navigation controls—are positioned relative to this router: the left column scrolls linearly top-to-bottom while the right column remains visually distinct on a slightly gray background.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static structural container that governs the two-column grid; it scrolls with the page but does not itself become sticky or fixed.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The claim-progress-indicator is placed in the right sidebar at the top, aligned horizontally with the top of the left column’s first main card for the current step. It is enclosed in a white, softly shadowed card that fills most of the sidebar width (about 80–90% of the right column), centered with 16–24px of padding on all sides and 24–32px margin from the top of the router. The card height is modest (roughly 140–200px), showing step labels in a horizontal or stepper format with the current step highlighted in red. Below the steps, there is room for a one- or two-line status message (e.g., “Step 2 of 4”). This card visually mirrors the mockup’s quote card, acting as the first focal point on the right side when the page loads.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Positioned in the right column with a sticky behavior: once the user scrolls past its initial position, the card remains pinned near the top of the viewport within the sidebar so the user can always see their progress while the left column content scrolls.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: On the step where incident details are collected, the incident-details-form occupies the upper portion of the left column, starting just below any visible claim-error-banner. It appears as a single, prominent white card spanning nearly the full width of the left column (about 90–100% of its width) with 20–24px internal padding and 24–32px margin above and below. A bold step title and concise explanatory text sit at the top of the card, followed by the form fields arranged in a single column, each separated by about 16–24px. The card height grows with content, but the top of this card visually aligns horizontally with the top of the right-side progress indicator card, maintaining the balanced hero-like layout established in the mockup.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Flows naturally in the left column, scrolling under the fixed navigation while the right sidebar progress card remains visible; no sticky behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: When the flow is on the owner information step, the owner-details-form replaces the incident-details-form in the same left-column slot. It uses the identical spatial pattern: a full-width white card within the left column, with a clear step heading at the top, a brief subheading or helper text, and stacked input fields below. The card’s internal padding and spacing between fields mirror the previous step (20–24px padding, 16–24px vertical gaps), and its top edge is aligned with the top of the right sidebar’s progress card. The vertical space between this card and the step-navigation-controller at the bottom remains around 24–32px to keep the layout breathable.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Reuses the main left-column card position, scrolling in sync with the page; no fixed positioning.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: On the free-text incident description step, the incident-description-form again occupies the main left column card area, following the same dimensions and positioning as the earlier step forms. The card’s top aligns with the right-side progress card, and it spans nearly the full width of the left column. At the top, a prominent heading and a short instruction line are followed by a large multi-line text area, possibly with a character counter or helper text beneath it. Vertical spacing around the text area is generous—around 16–24px above and below—to maintain the medium-low content density. Additional smaller fields, if any, are stacked below with consistent gaps.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard scrolling block in the left column; no sticky behavior, but always above the step-navigation-controller.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: In the review step, the claim-review-summary expands to become the dominant element of the left column. It appears as a tall, full-width summary card with 24px padding, starting just below any claim-error-banner and aligning horizontally with the top of the right sidebar progress card. Within the card, information is grouped into clear sections (Incident Details, Owner Details, Description), each separated by 16–24px and possibly divided by subtle lines. Labels are left-aligned with consistent spacing to the corresponding values, making it scannable like a corporate report. This summary card extends farther down the page than the earlier forms but maintains 24–32px of space above the claim-submit-control, which sits immediately below it.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Occupies the main left-column area; scrolls normally beneath the navigation while the right progress card remains in view.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The claim-submit-control is positioned directly below the claim-review-summary card in the review step, still within the left column. It spans the same width as the summary card (about 90–100% of the left column), with 24–32px of vertical margin separating it from the card above. Inside, a primary pill-shaped red 'Submit Claim' button is left-aligned and visually large, with approximately 12–16px vertical and 24–32px horizontal padding. A secondary, low-emphasis 'Back' or 'Edit' control may sit to its right or beneath it with a smaller visual weight. This block acts as the final focal point at the bottom of the left column, in line with the mockup’s prominent CTA pattern.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Placed at the bottom of the review step’s left-column content; it scrolls with the rest of the page and is not fixed, but it is kept close to the summary for a tight decision cluster.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: After a successful submission, the claim-submission-confirmation component replaces the previous forms and summary in the left column. It becomes the primary card in that column, starting just below any remaining claim-error-banner area (usually absent in this state) and aligning horizontally with the persistent right-side progress card, which now shows all steps as complete. The confirmation appears as a spacious white card with 24px padding, featuring a large, bold heading (e.g., 'Claim Submitted') and a short explanatory paragraph. Any reference number or next steps are laid out below with 16–24px spacing. The card height is moderate, leaving comfortable empty space below, preserving the clean marketing-like finish of the flow.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Occupies the standard left-column hero card position and scrolls normally; it does not fix to the viewport.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Throughout the multi-step process (except where replaced by claim-submit-control on the final review step), the step-navigation-controller is positioned at the bottom of the left column, directly under whichever form is currently active. There is a 24–32px vertical gap above it to separate the nav buttons from the last field group. The controller spans the full width of the left column’s content area and holds the step navigation actions: a dominant red pill-shaped 'Next' or 'Continue' button aligned to the left, with a lighter-weight 'Back' text or outline button to its left or right. This placement mirrors the mockup’s stacked CTA pattern, keeping the primary action highly visible at the end of the vertical scan.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Flows with the left column content; not sticky. On longer forms it may appear below the fold, encouraging users to complete fields before advancing.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The claim-error-banner sits at the top of the left column, directly below the global navigation and above the main card for the current step (form, summary, or confirmation). When visible, it spans nearly the full width of the left column, with 16–20px of internal padding and 16–24px of bottom margin before the next card. Its height adjusts to the error content but generally remains compact. The banner uses a red-tinted background in line with the brand color, making it immediately noticeable without overpowering the rest of the page. It does not extend into the right column, preserving the visual separation between main content and sidebar status.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Appears and disappears in the normal document flow, pushing the main left-column card down when present; it scrolls with the page and does not stick to the viewport.

name: Agent Search
title: Agent Search
description: Page for searching local boat insurance agents by ZIP code, distance radius, and sort order, and navigating to an individual agent detail page.

core_features: Accept ZIP code, radius, and sort criteria from the user or URL and invoke the searchAgents interface to retrieve matching agents.
Reflect the current search criteria in the URL as query parameters (zip, radiusMiles, sortBy) so that results are shareable and restorable via direct link.
Display a list of agents returned by searchAgents, including name, office address, primary phone, ZIP, and distance, sorted according to the selected sortBy option.
Provide navigation from each agent in the results list to agent_detail.html by passing the agentId in the URL.
Handle loading, error, and empty-result states for the searchAgents request so the user clearly understands what is happening.
Allow users to refine their search by adjusting ZIP code, search radius, and sort order, with each change updating both the URL and the search results.
user_workflows:
Workflow 1: Initial load with pre-filled ZIP from another page
1) User clicks a "Find an Agent" link elsewhere on the site that navigates to agent_search.html with a zip query parameter.
2) The page-init controller reads zip, radiusMiles, and sortBy from the URL.
3) If zip is present and valid, the controller derives the parameters for searchAgents (mapping zip → zipCode and using radiusMiles and sortBy if present or system defaults if not).
4) The controller sets the page into a loading state and invokes searchAgents.
5) When searchAgents returns, the controller updates the shared search state with searchLocation, radiusMiles, sortBy, and the agents array from the response.
6) The search form is initialized with the current zip, radius, and sortBy values so the user sees the criteria that produced the current results.
7) The results toolbar shows the normalized search location and radius, and the results list displays each agent with contact information and distance.
8) The user can click any agent name to navigate to agent_detail.html with agentId in the URL.

Workflow 2: User starts a new search directly on agent_search.html
1) User navigates to agent_search.html without query parameters.
2) The page-init controller detects that no zip query parameter is present and does not call searchAgents yet; it renders the search form in an idle state.
3) The user enters a ZIP code in the form and optionally adjusts radius and sort order.
4) The user submits the form; the form component validates the ZIP format at the UI level.
5) On successful validation, the form updates the URL query parameters (zip, radiusMiles, sortBy) and notifies the page-init controller that new search criteria are available.
6) The controller sets the loading state and calls searchAgents with the current criteria.
7) When results return, the list, toolbar, and summary are updated; the user sees agents near the entered ZIP.
8) The user can refine criteria and resubmit as needed, each time updating the URL and triggering searchAgents again.

Workflow 3: Changing radius or sort order with existing results
1) User is viewing search results based on an existing search.
2) The user changes the radius selection (for example, choosing a larger or smaller radius) in the toolbar or form.
3) When the user confirms the change (e.g., via form submission or a dedicated Apply action), the component updates the radiusMiles query parameter in the URL and keeps the existing zip and sortBy values.
4) The page-init controller detects the updated criteria, sets loading state, and calls searchAgents with the new radius.
5) The response returns agents filtered to the new radius; the toolbar and results list update to reflect the new radius and potentially different result set.
6) Similarly, if the user changes the sort order (e.g., to sort by distance), the component updates sortBy in the URL, triggers searchAgents, and the results list re-renders according to the new sort.

Workflow 4: Handling no results
1) User submits a search with certain ZIP and radius values.
2) searchAgents returns successfully with an empty agents array.
3) The controller sets a "no results" state while still keeping searchLocation, radiusMiles, and sortBy from the response.
4) The empty-state component displays a message indicating that no agents were found for the current criteria and suggests adjusting radius or ZIP.
5) The search form remains populated with the current criteria so the user can easily expand the radius or modify the ZIP and resubmit.
6) When the user changes criteria and resubmits, the URL and searchAgents call are updated, and the empty state is replaced by loading and then either a new results list or another empty state.

Workflow 5: Handling search errors
1) User submits a search and searchAgents is invoked.
2) A network error or interface error occurs and searchAgents does not return a successful response.
3) The controller switches from loading state to an error state, capturing any error information available from the failure.
4) The error component displays a generic failure message and a Retry action.
5) When the user clicks Retry, the controller reuses the current criteria from the URL (zip, radiusMiles, sortBy), re-enters loading state, and calls searchAgents again.
6) If the retry succeeds, the error state is cleared and replaced with the standard results or empty state.

Workflow 6: Navigating to an agent detail page
1) User is viewing search results for a given ZIP and radius.
2) The user clicks on the name of a specific agent in the results list.
3) The results list component retrieves the agentId from that result item.
4) The browser navigates to agent_detail.html with agentId appended as a query parameter (e.g., agent_detail.html?agentId=[agentId]).
5) The agent detail page uses the agentId to load and display detailed information about that agent (handled outside this page).
interactions: Typing in ZIP input: Updates the local form state for ZIP without immediately calling searchAgents; validation rules ensure that only ZIP values matching the allowed format are considered valid for submission.

Changing radius selection: Updates the local form or toolbar state for radiusMiles; upon user confirmation (form submit or apply action), the URL parameter radiusMiles is updated and a new searchAgents call is triggered.
Changing sort order: Updates the local sortBy state; on confirmation, the sortBy query parameter is updated in the URL and a new searchAgents call is made to retrieve results in the selected order.

Submitting the search form: Validates ZIP and any other required fields; if valid, composes a new URL with zip, radiusMiles, and sortBy, pushes it to the browser (e.g., via full reload or history API), and notifies the controller to call searchAgents with those parameters.

Initial page load or URL change: The page-init controller reads zip, radiusMiles, and sortBy from the URL; if zip is present and valid, it triggers searchAgents; the UI reflects a loading state until a response is received.

Viewing results: The results list component iterates over the agents array from searchAgents, rendering each agent’s name, office address, office phone, office ZIP, and distanceMiles; the list is ordered according to the sortBy value returned from searchAgents.
Clicking an agent name: Triggers navigation to agent_detail.html with the clicked agent’s agentId as a URL parameter, allowing deeper interaction on the agent detail page.

Retry after error: When an error is displayed, the user can click a Retry control; this reuses the current URL parameters as search criteria and calls searchAgents again while showing a loading state.

Back/forward navigation in the browser: When the user navigates using the browser’s back and forward buttons, the URL query parameters change; the page-init controller detects these changes and re-runs searchAgents based on the URL, ensuring the visible state always matches the URL.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The primary source of truth for search state is the URL query string, specifically the parameters zip, radiusMiles, and sortBy.

1) URL → Internal State
- On page load, the page-init controller parses the URL query parameters.
  - zip is mapped directly to the user-visible ZIP input and to the zipCode parameter for searchAgents.
  - radiusMiles, if present, is mapped directly to the internal radius state and to the radiusMiles parameter for searchAgents.
  - sortBy, if present, is mapped directly to the internal sortBy state and to the sortBy parameter for searchAgents.
- If zip is present and passes the client-side validation rules, the controller triggers a searchAgents call using these values.
- If radiusMiles or sortBy are missing, the controller uses application-level defaults (not defined in this page) when calling searchAgents. When the response returns, any normalized values (e.g., a defaulted radiusMiles or sortBy) from the searchAgents response are adopted into the page state.

2) Internal State → URL
- When the user submits the search form or explicitly applies changed criteria (ZIP, radius, sort), the form component composes a new set of query parameters based on its current internal state.
- It updates the browser URL (for example, by triggering a navigation to agent_search.html with the new query string, or by using the History API) to ensure that zip, radiusMiles, and sortBy in the URL match the intended criteria.
- After the URL is updated, the page-init controller reacts to the change and re-runs searchAgents using the new parameters, so the results always correspond to the URL.

3) Response State
- The response from searchAgents contains searchLocation, radiusMiles, sortBy, and an agents array.
- The controller uses searchLocation for user-facing text (e.g., identifying the area being searched) and overwrites any previous internal location description with this value.
- The radiusMiles and sortBy values in the response are considered authoritative; if they differ from the URL (for example, due to server-side normalization), the controller can optionally update the URL to match the response values to keep the URL and server state consistent.
- The agents array is used to populate the results list. If the array is empty, the controller sets an empty-state flag; if it contains items, the controller sets a results-available state.

4) Loading and Error States
- A transient loading state is set each time searchAgents is called and cleared when either a success or error is received.
- If an error occurs, an error flag and optional message are stored in internal state, but the URL is not changed; this allows the user to retry the same search criteria directly.

5) Navigation to Agent Detail
- Clicking an agent name reads its agentId from the current agents list and constructs a URL for agent_detail.html that includes agentId as a query parameter.
- The state on agent_search.html is not persisted beyond the normal browser navigation behavior; if the user later returns via the back button, the state will be reconstructed from the URL and, if zip is still present, searchAgents will be called again using the query parameters.

components.functionality: Coordinates initial page load behavior, synchronizes URL query parameters with internal search state, and triggers calls to the searchAgents interface. Determines whether to run an immediate search (if zip is present and valid) or show only the form. Manages top-level loading, error, and empty-result flags based on searchAgents responses.

Allows the user to enter or edit the ZIP code, select a radius, and choose a sort order. Performs client-side validation on the ZIP field. On submission, updates the URL query parameters (zip, radiusMiles, sortBy) and signals the controller that a new search is requested. Also initializes its fields from the URL-derived state so the inputs always reflect the current search criteria.

Displays summary information about the current search (for example, searchLocation and radiusMiles from searchAgents) and provides quick controls to adjust radius and sort order. When the user changes these controls, it updates the URL parameters and triggers a new search via the controller.

Renders the list of agents returned by searchAgents. For each agent, displays name, office phone, office address (including officeAddressLine1, officeCity, officeState, officeZip), and distanceMiles. Ensures the ordering of items matches the sortBy value provided by searchAgents. Handles click interactions on agent names to navigate to agent_detail.html with the appropriate agentId.

Displays a clear message when searchAgents returns successfully but the agents array is empty. Uses the current search criteria (searchLocation, radiusMiles, and sortBy) to contextualize the message. Encourages the user to modify criteria (such as radius or ZIP) by interacting with the search form or toolbar, without issuing its own API calls.

Shows a loading state whenever a searchAgents request is in progress. The controller toggles this component based on its internal loading flag. The indicator appears in place of results or empty/error messages until the response is received.

Shows an error message and retry option when a searchAgents call fails. It reads any available error details from the controller’s state (not from static text) and provides a Retry control. On Retry, it triggers the controller to re-run searchAgents using the current URL parameters without modifying the URL.

layout.grid_system: Likely a 12-column fixed-width grid (approx. 1140–1200px) centered within the viewport, with the left 7–8 columns for main content and right 4–5 columns for the sidebar CTA/agent column.
layout.layout_pattern: The page uses the same two‑column marketing layout as the mockup. Below the full‑width top navigation, a centered 12‑column content container holds a left main column (7–8 grid columns) and a right sidebar (4–5 grid columns). All of the agent search components live in the left column and stack vertically in a clear top‑to‑bottom flow: the agent-search-form sits at the top as the hero function, the agent-results-toolbar appears directly underneath when relevant, and beneath that lives a shared results area that is occupied by either the agent-results-list, the agent-results-empty-state, the agent-results-loading-indicator, or the agent-results-error-display. The right sidebar maintains its marketing/CTA content from the original design and is visually aligned to start at the same vertical height as the search form, mirroring the hero/sidebar relationship seen in the mockup.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page uses the same two‑column marketing layout as the mockup. Below the full‑width top navigation, a centered 12‑column content container holds a left main column (7–8 grid columns) and a right sidebar (4–5 grid columns). All of the agent search components live in the left column and stack vertically in a clear top‑to‑bottom flow: the agent-search-form sits at the top as the hero function, the agent-results-toolbar appears directly underneath when relevant, and beneath that lives a shared results area that is occupied by either the agent-results-list, the agent-results-empty-state, the agent-results-loading-indicator, or the agent-results-error-display. The right sidebar maintains its marketing/CTA content from the original design and is visually aligned to start at the same vertical height as the search form, mirroring the hero/sidebar relationship seen in the mockup.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Within the 12‑column grid, the left main content column for the search experience occupies roughly 60–65% of the width (about 7–8 columns), while the existing right sidebar occupies the remaining 35–40% (4–5 columns). Vertically, the search form plus the first state of the results area together fill the upper ~40–50% of the viewport, similar to the hero region in the mockup, with additional results extending downward and driving page scroll. Individual blocks (form, toolbar, list/empty/error) are separated by 24–32px of vertical spacing, and each card-like element (error display, list items if carded) maintains 20–24px internal padding to keep the page in line with the base 8px spacing system.
layout_strategies.content_density: The left column keeps a medium‑low content density, echoing the mockup: the search form is spacious with clear separation between fields and the primary search button; the toolbar is compact but not crowded; the list has comfortable gaps between agent cards. The results states (empty, loading, error) are given plenty of breathing room, typically occupying a generous block of space rather than being squeezed between other elements. This preserves a functional, professional feel while still surfacing enough information (filters, counts, multiple agent cards) to be useful at a glance.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow follows an F‑shaped pattern adapted for search: the eye first moves across the top navigation, then down to the left where a prominent search heading and search button in the agent-search-form act as the hero focal point. The existing right sidebar CTA card pulls some attention across to the right, mirroring the original Request a Quote emphasis. From there, the user naturally scans down the left through the agent-results-toolbar (showing counts and sort), then continues vertically into the results area where the agent-results-list, empty, loading, or error state is shown. The right sidebar continues to act as a secondary vertical scan path with its stacked CTAs and agent info cards, but the primary interaction path is anchored in the left column by the search components.

layout.overall_description: The Agent Search page sits within a centered, fixed‑width container beneath a full‑width red‑accented top navigation. The content area is split into a left main column and a right sidebar, echoing the original marketing layout: the left ~65% of the width is dedicated to the search experience, while the right ~35% continues to show a white quote/CTA card, secondary outline buttons, and an agent info card stacked on a soft gray background. At the top of the left column, aligned horizontally with the top of the right sidebar card, a spacious agent search form acts as the functional hero: a clear headline and short description sit above a horizontally arranged ZIP field, radius selector, and bold red pill‑shaped search button. Below this, a slim results toolbar shows the number of agents found and sorting controls. The large block of space beneath the toolbar is reserved for the results experience: when agents are found, it holds a vertical list of agent cards; when no results are available, it shows a centered empty state message in the same footprint; while loading, a centered spinner occupies this region; and on failure, an error card sits here instead. Each element is separated by consistent 24–32px vertical gaps and uses 8‑point‑based padding inside, keeping the page airy yet information‑rich. The net effect is a clean, modern, corporate layout where the search form and results dominate the left side, and a stable, reassuring sidebar of CTAs balances the composition on the right, guiding the eye first across the header, then down through the search flow in a controlled, F‑shaped pattern.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This controller component is non‑visual and does not occupy any explicit space in the layout. Conceptually, it wraps the entire Agent Search content area within the main left column, initializing state for the search form and results. It sits logically at the top of the page structure, just beneath the global navigation and surrounding the left column stack of components, but it does not render any visible UI, borders, or spacing on its own.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non‑visual; no spatial footprint or scrolling behavior. It exists purely for data and state orchestration.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The agent search form is positioned at the top of the left main content column, acting as the primary functional hero of the page. It aligns vertically with the top edge of the main quote/CTA card in the right sidebar so that both columns begin at the same horizontal baseline, mirroring the hero/sidebar relationship in the mockup. Within the centered 12‑column grid, the form spans approximately the first 7–8 columns (about 60–65% of the content width), flush left within the content container. There is a generous top margin of about 48–64px from the bottom of the global navigation to the form, and about 24–32px of space beneath it before the results toolbar. Inside the form, a bold, left‑aligned heading about finding a local boat insurance agent appears at the top, followed by a concise one‑line description, each separated by 8–16px vertical spacing. Below the text, the interactive fields are arranged in a single horizontal row on desktop: a ZIP/postal code input on the left (roughly 40% of the form width), a distance/radius dropdown in the middle (about 20–25%), optionally a sort or filter inline if needed, and a prominent red pill‑shaped search button on the right (about 20–25%), aligned to the form’s far right edge. The row has 16–20px vertical padding above and below, with 16–24px horizontal gaps between controls, following the 8px spacing scale. The overall form has internal padding of around 24px if it is carded, or sits directly on the white background with breathing room around it if it is simple. On smaller breakpoints, these controls stack vertically with 16px gaps, but on desktop they read as a cohesive, horizontally balanced control bar under the heading.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block element within the main scroll; it moves with the page and is not fixed or sticky.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The results toolbar sits directly below the agent-search-form, within the same left main column and aligned to the same left and right edges as the form inputs. It is separated from the form by a vertical gap of about 24px, creating a clear visual step from 'set your criteria' to 'view your results.' The toolbar spans the full width of the left column (those same 7–8 grid columns) and has a low‑profile, bar‑like appearance: a white or very light gray background with subtle top and bottom padding of around 12–16px, and optionally a thin bottom border or light shadow to gently distinguish it from the results area below. Inside the toolbar, the left side displays the results summary (e.g., number of agents found and possibly the active filters), while the right side holds sorting and view controls aligned to the right edge, following the F‑pattern of scanning left to right. Internal content uses a concise layout with 16px horizontal spacing between elements, ensuring it feels organized but not crowded. A 16–24px gap separates the bottom of the toolbar from the top of whichever results state (list, empty, loading, or error) is currently visible.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard scrolling behavior; it remains in document flow and scrolls out of view as the user moves down through a longer list of agents. No sticky behavior by default.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The results list occupies the main results area directly under the agent-results-toolbar within the left column. It is aligned flush left and right with the toolbar and form above, spanning roughly 7–8 grid columns (about 60–65% of the page width). There is a 16–24px vertical gap between the toolbar and the first list item, maintaining consistent separation. The list itself is a vertically stacked column of agent result entries (typically card‑like rows), each agent card spanning almost the full width of the left column with 20–24px padding internally and a vertical gap of 16–24px between cards. The list extends downward as needed and is the primary driver of vertical scroll on the page, while the right sidebar scrolls in parallel. In the initial above‑the‑fold view, users typically see the form, toolbar, and the first one or two agent cards, reinforcing the top‑down flow. The background behind the list is white, maintaining a clean, corporate feel with no heavy dividers aside from subtle shadows or borders on individual cards. When the list is visible, it fully owns this results area; the empty state, loading indicator, and error display are all hidden.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block that grows vertically with the number of agents. It scrolls normally with the page and does not overlay or fix itself relative to the viewport.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The empty state display shares the same footprint as the results list and appears in its place when there are no matching agents. It is positioned directly beneath the agent-results-toolbar, aligned to the same left and right bounds of the left column (7–8 grid columns wide), with a 16–24px gap from the toolbar above. Instead of multiple cards, the empty state presents a single, centered message within a generous vertical block of space, roughly 40–50% of the visible viewport height. The content is horizontally centered within this left column area: an optional simple illustration or icon at the top, a short heading explaining that no agents were found, and a brief paragraph suggesting alternate actions (adjusting radius, double‑checking ZIP, etc.), with 8–16px vertical spacing between these elements. The block resembles a light, unobtrusive card, with 24px internal padding and plenty of surrounding whitespace so it feels calm and informative, not alarming. When this component is active, the agent-results-list, loading indicator, and error display are all hidden, and the right sidebar remains visible to the right as a stable context.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block occupying the results area; it scrolls with the page if content above it (form and toolbar) is taller than the viewport but typically appears mostly within the initial viewport.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The loading indicator also inhabits the same results area directly below the agent-results-toolbar, visible while a search is in progress. It aligns to the left and right edges of the left column and starts about 16–24px under the toolbar. The loading region is given a minimum height roughly equivalent to two or three agent cards (around 40–50% of the viewport height) so that it feels substantial and centered, rather than cramped. Within this region, a spinner or progress indicator is centered both horizontally and vertically in the left column, possibly with a short 'Searching for agents...' label below it, separated by 8–12px. The background remains white and uncluttered, reinforcing the page’s modern, minimal corporate aesthetic. If there was previous content, the loading indicator may temporarily overlay or replace the list content in this area, but visually it appears as a clean, standalone block in place of the results. While it is shown, the list, empty state, and error display are all hidden from view.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: It occupies the same block position as the list and scrolls with the page. Optionally, it can overlay the previous results in this region while dimming them slightly, but it remains confined to the left column and does not fix itself to the viewport.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The error display appears in the results area when a search request fails and takes over the same footprint used by the list and empty state. It is positioned immediately below the agent-results-toolbar, with a 16–24px vertical gap, and spans the full width of the left column (7–8 grid columns). Visually, it behaves like a prominent alert card: a white background with a red or red‑accented border or left stripe, subtle shadow, and about 20–24px internal padding on all sides. Inside, an error icon on the left and a concise error headline on the right form the top row, with a short explanation and possible retry instructions beneath, each separated by 8–12px. The overall height of the card is enough to feel noticeable (roughly the height of one agent card), but it is not overly tall; there may be additional whitespace beneath it before the fold, preserving the medium‑low density. When this component is active, no agent cards or empty state messaging are shown below it in the same area, ensuring the error is clearly understood as the current outcome of the search.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static card within the scrollable content. It pushes the content below (if any) further down the page and scrolls out of view as the user moves up or down. No fixed or sticky behavior.

name: Agent Detail
title: Agent Detail
description: Displays detailed information for a selected local agent, including contact options and a consultation request form.

core_features: Load and display a specific agent’s details (name, full office address, phone numbers, email if available, and service area) using the agentId URL parameter and getAgentDetail interface.
Provide a primary click-to-call action that initiates a phone call to the agent’s primary office phone number, with optional access to a secondary number when available.
Display the agent’s office address in a structured format that can be used by the user for navigation or in external map tools.
Display the agent’s service area summary when serviceRadiusMiles is available.
Provide a "Request a Consultation" form that collects the visitor’s name, email, optional phone, and a free-text message about their boat insurance needs.
Validate consultation form inputs (required fields present, email format basic check) before submission.
Submit consultation requests to the agent via the submitAgentConsultationRequest interface using the currently viewed agent’s agentId.
Display submission status feedback including success confirmation (with requestId and createdAt) or error messaging when submission fails.

Support navigation back to the previous search results by exposing a "Back to Search Results" link that uses URL parameters (lastSearchZip, lastSearchRadius, lastSortBy) to build the agent_search.html URL.
Handle edge states: missing agentId, failed agent load, missing agent fields (such as no secondary phone or no email) by adjusting visible actions and messaging.
user_workflows:
View agent details from search results:
1. User arrives at agent_detail.html with URL parameters including agentId and optional lastSearchZip, lastSearchRadius, and lastSortBy.
2. On page initialization, the page reads agentId from the URL.
3. The page calls getAgentDetail with agentId.
4. While loading, a loading state is shown instead of agent details.
5. If getAgentDetail succeeds, agent information (name, phones, email, address, serviceRadiusMiles, bio) is displayed.
6. If getAgentDetail fails or returns an error condition, an error message is displayed with the option to navigate back to search results (if lastSearch* parameters are present).

Call the agent’s office:
1. After agent details are loaded, the primary call-to-action button displays the primary office phone number from officePhonePrimary.
2. User clicks the primary call button.
3. The click triggers a tel: link using officePhonePrimary to open the default calling application on the user’s device.
4. If officePhoneSecondary is available, a secondary call link is also displayed; clicking it triggers a tel: link using officePhoneSecondary.
5. No additional API calls are made for click-to-call actions.

Request a consultation with the agent:
1. User scrolls to the "Request a Consultation" form below the agent contact details.
2. User fills in required fields: name (fromName), email (fromEmail), and message. User may optionally fill in phone (fromPhone).
3. On submit, client-side validation checks that fromName, fromEmail, and message are non-empty and that fromEmail matches a basic email format pattern.
4. If validation fails, the form shows inline validation messages and prevents submission.
5. If validation passes, the form enters a submitting state (submit button disabled, progress indication) and calls submitAgentConsultationRequest with parameters: agentId (from page state), fromName, fromEmail, fromPhone, message.
6. If submitAgentConsultationRequest returns success = true, the form switches to a success state, shows a confirmation message including agentName and createdAt where available, and may display requestId for reference.
7. The form fields are cleared or disabled once submission is successful.
8. If submitAgentConsultationRequest returns success = false or an error occurs, an error message is displayed and the form returns to an editable state, allowing the user to retry.

Return to search results:
1. User arrives from agent_search.html which passes zip, radiusMiles, and sortBy as URL parameters mapped to lastSearchZip, lastSearchRadius, lastSortBy in agent_detail.html.
2. The page reads lastSearchZip, lastSearchRadius, and lastSortBy from the URL on initialization.
3. If at least lastSearchZip is present, a "Back to Search Results" link is displayed.
4. When the user clicks the link, the browser navigates to agent_search.html with URL parameters zip, radiusMiles, and sortBy set to the values stored in lastSearchZip, lastSearchRadius, and lastSortBy from the current page’s URL.
5. The agent search page is able to reconstruct the user’s previous search based on these parameters.
6. If lastSearch parameters are not present, the page may hide the "Back to Search Results" link or navigate to agent_search.html without any parameters.

Handle deep-link access without search context:
1. User opens agent_detail.html with only agentId in the URL (no lastSearch* parameters).
2. Page loads agent details via getAgentDetail as usual.
3. Because there is no search context, the "Back to Search Results" link is not shown or leads to a generic search page without pre-filled filters.
4. Consultation request and call actions work the same as in the standard workflow.
interactions:
Page initialization:
- Trigger: Page load event.
- Behavior: Read agentId, lastSearchZip, lastSearchRadius, lastSortBy from the URL query parameters. If agentId exists, call getAgentDetail(agentId). Manage internal state: loading, loaded, or error.

Agent detail loading:
- Trigger: getAgentDetail request initiated.
- Behavior: Show a loading indicator and disable actions that depend on agent data. Once a response is received, populate a shared agent state with the returned fields and hide the loading indicator.
- Error: If the interface call fails or returns incomplete critical data (such as missing officePhonePrimary), switch to an error display state and optionally allow back navigation.

Click primary call button:
- Trigger: User clicks the primary call-to-action button for calling the agent.
- Behavior: Use the current agent state’s officePhonePrimary to open a tel: link. The button is only enabled when agent details have successfully loaded.
- Edge: If officePhonePrimary is not available in the loaded data, do not render the primary call button.

Click secondary call link:
- Trigger: User clicks the secondary call link (if displayed).
- Behavior: Use the current agent state’s officePhoneSecondary to open a tel: link. The link appears only when officePhoneSecondary is present from getAgentDetail.

Consultation form input:
- Trigger: User types into the name, email, phone, or message fields.
- Behavior: Update local form state in real time, clear associated validation errors when the user modifies a previously invalid field.

Consultation form submission:
- Trigger: User clicks the form submit button.
- Behavior: Run client-side validation on fromName, fromEmail, message. If valid, call submitAgentConsultationRequest with agentId (from page state) and form values.
- While awaiting response: Disable the submit button and show a submitting indicator.
- On success (success = true): Show confirmation text using returned message, agentName, requestId, and createdAt if provided. Optionally hide or lock the form fields. The state changes to a submitted/success state.
- On failure (success = false or request error): Display an error message, keep existing form inputs so the user can modify and resubmit, change state back to idle to allow another submission.

Back to search results navigation:
- Trigger: User clicks the "Back to Search Results" link.
- Behavior: Use values from lastSearchZip, lastSearchRadius, lastSortBy (read from this page’s URL on initialization) to build a query string for agent_search.html: ?zip={lastSearchZip}&radiusMiles={lastSearchRadius}&sortBy={lastSortBy}. Navigate the browser to that URL. The link is only rendered when lastSearchZip (and/or other lastSearch* parameters) are present.

Error-handling interactions:
- Trigger: getAgentDetail fails or agentId is missing.
- Behavior: Show an error section explaining that the agent details could not be loaded and, if lastSearch* parameters are available, show a back-to-search link using them. Disable consultation submission and call buttons when agent data is not available.

URL-based refresh or direct navigation:
- Trigger: User refreshes the page or shares the URL including agentId and lastSearch* parameters.
- Behavior: On each load, the same URL parameters are read again, getAgentDetail is called again, and the back-to-search link is regenerated consistently without relying on localStorage or session state.
page_functionality.state_logic: The primary state for this page is derived from URL parameters and API responses rather than any local storage. On every page load, the application reads the query parameters: agentId, lastSearchZip, lastSearchRadius, and lastSortBy. If agentId is present, the page triggers getAgentDetail(agentId) and enters a 'loading' state. Once the response arrives, the page transitions to a 'loaded' state with an in-memory agent object populated with the returned fields (agentId, name, officePhonePrimary, officePhoneSecondary, email, officeAddressLine1, officeAddressLine2, officeCity, officeState, officeZip, serviceRadiusMiles, bio). If the call fails or agentId is missing, the page transitions to an 'error' state where agent-dependent components (call button, consultation form) are disabled or hidden. The presence of lastSearchZip, lastSearchRadius, and lastSortBy in the URL determines whether the 'Back to Search Results' link is shown and how its href is constructed. These lastSearch* parameters are not modified by the page; they are simply read and re-used when building the navigation URL to agent_search.html. The consultation form maintains its own local state (form field values, validation errors, and submission status) but does not persist beyond the current session and is reset if the page is reloaded. When the user submits the consultation form, submitAgentConsultationRequest is called using the current agentId from state and the current form values. Based on the response, the form's state transitions among 'idle', 'validating', 'submitting', 'success', and 'error'. No localStorage or sessionStorage is used; all persistent navigation context is entirely based on the URL parameters.

components.functionality: Initializes the page by reading URL parameters (agentId, lastSearchZip, lastSearchRadius, lastSortBy), triggers the getAgentDetail interface when agentId is present, and manages global loading and error states for agent data. It exposes the loaded agent object and lastSearch* values to child components.
Displays the agent’s name and optionally a short descriptor such as the city and state from the loaded Agent data. Only renders content when the agent-detail-loader indicates a successful load.

Shows the agent’s office address using officeAddressLine1, optional officeAddressLine2, officeCity, officeState, and officeZip from the Agent model. The component structures the address for easy reading and may expose it in a single-line format suitable for copying into map applications.

Displays a short textual summary of the agent’s service area if serviceRadiusMiles is available in the Agent data (for example, describing that the agent typically serves clients within that radius around the office address). If serviceRadiusMiles is null or undefined, this component remains hidden or displays a generic message based only on the office city and state.

Provides actionable contact options based on loaded agent data: a primary 'Call Office' button using officePhonePrimary, an optional secondary phone link using officePhoneSecondary, and an optional 'Email Agent' mailto link using the agent’s email if present. Buttons are disabled or hidden until agent data is loaded.

Renders a 'Back to Search Results' navigation link when lastSearchZip (and optionally lastSearchRadius and lastSortBy) are available from the URL. It constructs the href for agent_search.html with zip, radiusMiles, and sortBy query parameters mapped directly from lastSearchZip, lastSearchRadius, and lastSortBy. If lastSearchZip is not present, the link is hidden or omitted.
Displays the agent’s bio text from the bio field returned by getAgentDetail when available. If no bio is provided, the component remains hidden without showing placeholder text.

Collects user input for requesting a consultation with the agent, including fromName (required), fromEmail (required), fromPhone (optional), and message (required). Validates input fields, and on successful validation, calls submitAgentConsultationRequest with the current agentId from the loader state and the user’s inputs. Manages its own idle, validating, submitting, success, and error states and exposes them to the confirmation component.

Displays the outcome of the consultation request submission. When the consultation-request-form reports a success state, this component shows a confirmation message using data returned from submitAgentConsultationRequest, such as message, agentName, requestId, and createdAt. When an error occurs, it displays an error state with guidance to retry while preserving the user’s input in the form.

Shows an error banner when agent-detail-loader reports a failed getAgentDetail call or when agentId is missing from the URL. It can optionally include a 'Back to Search Results' action by reusing the same navigation logic as the back-to-search-link component if lastSearch* parameters exist.

layout.grid_system: Likely a 12-column fixed-width grid (approx. 1140–1200px) centered within the viewport, with the left 7–8 columns for main content and right 4–5 columns for the sidebar CTA/agent column.
layout.layout_pattern: Use a two-column desktop layout beneath the existing full-width top navigation. The left column serves as the main informational track, starting with a hero-style agent header, then flowing downward through address, service area summary, and a more detailed bio. The right column functions as a focused action sidebar, starting at the same vertical level as the agent header and containing the primary consultation request form at the top, followed by secondary contact action buttons. Status components (loader, error banner, confirmation) temporarily override or sit above these base components but do not change the underlying two-column structure.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Use a two-column desktop layout beneath the existing full-width top navigation. The left column serves as the main informational track, starting with a hero-style agent header, then flowing downward through address, service area summary, and a more detailed bio. The right column functions as a focused action sidebar, starting at the same vertical level as the agent header and containing the primary consultation request form at the top, followed by secondary contact action buttons. Status components (loader, error banner, confirmation) temporarily override or sit above these base components but do not change the underlying two-column structure.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Apply a 12‑column centered grid (~1140–1200px). Allocate roughly 7–8 columns (≈60–65%) to the left content column and 4–5 columns (≈35–40%) to the right sidebar. The top hero band (agent header + consultation form) should occupy roughly the upper 40% of the initial viewport on a typical desktop. Within the right sidebar, the consultation form/confirmation card should occupy the top ~40% of the sidebar height above the fold, with the stacked contact actions filling the remaining visible sidebar area and extending below the fold as needed.
layout_strategies.content_density: Maintain medium-low density: generous whitespace between sections (32–48px vertical spacing), comfortable internal padding (20–24px) in cards and form areas, and clear separation between stacked blocks. Text blocks (bio, address, service area description) should be readable and not cramped, while still presenting all necessary information on a single scroll view for typical desktop heights.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Preserve the F-shaped scanning pattern: users first scan the top navigation, then land on the large agent name and title at the top-left (hero header), then are pulled across to the right by a strong red primary CTA button inside the consultation request form card. From there, the eye moves down the left column through address and service area details to the agent bio, while secondary attention moves down the right through the outlined contact buttons. Error and success states temporarily intercept attention at the very top (error banner) or in the primary action area (form/confirmation), but the flow remains left hero → right primary CTA → down both columns.

layout.overall_description: Below the full-width navigation bar, the page content is centered in a fixed-width, two-column grid. At the very top-left of this content area, a subtle 'Back to search' link appears, followed immediately below by a hero-like agent header that fills the left 60–65% of the width. This header prominently features the agent’s name and role, visually anchoring the page. Aligned with the top of this header on the right is a white consultation request form card, floating on a light gray background and framed with a soft shadow; a bold red pill-shaped submit button inside this card becomes the primary call to action, mirroring the quote card from the original mockup. As users scroll, the left column continues downward in a clean vertical stack: first the agent’s address block, then a service area summary with a concise description and town/location chips, and finally a more text-heavy bio section. The right column continues with a column of large, pill-shaped outlined buttons offering direct contact actions (call, email, directions), each spanning the width of the sidebar and separated by comfortable vertical gaps. If a consultation is submitted, the form area is replaced in-place by a confirmation card of similar size and visual weight. If loading or errors occur, a centered loader overlay or a prominent full-width error banner just below the nav momentarily dominate attention. Overall, the layout feels modern and corporate: a structured two-column marketing/detail page with a strong top-left agent identity, a strong top-right action card, and a clear downward reading flow through supporting details.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: When active, the loader sits above all main content within the central grid area, visually centered both horizontally and vertically in the viewport. It appears as an overlay spanning roughly the full width of the content container (almost the full 12-column grid, leaving normal page side margins) and extends enough vertically to dim the underlying two-column layout. The loader indicator itself (spinner or progress element) is placed at the center of this overlay, with generous padding around it (at least 48px top and bottom) to create a calm, focused loading state. The underlying left/right columns remain in their positions but are visually subdued (e.g., slightly dimmed or blurred) to reinforce that interaction is temporarily disabled.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as a temporary overlay layer anchored to the main content region below the navigation; it is effectively fixed relative to the viewport while active, preventing interaction with the underlying two-column layout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The error banner appears directly beneath the global top navigation and spans the full width of the content container, visually cutting across both the left and right columns. It takes the form of a horizontal bar with a strong red background aligned with the grid’s left and right edges. The banner height is modest (roughly 56–72px, depending on message length), with 16–20px of vertical padding inside. There is a small vertical gap (about 16px) between the bottom of this banner and the first in-page element (the back-to-search link), so the banner reads as a distinct layer of feedback above the standard content. All other components (back link, header, columns) are pushed downward when the banner is present.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, scrolls with the page as part of the normal document flow; always sits at the top of the content area below the navigation bar when rendered.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The back-to-search link is positioned at the top-left of the main content area, aligned with the left edge of the left column. It appears as a small, understated text link with an accompanying left arrow icon, occupying only a single line of text and approximately 10–15% of the content width. There is a comfortable margin (about 24px) below this link before the agent header begins, and a smaller gap (around 16px) above it relative to the error banner (if present). It helps orient users without competing visually with the hero header’s typography or the primary CTA.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard inline element within the left column’s flow; scrolls away with content and does not remain fixed.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The agent header sits just below the back-to-search link, aligned flush with the left margin of the content grid and occupying the full width of the left column (about 7–8 of 12 grid columns). Vertically, it forms the left half of the hero row and is aligned at its top edge with the top of the consultation request form card on the right, establishing a strong horizontal baseline. Inside this header area, the agent name is the dominant element: a large, bold headline in the brand’s dark text color, spanning roughly 70–80% of the left column width. Directly beneath the name is a secondary line with the agent’s title and possibly agency name, set in smaller type. A circular headshot can be placed either to the left of the text stack or above it, but still within this left column block, with consistent internal spacing of 16–24px around all elements. The overall header block height takes up roughly 20–30% of the visible viewport height on desktop, leaving generous whitespace below before the subsequent address and summary sections.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Anchored at the top of the left column as part of the standard scrolling content; it defines the starting vertical alignment for the hero row but does not remain sticky.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The consultation request form occupies the top of the right sidebar column and is designed as a prominent card that visually mirrors the quote card in the original mockup. It is aligned so that its top edge sits on the same horizontal line as the top of the agent header on the left, occupying the full width of the right column (about 4–5 of 12 grid columns). The card sits on a subtly off-white or light gray page background with a white interior, rounded corners, and a very light shadow. Inside, there is generous internal padding of about 24px on all sides. At the top of the card, a short, bold title (e.g., 'Request a Consultation') spans most of the card width, followed by a vertical stack of compact form fields (name, contact info, preferred time, etc.) with 12–16px vertical spacing between them. At the bottom of this stack is a large, pill-shaped red submit button spanning nearly the full width of the card, with 12–16px vertical padding and 24–32px horizontal padding, acting as the visual focal point. The overall card height is tuned so that, on a typical desktop, the entire form including the button is visible without scrolling, occupying roughly the top 40% of the sidebar height above the fold.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard static card within the right column’s flow; aligns top with the agent header and scrolls with the page. When the confirmation is shown, this form is visually replaced in-place by the confirmation content within the same card footprint.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The agent contact actions panel sits in the right column directly below the consultation request form (or confirmation card), separated by a vertical gap of approximately 24–32px to clearly distinguish it as a secondary but related section. It spans the full width of the right column. Inside this panel, individual contact actions (such as 'Call', 'Email', 'Get Directions') are presented as large, pill-shaped outline buttons stacked vertically. Each button nearly fills the width of the sidebar, with 12–16px vertical padding and 24–32px horizontal padding, and has a red border with subtle hover effects. Buttons are separated by consistent vertical spacing (16–20px) so that each action is easily scannable and clickable. If there are more actions than fit above the fold, they gracefully extend downward; the section does not need to be constrained to a fixed height.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Flows naturally below the primary sidebar card within the right column; scrolls with the page and does not stick. It maintains alignment with the right grid margin at all times.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Upon successful submission, the consultation request confirmation appears in the exact footprint previously occupied by the consultation request form card at the top of the right column. The card maintains the same width (full right column) and outer styling (white background, rounded corners, light shadow, ~24px padding). Inside, the form fields are replaced by a compact success icon or checkmark aligned toward the top-left, a bold confirmation headline (e.g., 'Consultation Request Sent'), and a short explanatory paragraph. Additional secondary text (like expected response time) appears below in smaller type. The red pill-shaped button area may be reused either as a subtle 'Done' or 'Back to agent details' button, or omitted entirely if not needed, but the card’s vertical height remains similar to the form so that the overall hero composition (agent header left, card right) stays visually balanced.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Occupies the same static position as the consultation form within the right column, effectively replacing its content within the same card. It scrolls with the page and is not sticky.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The agent address block appears in the left column directly beneath the agent header, with a vertical gap of about 24–32px to separate it from the hero text. It spans the full width of the left column and is presented either as a lightly framed block (e.g., subtle top border) or as clean stacked text to maintain the page’s minimal, corporate feel. The content is laid out as a short label or subheading (e.g., 'Office Location') followed by the address lines, each on its own line. A compact action (like 'View on Map') may appear as a small, right-aligned text link or icon within this block. Internal vertical spacing between the label and the first address line is about 8–12px, keeping the block compact but readable. The overall height of this block is relatively modest, so the user can see both it and the beginning of the service area section without needing to scroll much.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block-level component in the left column’s vertical flow; it sits between the agent header above and the service area summary below and scrolls away normally.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The service area summary is located in the left column just below the agent address block, separated by approximately 24px of vertical space. It occupies the full width of the left column and serves as a structured, slightly more visual section. At the top of this block is a concise subheading (e.g., 'Service Areas') in a slightly emphasized style. Beneath this heading is a brief one- or two-line description explaining the types of coverage or regions served, followed by a grid of pill-shaped location chips similar to those in the original mockup. These chips are arranged in multiple rows, left-aligned, with each chip having horizontal padding of 16–24px and vertical padding of 8–12px, and consistent horizontal and vertical gaps of about 8–12px between them. The overall section height can range from one to several rows of chips depending on the number of locations but preserves ample white space above and below the chip grid so it doesn’t feel crowded.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static informational section in the left column’s flow; it sits between the address block and the bio section and scrolls normally. The chip grid wraps responsively within the left column width.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The agent bio section sits directly below the service area summary in the left column, with a vertical gap of about 32px to clearly delineate it as a new content block.
