It spans the full width of the left column and is somewhat more text-dense than the sections above. At the top, a subheading such as 'About [Agent Name]' appears, followed by one or more short paragraphs of biography text laid out in a single column. Line length is comfortable (since it’s constrained to the 7–8-column left width), and line spacing is slightly generous to preserve the medium-low density feel. If additional elements such as bullet points (e.g., certifications, specialties) are present, they appear beneath the introductory paragraph with 8–12px vertical spacing between items. The section extends downward as needed, typically occupying the majority of the remaining left-column space on the initial viewport and continuing below the fold if content is long.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard scrolling section anchored in the left column; it forms the primary body content area and scrolls naturally beneath the hero and summary sections.

name: Help Center / FAQs
title: Help Center / FAQs
description: Help Center page where users can search and browse support and FAQ articles, then open detailed help articles.

core_features: Search form that lets users submit a text query to find help articles using the searchHelpArticles interface.
Display of search results grouped as a list of help articles returned by searchHelpArticles, each linking to the full article page via slug.
Help center overview view that shows introductory text and categorized topics with featured articles using getHelpCenterOverview when no search query is active.
Navigation from the Help Center to individual help articles (help_article.html) using the article slug, and back to the home page (index.html) via header/footer links.
URL-driven state using the query parameter so that searches can be linked to directly and revisited via browser navigation.
user_workflows:
Workflow: Arrive on Help Center without a search query
1. User navigates to help_center.html via the site header or footer.
2. On page load, the controller reads the URL and detects that there is no query parameter or it is empty.
3. The controller calls getHelpCenterOverview.
4. The overview topics and featured articles from getHelpCenterOverview are rendered for the user to browse.
5. The user can click any article title to navigate to help_article.html with the appropriate slug parameter.
6. The user can use the search form at any time; submitting the form updates the URL to include query and switches the view to search results.

Workflow: Arrive on Help Center with a search query (from home page or direct link)
1. User arrives on help_center.html with a query parameter in the URL.
2. On page load, the controller reads the query parameter value and sets it as the current search term.
3. The controller calls searchHelpArticles with the query value.
4. When results are returned, the page displays the search results list and shows the query in the search input.
5. The user can click any search result to navigate to help_article.html with the selected article’s slug.
6. The user can modify the search term in the input and resubmit; the URL query parameter and results are updated accordingly.
7. If the user clears the search term and submits an empty query, the controller removes the query parameter from the URL and switches back to the overview view (using getHelpCenterOverview).

Workflow: Search refinement within the Help Center
1. User is viewing search results for a particular query.
2. User edits the text in the search input (e.g., to narrow or broaden the query).
3. On submit, the controller updates the query parameter in the URL to match the new term.
4. The controller calls searchHelpArticles with the updated query.
5. The search results list is refreshed with the new results.
6. Browser back/forward navigation moves between prior queries by changing the URL; the controller reacts to URL changes and automatically re-runs searchHelpArticles with the historical query values.

Workflow: Browse topics and featured articles
1. User arrives on help_center.html without a query parameter.
2. The controller calls getHelpCenterOverview and receives introductory content plus a list of topics, each with a topic code, label, description, and featuredArticles list.
3. The page displays the overview text and renders one section per topic.
4. Within each topic section, a list of featured articles is displayed based on the featuredArticles data.
5. User clicks a featured article title; the browser navigates to help_article.html with the article’s slug in the URL.
6. If the user navigates back from the article page, the browser returns to help_center.html without a query parameter, so the overview view is shown again.

Workflow: Navigate between Help Center and other site pages
1. While on help_center.html, user clicks the site logo or a dedicated link to go back to the home page.
2. Browser navigates to index.html.
3. If index.html includes a help search field that links to help_center.html with a query parameter, submitting that form sends the user back to help_center.html?query=..., where the controller runs searchHelpArticles for that query.
4. User can repeat navigation as needed; all state is driven by the URL and re-initialized on each load.
interactions:
Interaction: Search input typing
- User types in the search input.
- The component updates its internal input value but does not call searchHelpArticles until the form is submitted.
- The current value is used to construct the query parameter on submit.

Interaction: Submit search form
- Trigger: User presses the search button or presses Enter in the search input.
- The form submission handler prevents default browser form submission.
- If the trimmed input is non-empty, the handler updates the browser URL to help_center.html with query set to the entered value.
- If the input is empty, the handler updates the browser URL to help_center.html without the query parameter.
- The page controller reacts to the URL change: if query is present, it calls searchHelpArticles with that value; if not, it calls getHelpCenterOverview.

Interaction: Click a search result article
- Trigger: User clicks on a search result list item (e.g., article title or 'Read more' action).
- The click handler reads the slug field from that result.
- Browser navigates to help_article.html?slug=<slug> using standard link navigation.
- No Help Center interface is called as part of the click; the next page handles its own data fetch.

Interaction: Click a featured article in overview
- Trigger: User clicks on a featured article within a topic section in the overview view.
- The click handler reads the slug of the selected featured article from getHelpCenterOverview response data.
- Browser navigates to help_article.html?slug=<slug>.

Interaction: Click header or footer Help & FAQs link (from other pages)
- Trigger: User clicks a Help & FAQs link in the site header or footer from any page.
- Browser navigates to help_center.html without a query parameter.
- On load, the controller detects no query and calls getHelpCenterOverview.

Interaction: Click logo or Back to Home link
- Trigger: User clicks the logo or a back-to-home link on help_center.html.
- Browser navigates to index.html with no additional parameters from this page.
- No Help Center interfaces are called as part of this interaction.

Interaction: Browser back/forward navigation
- Trigger: User presses back or forward in the browser while on help_center.html or after navigating away and back.
- The page listens for URL changes (e.g., popstate events) and re-reads the query parameter.
- If query is present, the controller calls searchHelpArticles with that value and re-renders search results.
- If query is not present, the controller calls getHelpCenterOverview and re-renders the overview view.
page_functionality.state_logic: The main state of the page is controlled by the query URL parameter. On every initial load and on any URL change: (1) The controller reads the query parameter from the URL. (2) If query exists and is not an empty string, the page is in 'search mode': the current search term is set to query; searchHelpArticles is called with this query value; the search results component is shown; the search form input is initialized to this value; the overview topics may optionally still be loaded or hidden, but the primary focus is the search results. (3) If query does not exist or is an empty string, the page is in 'overview mode': getHelpCenterOverview is called; the overview topics and featured articles are displayed; no searchHelpArticles call is made. (4) When the user submits a search via the search form, the handler updates the URL (adding or updating the query parameter if the input is non-empty, or removing it if empty). This URL change re-triggers the same state logic. (5) Because the current search term and mode are derived exclusively from the URL query parameter, browser back/forward navigation between different searches or between search and overview automatically restores the correct state without any localStorage or other client-side persistence.

components.functionality: Central controller that initializes and manages the Help Center page state based on the URL query parameter. It decides whether to show the overview view or search results view, calls getHelpCenterOverview or searchHelpArticles accordingly, and passes data down to child components.

Provides a single-line text input and a submit action for users to search Help Center articles. It reads the current query parameter to pre-fill the input when in search mode and updates the query parameter in the URL when the form is submitted.

Displays search results returned by the searchHelpArticles interface for the current query. Each result shows the article title and excerpt and includes a link to the full article. It also handles the 'no results' state when the results array is empty.

Renders the Help Center overview when there is no active search query. It uses the getHelpCenterOverview response to display introductory text and then iterates over the topics array, rendering a section for each topic with its label, description, and a list of featured articles.

Sub-component used by help-overview-topics. For a single topic entry in the topics array from getHelpCenterOverview, it displays the topic label and description, and then lists its featuredArticles, which the user can click to open the full article page.

Shows contextual information when there are no search results for the current query, or when an error prevents searchHelpArticles from returning results. It can suggest adjusting the search term and provides a link or action to clear the search and return to the overview view.

Provides navigation elements relevant to the Help Center page, including the site logo and a link to return to the home page. It also can include the Help & FAQs link to indicate the current page in the global navigation context.

Provides footer-level navigation including a Help & FAQs link (which routes to help_center.html without a query parameter) and other global links. On this page, the Help & FAQs link routes to the same URL so users can reset to the overview mode.

layout.grid_system: Likely a 12-column fixed-width grid (approx. 1140–1200px) centered within the viewport, with the left 7–8 columns for main content and right 4–5 columns for the sidebar CTA/agent column.
layout.layout_pattern: Use a full-width top navigation bar, followed by a centered fixed-width content container built on a 12-column grid. Inside this container, maintain a two-column layout: the left column is the primary help area (search, results, and article content) and the right column is a secondary support column (overview topics / quick links). Content in each column flows linearly from top to bottom, with clear section breaks and generous spacing. Conditional components (results, topic detail, empty state) all occupy the same primary content zone beneath the search form, controlled by state rather than layout changes.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Use a full-width top navigation bar, followed by a centered fixed-width content container built on a 12-column grid. Inside this container, maintain a two-column layout: the left column is the primary help area (search, results, and article content) and the right column is a secondary support column (overview topics / quick links). Content in each column flows linearly from top to bottom, with clear section breaks and generous spacing. Conditional components (results, topic detail, empty state) all occupy the same primary content zone beneath the search form, controlled by state rather than layout changes.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Within the central content container (approximately 1140–1200px wide), allocate roughly 60–65% of the width to the left main column and 35–40% to the right sidebar column, echoing the mockup. Vertically, the initial help ‘hero’ band (search form in the left column, topics overview card in the right column) occupies the upper ~40% of the viewport on desktop. Below that, search results or article content extend down the left, while the right sidebar remains shorter and visually secondary. Maintain 24–32px vertical gaps between stacked blocks and 16–24px internal padding for cards and key components.
layout_strategies.content_density: Maintain medium-low density: a clear, functional help experience with generous whitespace. The search form and each content block sit in their own visually distinct areas with ample breathing room. Lists (topics, results, article sections) are not crowded; each item has enough padding and separation to scan comfortably. The page shows useful information and multiple interactive elements at once, but avoids feeling text-heavy or cramped by using consistent spacing and clear typographic hierarchy.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Replicate the F-shaped scanning pattern: first, the eye runs across the full-width header navigation (brand on the left, key links on the right). Then it drops to the main hero band where a strong left-aligned help headline and search field draw attention. From there, attention is pulled to the right sidebar card of overview topics. The scan then proceeds down the left column through either search results or article content, with occasional glances to the right column when users look for alternative paths (browsing topics). The flow ends near the bottom with the footer navigation stretching across the page.

layout.overall_description: The Help Center page is structured as a modern, two-column support hub beneath a full-width red-accented navigation bar. A centered content container, about 1140–1200px wide, sits within a light gray or white background, divided into a wide left main column and a narrower right sidebar. At the top of the left column, a prominent help headline and search form create a hero-like support entry point, mirroring the marketing hero in the original mockup but focused on finding answers instead of requesting a quote. To the right, a stacked card of help topics acts as a secondary focal point, analogous to the quote/CTA card in the mockup. As users scroll, the left column becomes a vertical reading zone for search results and article content, while the right column remains a stable reference panel of topics. Generous margins, pill-shaped buttons, subtle card shadows, and consistent 8px-based spacing unify the layout, creating a trustworthy, corporate support experience where users can either search, browse topics, or drill into detailed FAQ articles without ever feeling visually overwhelmed.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This is a non-visual, structural component that conceptually sits behind the entire Help Center layout. It does not render any visible UI but governs which primary content appears in the left main column: the default overview state (search + topics), the search results state (search + results or empty state), or the article view state (search + topic section). Spatially, it occupies no physical space on the page; instead, it determines whether the help-search-results, help-topic-section, or help-empty-state component is mounted in the main content zone beneath the help-search-form. From a mental model perspective, imagine it as an invisible switchboard controlling the left column’s content area between the hero band and the footer.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual state manager that does not occupy layout space; it dynamically shows or hides other components in the left main column based on user actions (searching, selecting a topic or article).

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The header navigation is a full-width horizontal bar anchored to the top of the viewport. It spans 100% of the page width and sits on a solid or subtly gradient red background that establishes brand presence. Within it, content is centered in the same fixed-width container as the rest of the page (approx. 1140–1200px). The logo appears on the far left, followed by primary navigation links aligned to the right in a single row. Vertical height is moderate (about 64–80px) with 16–24px of vertical padding. There is clear spacing above the start of the main content: the help-search-form and right sidebar content begin around 24–32px below the bottom edge of the nav, preserving the mockup’s spacious separation between nav and hero area.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard top-of-page bar; typically remains at the top of the document flow. It can be implemented as sticky on scroll if desired, but the layout does not depend on it being fixed.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The help-search-form occupies the top portion of the left main column, acting as the hero of the Help Center page. It sits directly beneath the header navigation, inside the centered content container, aligned to the left. Horizontally, it spans roughly 60–65% of the container width (about 7–8 of 12 grid columns). Within this area, a large, bold heading such as “How can we help with your boat insurance?” is left-aligned at the very top, with a short explanatory subtitle beneath it (spacing about 8–16px). Below the text, the primary search input stretches across most of the left column (around 70–80% of the left column width), with pill-shaped edges and a clearly visible red-accented search button either integrated on the right side of the field or immediately adjacent. The entire search block has generous padding (around 24px) and ample whitespace above and below: approximately 32–48px of vertical space separates the bottom of this hero search area from the next content block in the left column (results, empty state, or topic section). This arrangement mirrors the mockup’s large hero headline and supporting copy before the interactive chips, but replaces location chips with the search bar as the primary interaction.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block at the top of the left column; it remains in place as the user scrolls the page content beneath it. On very small screens, it naturally expands to full width above other content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The help-search-results component appears directly below the help-search-form in the left main column when a search has been performed and matching content exists. It aligns to the same left margin as the search form and spans the same left column width (about 60–65% of the overall page container, 7–8 grid columns). The top of this component begins about 24–32px below the bottom of the search form, maintaining a clear but connected relationship. The results are presented in a vertical list of cards or rows, each with 16–20px of vertical padding and 12–16px of spacing between items, keeping density comfortable. Each result title is left-aligned, with a short summary and possibly a small category label. The list can extend downward as needed, forming the main scrollable content zone of the left column. There is additional vertical padding (around 32px) at the bottom of the list before any subsequent elements or before the footer navigation comes into view. This placement follows the mockup’s pattern where, after the hero content, the main informative content continues down the left side in a linear, scannable fashion.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Appears and disappears conditionally in the left column depending on user searches; scrolls naturally with the page as a long vertical list. It replaces the help-empty-state in this area when results are found.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The help-overview-topics component anchors the top of the right sidebar column, mirroring the quote/CTA card position in the original mockup. It begins at the same vertical level as the help-search-form hero area, but occupies the right 35–40% of the content container (around 4–5 of 12 grid columns), aligned to the right edge. This component is styled as one or more stacked white cards set against a slightly off-white or light gray page background, with subtle shadow and 20–24px internal padding. At the top of the main card, a concise heading such as “Browse Help Topics” appears, with a list of topic groups underneath, each on its own line or in compact pill/chip form, with 8–12px vertical spacing between them. The overall height of this card in the hero band is roughly similar to the combined height of the left search heading and input, so that the top of the page forms a visually balanced band across both columns. Beneath this card, there is 24–32px of vertical spacing before any additional sidebar content (if present in the design system). Across the page, this card serves as the right-side attractor in the F-pattern, providing an alternate entry route for users who prefer browsing over searching.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static sidebar block pinned to the right column; it scrolls with the page as users move down through results or article content in the left column. On narrow screens, it stacks below the search form and above results, spanning full width.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The help-topic-section component is the detailed article or FAQ view that occupies the primary content area of the left column when the user selects a topic or specific help item. It sits in the same left column position as the search results: directly below the help-search-form, starting about 24–32px beneath it, aligned to the same left margin, and spanning the same 60–65% width of the container. When active, it typically replaces the help-search-results (the state-controller decides whether results or article content is rendered here). The topic-section content is structured like a well-padded article card: the top includes the article title and possibly a small breadcrumb or topic label; below that, body text, subsections, and any inline lists or FAQs flow vertically with 16–24px spacing between paragraphs and sections. The width is kept consistent so that lines of text remain readable (no full-bleed width). Vertical margins at the top and bottom follow the same 24–32px rhythm used for other blocks. This mirrors the mockup’s lower content area where more detailed supportive information and imagery appear beneath the initial hero section, but here focused purely on textual help content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Conditionally occupies the main left-column content region beneath the search form; scrolls vertically as a long-form article. It typically replaces the help-search-results or empty state in this zone when an article is selected.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The help-empty-state component is an informational block that appears in the left column when a search is performed but no results are found, or when the system wants to prompt the user to try a different search. It is positioned exactly where the help-search-results or help-topic-section would normally sit: directly below the help-search-form, starting about 24–32px beneath it, and aligned to the same left margin with the same left column width (roughly 60–65% of the container). The empty state is presented as a clean, centered message within a white or very light card, with 20–24px internal padding and possibly a subtle icon at the top. The main message text is centered horizontally within the card, but the card itself is left-aligned, keeping consistency with the main content column. Below the message, there may be a pill-shaped button suggesting actions (e.g., ‘Browse Topics’), visually echoing the primary CTAs from the mockup. The space around this component is generous, with at least 32px of vertical breathing room below it before the footer region, to avoid it feeling like an error and instead like a helpful guide.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Appears only when there are no search results; it replaces the results list or article view in the same left-column content slot. It scrolls with the page and does not affect the right sidebar positioning.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The footer navigation spans the full width of the page at the bottom, mirroring the structural role of the header but with lower visual weight. It sits below all main content from both columns, separated by a generous vertical margin (around 48–64px) to clearly mark the end of the Help Center content. The footer background is a light gray or muted tone that contrasts softly with the main content area, and the links are organized within the same centered 1140–1200px container. Content within the footer is arranged in one or two horizontal rows of links (e.g., Support, Privacy Policy, Contact, etc.), left-aligned or distributed, with 16–24px vertical padding. This wide strip visually anchors the page and closes the F-shaped scanning path with a stable horizontal line across the bottom of the viewport.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block at the bottom of the page content; it appears after users scroll past the left-column content and right sidebar topics. It spans full width and does not float or stick.

name: Help Article
title: Help Article
description: Displays a single help article based on a URL parameter (slug or articleId), including any eligibility metadata such as maximum boat length for online quotes, and provides navigation back to the Help Center, the homepage, and a contextual Start a Quote link when applicable.

core_features: Resolve which help article to display by reading the slug or articleId URL parameter and normalizing them into a single effective slug.
Fetch the selected article’s full data (title, content, topic, maxOnlineQuoteBoatLengthFeet) using the getHelpArticleBySlug interface.
Display the article title and body content returned from the API without hardcoding any article-specific text or rules on the page.

When the article includes eligibility metadata (e.g., maxOnlineQuoteBoatLengthFeet) or has an eligibility-related topic, display a dedicated eligibility section that highlights those rules based solely on API data.
Show a contextual Start a Quote navigation link for eligibility-related articles, navigating to the quote_boat_details.html page with the required entrySource URL parameter.
Provide persistent navigation controls: a Back to Help Center link to help_center.html and a Home link or clickable site logo to index.html.
Handle loading and error states for the article fetch, including cases where required URL parameters are missing or the article cannot be retrieved, using generic, implementation-defined messaging.
Allow direct linking/bookmarking to specific articles using either slug or articleId, and update the displayed article when the URL parameters change without reloading the entire site shell.
user_workflows:
View a help article from the Help Center:
1) User clicks an article in help_center.html; that page navigates to help_article.html with a slug parameter.
2) On page load, the Help Article page reads the slug from the URL, normalizes it as the effective slug, and calls getHelpArticleBySlug.
3) The page shows a loading state while waiting for the API.
4) Once data is returned, the page displays the article title and content.
5) If the article has eligibility metadata, the eligibility section becomes visible and shows the rules based on the returned fields.
6) The user can click Back to Help Center to return to help_center.html.

Open a legal or informational page from the footer:
1) User clicks a footer link (for example, a link that sets articleId in the URL such as a legal or informational page).
2) The browser navigates to help_article.html with articleId set as a query parameter.
3) The Help Article page resolves articleId as the effective slug and calls getHelpArticleBySlug with that value.
4) After loading, the title and content from the API are displayed as the article.
5) The user can navigate to Home via the site logo or a Home link at any time.

Start an online quote from an eligibility article:
1) User opens an eligibility-related article (via Help Center, footer, or a direct deep link) that returns eligibility metadata such as maxOnlineQuoteBoatLengthFeet.
2) The Help Article page detects via API data that the article is eligibility-related and renders an eligibility section and a Start a Quote link.
3) The user reviews the eligibility rules shown from the article data.
4) The user clicks the Start a Quote link.
5) The page navigates to quote_boat_details.html with the entrySource URL parameter set according to the defined navigation rules (e.g., entrySource=coverage_overview_page).

Switch between different help articles via URL changes:
1) A user or an internal link changes the current URL to help_article.html with a different slug or articleId.
2) The page detects the URL parameter change and determines the new effective slug.
3) The page calls getHelpArticleBySlug again with the new slug.
4) While loading, the previous article can be hidden or replaced with a loading state, depending on implementation.
5) Once the new article data is returned, the title, content, and eligibility section (if applicable) update to reflect the new article.
interactions: On initial page load, the page reads the slug and articleId URL parameters and triggers the article fetch via getHelpArticleBySlug using the resolved effective slug.
When the user clicks the Back to Help Center link, the browser navigates to help_center.html without additional parameters.
When the user clicks the Home link or site logo, the browser navigates to index.html without additional parameters.

When the user clicks the Start a Quote link in an eligibility-related article, the browser navigates to quote_boat_details.html with the entrySource URL parameter set as specified (e.g., entrySource=coverage_overview_page).

If the URL slug or articleId parameter changes while the user is on the page (for example, via client-side routing or an internal link that stays on help_article.html), the page reacts by re-resolving the effective slug and re-calling getHelpArticleBySlug to update the displayed article.

If getHelpArticleBySlug returns an error or an unpublished/nonexistent article, the page displays a generic error or fallback state (implementation-defined) and keeps navigation links (Home, Back to Help Center) available.

If the article returned by getHelpArticleBySlug includes maxOnlineQuoteBoatLengthFeet or has an eligibility-related topic, the page enables visibility of the eligibility section and the Start a Quote CTA; if not, those elements remain hidden or disabled.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The primary state of the Help Article page is derived from URL query parameters and the result of the getHelpArticleBySlug API call.

1) URL parameter resolution:
- The page reads two optional query parameters: slug and articleId.
- If both are present, a deterministic rule must be applied; for example, slug can be given precedence and articleId ignored, or vice versa. The selected value becomes the effectiveSlug.
- If only one of slug or articleId is present, that parameter value becomes the effectiveSlug.
- If neither is present, the page cannot determine which article to load and should show a generic error or guidance state until a valid URL is used.

2) Data fetching state:
- On page initialization and whenever effectiveSlug changes, the page calls getHelpArticleBySlug with effectiveSlug.
- While awaiting a response, the page enters a loading state, during which core content components display loading indicators or placeholders.
- On successful response, the page stores the returned article data (helpArticleId, slug, title, content, topic, maxOnlineQuoteBoatLengthFeet) in in-memory state (e.g., a page-level store or component-level state) and leaves the loading state.
- On failure (e.g., network error, article not found, article not published), the page sets an error state and renders a non-article error view while keeping global navigation accessible.

3) Derived UI state from article data:
- The visibility of the eligibility section is derived purely from the fetched article data. It becomes visible only if the article’s topic and/or maxOnlineQuoteBoatLengthFeet indicate that the article contains eligibility information. The logic must not rely on hardcoded slugs or hardcoded business thresholds; it should rely only on the fields returned by getHelpArticleBySlug.
- The visibility of the Start a Quote link is derived from the same eligibility-related conditions as the eligibility section.
- Any displayed eligibility values (such as maximum boat length for online quotes) are read directly from maxOnlineQuoteBoatLengthFeet and are not hardcoded.

4) Navigation state:
- Clicking Back to Help Center replaces the current URL with help_center.html, thus leaving the Help Article page.
- Clicking Home replaces the current URL with index.html.
- Clicking Start a Quote navigates to quote_boat_details.html with a fixed entrySource URL parameter value as defined by the application’s navigation rules.

All state transitions are driven by URL changes and API responses; no persistent state (such as localStorage) is used for determining which article to show or how to render eligibility rules.

components.functionality: Reads the slug and articleId query parameters from the current URL, applies precedence rules to derive a single effectiveSlug value, and exposes that value to downstream components responsible for data fetching.
event_handlers: onPageLoad: Parse current URL for slug and articleId, determine effectiveSlug, and emit a routeResolved event with effectiveSlug.
onUrlChange: When the URL changes while staying on help_article.html, re-parse parameters, recompute effectiveSlug, and emit a routeResolved event with the new value.

components.functionality: Consumes effectiveSlug from the route resolver and uses getHelpArticleBySlug to retrieve the corresponding help article data, managing loading, success, and error states.
event_handlers: onRouteResolved: When a routeResolved event with effectiveSlug is received, call getHelpArticleBySlug({ slug: effectiveSlug }) and transition to a loading state.
onFetchSuccess: When getHelpArticleBySlug resolves successfully, store the returned article object in component state, clear any previous error, and emit an articleLoaded event with the article data.
onFetchError: When getHelpArticleBySlug fails or returns invalid data, set an error state (e.g., articleNotFound or fetchError), clear any previous article data, and emit an articleLoadFailed event.

components.functionality: Displays the article title and optional contextual information such as the article topic, once article data has been loaded.
event_handlers: onArticleLoaded: Receive the article data and render the article.title. Optionally render topic-based labels or breadcrumbs derived from article.topic. If the article fails to load, hide the header or show an error-friendly header state.

components.functionality: Renders the main article body using the content field returned from getHelpArticleBySlug, without hardcoded text or formatting rules tied to specific articles.
event_handlers: onArticleLoaded: Receive article.content and render it as structured content (e.g., HTML or rich text) according to the application’s rendering engine. If content is empty or absent, display a generic fallback state indicating no content is available.
onArticleLoadFailed: Clear any existing content and switch to an error or empty state appropriate for failed loads.

components.functionality: Conditionally displays eligibility-related information extracted from the article, such as maximum boat length for online quotes, driven entirely by the article’s topic and maxOnlineQuoteBoatLengthFeet values.
event_handlers: onArticleLoaded: Inspect the article’s topic and maxOnlineQuoteBoatLengthFeet. If the data indicates that the article describes eligibility rules (for example, if topic is an eligibility-related value or maxOnlineQuoteBoatLengthFeet is present), then show the panel and populate it with the eligibility information from the article. If not, hide the panel.
onArticleLoadFailed: Hide the eligibility panel and clear any previously shown eligibility information.

components.functionality: Provides a Start a Quote link that appears only when the current article is eligibility-related, and navigates users to the quote_boat_details.html page with the appropriate entrySource parameter.
event_handlers: onArticleLoaded: Determine whether the article qualifies as eligibility-related (for example, based on topic or presence of maxOnlineQuoteBoatLengthFeet). If yes, render an active Start a Quote link; if not, keep the link hidden.

onClick: When the Start a Quote link is clicked, navigate to quote_boat_details.html with the entrySource URL parameter set according to the defined navigation rules (e.g., entrySource=coverage_overview_page). The handler does not use any hardcoded eligibility values and does not modify quote data directly; it only controls navigation.

components.functionality: Provides navigation controls tailored to the Help Article context, including Back to Help Center, Home (or clickable logo), and optional indicators of the current article.
event_handlers: onClickBackToHelpCenter: Navigate the browser to help_center.html, clearing any Help Article–specific query parameters.
onClickHomeOrLogo: Navigate the browser to index.html, clearing any Help Article–specific query parameters.
onArticleLoaded: Optionally use the article title to highlight the current context in navigation (for example, showing the article title in a breadcrumb), without altering navigation destinations.

components.functionality: Manages and displays loading and error states for the article view so users understand whether the article is being fetched, has loaded, or failed to load.
event_handlers: onFetchStart: When article-data-fetcher initiates getHelpArticleBySlug, show a loading indicator and temporarily hide or dim the article-header, article-body, eligibility-rules-panel, and contextual-quote-link as appropriate.
onArticleLoaded: Hide the loading indicator and ensure all content components render the new article data.
onArticleLoadFailed: Hide the loading indicator, show a generic error state (e.g., message and retry guidance), and keep Back to Help Center and Home navigation accessible.

layout.grid_system: Likely a 12-column fixed-width grid (approx. 1140–1200px) centered within the viewport, with the left 7–8 columns for main content and right 4–5 columns for the sidebar CTA/agent column.
layout.layout_pattern: Use a full-width top navigation bar, then a centered fixed-width content container (approx. 1140–1200px) split into two columns: a wider left column for the article (header + body) and a narrower right sidebar for contextual quote actions and eligibility information. Within each column, components are stacked vertically in a clear, linear, top-to-bottom order with consistent gaps.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Use a full-width top navigation bar, then a centered fixed-width content container (approx. 1140–1200px) split into two columns: a wider left column for the article (header + body) and a narrower right sidebar for contextual quote actions and eligibility information. Within each column, components are stacked vertically in a clear, linear, top-to-bottom order with consistent gaps.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Inside the main content container, allocate roughly 60–65% of the width to the left column (article content) and 35–40% to the right sidebar (quote and eligibility panels). Vertically, the article header (left) and contextual quote card (right) together form a hero-like top band occupying the upper third to half of the initial viewport, with the article body extending downward on the left and sidebar panels stepping down on the right.
layout_strategies.content_density: Maintain a medium-low density: generous white space around the article header, cards, and CTAs; 24–32px vertical gaps between stacked sections; 20–24px padding inside cards; and content lines kept to comfortable lengths. The article body can be text-heavy but broken by headings, lists, and panels, with plenty of breathing room to avoid visual clutter.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Reinforce an F-shaped reading pattern: the eye moves left-to-right across the top navigation, down to the prominent article title in the left column, then across to the bold red contextual quote CTA in the right sidebar. From there, attention drops down the left through the article body while periodically catching on the right-hand sidebar cards (eligibility panel), finishing lower on the page where the article content ends.

layout.overall_description: The page opens with a full-width help navigation bar anchored at the very top, using the brand’s red accents and a flat, modern treatment. Below it, a centered content container spans roughly 1140–1200px, divided into a wide left article column and a narrower right sidebar. At the top of the left column, a bold article title and brief meta text form a hero-style header with generous top and bottom padding. At the same vertical level on the right, a prominent white card on a light gray background holds a large red pill-shaped “Start a Quote” or equivalent contextual quote button, visually echoing the primary CTA from the marketing mockup. As users scroll, the left column flows into the article body, rendered with clear headings and comfortable line lengths, while the right column shows a stacked set of compact cards: eligibility details in a subtle info panel beneath the quote card and any additional contextual messaging below. Vertical spacing between each block is consistent (about 24–32px), keeping the layout clean and breathable. The overall impression is a modern, corporate, two-column help article page where the main content is on the left and supportive quote and eligibility information is neatly framed on the right, guiding users smoothly from understanding to action.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This component operates behind the scenes and does not occupy visible space in the layout. Conceptually, it sits at the top of the page logic, resolving the article slug or ID before any visual components in the main content container are rendered. From a layout perspective, it ensures that either the normal two-column layout (navigation bar + article + sidebar) or an appropriate error/empty state (managed by the article-status-handler) is displayed, but it introduces no additional padding, margins, or visual elements of its own.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual; it does not render any DOM elements affecting layout. It solely controls which visual components are mounted into the existing layout frame.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Like the route resolver, the data fetcher is non-visual. It should not render a standalone container or take up any visible space in the two-column grid. Its role is to populate the article header, body, and eligibility panel with data. While data is being loaded or if an error occurs, the spatial effect is shown through the article-status-handler layered into the left column (and optionally a subtle loading indication in the sidebar), not through this component directly.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual; it mounts no visible structure and instead triggers state changes that the article-status-handler reflects within the established layout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The article header sits in the left column at the top of the main content area, directly below the full-width help-navigation-bar. It spans the full width of the left column (roughly 60–65% of the centered content container, equivalent to about 7–8 of 12 grid columns). The header block includes, from top to bottom: a small, left-aligned breadcrumb/back link to the Help Center (and/or home), then the article title in a large, bold type, followed by optional meta information (e.g., last updated, read time) in a lighter weight. There is generous vertical padding around this block: about 32–48px from the navigation bar above and 24–32px below, aligning visually with the hero spacing from the mockup. The title is the primary focal point on the left side, using brand red or a strong dark color to echo the hero headline treatment. The total header height combined with the sidebar’s top card gives the page a hero-like band across the upper portion of the viewport.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block-level element within the left column, flowing naturally with the document; it does not stick or float during scroll.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The article body is positioned directly beneath the article-header within the left column, maintaining the same width (about 60–65% of the main content container). There is a comfortable vertical gap of roughly 24–32px between the bottom of the header and the start of the body text. The body content flows downward in a linear, long-form article style: paragraphs, subheadings, lists, and inline links, all left-aligned. Line length is constrained by the column width to enhance readability. Internal spacing follows the 8px grid: 16–24px between paragraphs and 24–32px above section headings. Any inline callouts or images would be kept within this column’s width. The body extends further down the page than the sidebar in most cases, creating a familiar article scroll experience while the right sidebar is visible at least for the initial viewport height.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, scrollable content within the left column; it extends vertically as needed, pushing the page height with no special sticky behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The eligibility rules panel appears as a compact, white card within the right sidebar, stacked beneath the contextual-quote-link card. It is aligned to the right column’s grid (about 35–40% of the container width, roughly 4–5 of 12 columns) and shares the same left and right padding as the contextual quote card so the sidebar reads as a coherent stack. A vertical gap of around 24–32px separates it from the quote card above. Internally, the panel has 20–24px padding on all sides and a subtle light-gray border or extremely soft shadow, consistent with the flat, lightly elevated style from the mockup. Content inside is structured with a concise heading (e.g., “Online Quote Eligibility”) at the top, followed by 1–3 bullet points or short lines indicating rules such as maximum boat length. Text is left-aligned, medium density, and uses a smaller type size than the main article title and CTA so it reads as secondary but clearly supportive information. If no eligibility data exists, this card is omitted entirely, with the sidebar stack visually closing the gap.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static card in the right sidebar; it scrolls with the page along with the rest of the content and does not have sticky behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This component is the primary CTA in the right sidebar and visually mirrors the quote card from the marketing mockup. It is positioned at the very top of the right column, horizontally aligned with the article-header in the left column so they form a balanced hero region across the page. The quote link is rendered as a white card set against a light gray page background, occupying the full width of the right column (approximately 35–40% of the content container). Inside the card, there is generous internal padding of about 24px on all sides. A concise prompt (e.g., “Ready to start a boat insurance quote?”) is placed at the top left of the card, with 8–16px of space above the main CTA. The primary CTA is a large, pill-shaped red button, centered or left-aligned within the card, with ample padding (12–16px vertical, 24–32px horizontal). The button stands out strongly in the sidebar, drawing the eye from the article title to the right, mimicking the hero-to-CTA relationship in the mockup. Additional small helper text (e.g., “Takes about 3 minutes”) can sit under the button with 8–12px spacing. A vertical gap of 24–32px below the card separates it from the next sidebar element (eligibility-rules-panel).
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static sidebar card; it stays at the top of the right column content and scrolls out of view normally as the user scrolls down the article.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The help navigation bar runs full-width across the very top of the page, above the two-column content area. It is horizontally aligned with the viewport, not constrained to the 1140–1200px content container, and uses the brand’s red and white palette. Inside, the content (logo, Help Center link, perhaps a link back to the main site and search) is centered to the same fixed width as the main content container, aligning left and right edges visually. Vertical padding is around 16–20px, with a flat design and maybe a very subtle bottom border or shadow to distinguish it from the page background. This bar is the first element the user sees and sets the corporate tone before the article content. Below it, there is about 32px of vertical spacing before the article-header and contextual-quote-link align as the next visual band.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static at the top of the document; it scrolls away with the page (not sticky) unless a global pattern dictates otherwise.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The article-status-handler occupies the same general region as the article content in the left column and, optionally, a subtle reflection in the right sidebar. When the article is loading, it displays a centered loading state within the left column below the navigation bar—this might be skeleton lines mimicking the article-header and first paragraphs or a simple spinner with brief text. Spacing matches the normal layout: the loader starts roughly where the article-header would, with around 32px of space below the navigation bar. On error (e.g., article not found), the handler shows a clearly styled message card in the left column, approximately 60–65% width of the content container, with 24px internal padding, flat white background, and a short apology message plus links back to the Help Center and home, echoing the card style used elsewhere. In these states, the right sidebar may still show the contextual-quote-link if appropriate, but the status messaging dominates the left column visually until resolved. When data is successfully loaded, the handler becomes invisible and yields space to the article-header and article-body.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Conditionally replaces or overlays the article-header/article-body area in the left column depending on state (loading, error, empty). It scrolls normally with the page and does not fix itself in place.
